recruiting_status.rationale: lab page exists but no funding, publication, or recruitment signals

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a pretty collegial feel — people share ideas freely and there's not a lot of territorial behavior over projects. That said, it's a smaller group, so if you need a big bustling community of peers to bounce ideas off daily, it can feel a bit quiet.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We do one-on-ones with Grady roughly every week or two, though during crunch times around deadlines he's pretty accessible over email and will make time. Group meetings happen but aren't always on a rigid schedule, which I've learned to treat as flexibility rather than disorganization.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own work — Grady will point you toward interesting problems and give useful feedback, but he's not going to hand you a step-by-step plan. I found this empowering after a rough adjustment period, but students who need tightly structured guidance may initially feel adrift.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I had come in with a clearer sense of what questions genuinely excited me, because the open-ended environment rewards students who already have some intellectual direction. Also, Cal Lutheran is a teaching-focused institution, so calibrate your expectations about research infrastructure relative to a big R1.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are self-motivated, comfortable with ambiguity, and genuinely curious about the subject matter seem to get the most out of working with Grady. If you enjoy reading broadly and connecting ideas across subfields, you'll find the conversations rewarding.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need frequent, structured check-ins or detailed task lists tend to feel unmoored here. The pace can also be slower than students used to large funded lab pipelines might expect, so patience with the process matters.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a low-drama environment — Grady keeps things professional without being cold, and there's genuine respect between students and faculty. Collaboration is encouraged but not forced, so introverts and extroverts both seem to find a workable rhythm.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Meetings are fairly regular but vary depending on where you are in your project cycle — early-stage work might mean less frequent formal check-ins while you're still exploring, and more contact when you're writing or presenting. It works fine once you learn to proactively ask for time when you need it.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a lot of room to shape your own research direction, which I genuinely value now but was honestly a little intimidated by at first. Grady seems to trust students to figure things out, sometimes more than the students trust themselves.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The resources available at a smaller university like Cal Lutheran are different from what you'd find at a major research university — not necessarily worse, but different, and it's worth knowing that going in so you can plan accordingly. Also, build relationships outside the lab early; the broader academic network matters.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are intellectually independent and willing to do a lot of reading and thinking before expecting answers from their advisor tend to flourish. If you're comfortable with some uncertainty and can sustain motivation through slower periods, it's a good fit.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came expecting a large, well-funded lab with postdocs and a clear pipeline of funded projects to plug into, this setup will feel unfamiliar. Students who struggle with self-direction or get anxious without frequent external validation tend to find it harder to stay on track.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a pretty collegial feel — people share code and paper drafts without much territorial behavior, which I didn't expect coming in. It's small enough that you actually know what everyone else is working on, for better or worse.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have a one-on-one with Dr. Husain roughly every two weeks, and there's a group meeting that happens maybe once a month when schedules align. Honestly I'd prefer more structured check-ins, but you learn to send proactive email updates if you want timely feedback.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own project pretty quickly — by the second semester there's no hand-holding on literature reviews or problem scoping. That can feel like being thrown into the deep end, but looking back it built real research muscle.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much of the progress depends on your own self-discipline, especially with no strict lab hours or required desk time. Coming from an undergraduate program with a lot of structure, the open-endedness caught me off guard.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are comfortable with ambiguity and can translate a broad topic into a concrete research question on their own tend to do really well here. If you already have some programming or systems chops and just need intellectual direction, this environment rewards that.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need frequent external deadlines or a lot of emotional encouragement from their advisor can find the autonomy draining rather than freeing. The feedback you do get is substantive, but you might wait longer for it than you'd like.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a low-drama environment, which I genuinely appreciate — Dr. Husain isn't the type to micromanage or create competitive tension between students. The flip side is that the lab can feel quiet and a bit isolated if you're not proactively building your own academic community.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We meet formally about every other week, though he's responsive over email for quick technical questions between meetings. Group meetings are infrequent enough that I sometimes forget we're officially a 'group,' so a lot of intellectual exchange happens informally or not at all.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a high degree of ownership over your research direction, which I've come to value, but it also means you can spend months going down a wrong path before getting redirected. The advisor trusts you to self-correct, which is empowering once you've calibrated your own judgment.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish someone had told me that 'open research direction' really means you should come in with at least a rough area of interest already — showing up expecting the advisor to hand you a topic leads to a frustrating first year. The students who hit the ground running had done their homework beforehand.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Self-starters with a specific technical curiosity, especially in computing systems or applied CS areas, seem to get a lot out of working here. If you can set your own milestones and don't need someone to validate every small decision, you'll make steady progress.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Anyone expecting a heavily structured mentorship model with regular feedback on writing or presentation skills may feel underserved — that developmental coaching isn't really the default mode here and you'd need to seek it out yourself through workshops or other faculty.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a collaborative but quietly competitive atmosphere — people share code and paper drafts freely, but there's an unspoken expectation that you're always pushing toward publication. It took me a semester to realize that asking for help is genuinely welcomed, not seen as a weakness.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have a one-on-one with Hao roughly every two weeks, and a full group meeting weekly where someone presents either a paper or their own work-in-progress. The group meetings can run long if the discussion gets heated, which it often does.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own research direction after the first year — Hao will suggest broad problem spaces but won't hand you a pre-scoped project. That freedom is exciting if you're ready for it, but it caught me off guard coming from an undergrad environment where everything was more structured.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much the job falls on you to flag when you're stuck; Hao isn't the type to check in proactively if he hasn't heard from you. Building the habit of sending a short weekly update email made a real difference for me.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who are self-motivated, comfortable with ambiguity, and genuinely excited about the technical depth of the problems do really well here. If you love going deep on a niche topic for months and then surfacing with something solid, this environment rewards that.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who need frequent structured feedback or who thrive on clear short-term milestones can find it frustrating — the goalposts feel far away sometimes, and you have to manufacture your own intermediate checkpoints.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Honestly it's a pretty small, tight-knit group and that cuts both ways — you get real mentorship from senior students and there's genuine camaraderie, but there's not much separation between 'work mode' and 'lab social life.' The vibe is serious but not cold.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: One-on-ones happen about once a week when a deadline is approaching, but can drift to every three weeks during quieter stretches. I've learned to request a meeting explicitly rather than waiting for one to appear on the calendar.
student_survey_responses.independence: Hao is clearly hands-off by design — he'll ask sharp questions that reframe your thinking without telling you the answer, which I've come to appreciate even though it was maddening at first. By year two I had full ownership of my project direction.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The onboarding is pretty informal; there's no structured ramp-up, so your first few months are largely spent reading papers and figuring out what the lab actually does day-to-day. Reaching out to a current student before joining would have saved me a lot of confusion.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People with a strong background in the fundamentals and a habit of reading broadly tend to flourish — Hao seems to value intellectual range and will engage enthusiastically if you bring ideas from adjacent fields into the conversation.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came here expecting a lot of direct technical co-working with the PI — sitting down and coding or deriving things together — that's not really the dynamic, and some people find that isolating, especially in the first year before the senior students adopt you into their workflow.

name: Foaad Khosmood
institution: California Polytechnic State University, San Luis Obispo

recruiting_status.rationale: active lab signals present (funding or publications) but no explicit recruitment statement

recent_papers.title: MultiHuSE: A Multimodal Dataset for Humour Styles and Emotions
recent_papers.venue: International Conference on Content-Based Multimedia Indexing
recent_papers.abstract: Computational recognition of verbal humour re-mains a challenging task, requiring an understanding of lan-guage, delivery style, emotions, and cultural context. Most existing approaches focus on binary classification and lack datasets that capture psychological dimensions of humour alongside variations in expression. We introduce MultiHuSE, a multi-modal dataset comprising 2,407 high-definition videos of 50 demographically diverse actors performing 1,463 text samples across four psychological humour styles (affiliative, aggressive, self-enhancing, and self-deprecating), as well as neutral content. A subset is additionally annotated for underlying emotions. The dataset uniquely captures multiple actor interpretations of the same texts, enabling systematic analysis of expressive diversity. Baseline experiments show that multimodal fusion outperforms unimodal approaches (80.1 % vs. 77.4% accuracy) in humour style classification, with particularly strong gains for affiliative humour (66 % to 74 %). While text provides the strongest individual signal, fusion models deliver meaningful im-provements. We hope that MultiHuSE provides empirical support for psychological theories linking humour and emotion, while also opening new avenues for research in human communication, well-being, and AI-driven interaction. The dataset is available for academic use under an End-User Licence Agreement.

recent_papers.title: Explaining Humour Style Classifications: An XAI Approach to Understanding Computational Humour Analysis
recent_papers.venue: Journal of Data Mining & Digital Humanities
recent_papers.abstract: Humour styles can have either a negative or a positive impact on well-being. Given the importance of these styles to mental health, significant research has been conducted on their automatic identification. However, the automated machine learning models used for this purpose are black boxes, making their prediction decisions opaque. Clarity and transparency are vital in the field of mental health. This paper presents an explainable AI (XAI) framework for understanding humour style classification, building upon previous work in computational humour analysis. Using the best-performing single model (ALI+XGBoost) from prior research, we apply comprehensive XAI techniques to analyse how linguistic, emotional, and semantic features contribute to humour style classification decisions. Our analysis reveals distinct patterns in how different humour styles are characterised and misclassified, with particular emphasis on the challenges in distinguishing affiliative humour from other styles. Through detailed examination of feature importance, error patterns, and misclassification cases, we identify key factors influencing model decisions, including emotional ambiguity, context misinterpretation, and target identification. The framework demonstrates significant utility in understanding model behaviour, achieving interpretable insights into the complex interplay of features that define different humour styles. recent_papers.title: A Two-Model Approach for Humour Style Recognition
recent_papers.abstract: Humour, a fundamental aspect of human communication, manifests itself in various styles that significantly impact social interactions and mental health. Recognising different humour styles poses challenges due to the lack of established datasets and machine learning (ML) models. To address this gap, we present a new text dataset for humour style recognition, comprising 1463 instances across four styles (self-enhancing, self-deprecating, affiliative, and aggressive) and non-humorous text, with lengths ranging from 4 to 229 words. Our research employs various computational methods, including classic machine learning classifiers, text embedding models, and DistilBERT, to establish baseline performance. Additionally, we propose a two-model approach to enhance humour style recognition, particularly in distinguishing between affiliative and aggressive styles. Our method demonstrates an 11.61% improvement in f1-score for affiliative humour classification, with consistent improvements in the 14 models tested. Our findings contribute to the computational analysis of humour in text, offering new tools for studying humour in literature, social media, and other textual sources.

recent_papers.title: Exploring Description-Augmented Dataless Intent Classification
recent_papers.abstract: In this work, we introduce several schemes to leverage description-augmented embedding similarity for dataless intent classification using current state-of-the-art (SOTA) text embedding models. We report results of our methods on four commonly used intent classification datasets and compare against previous works of a similar nature. Our work shows promising results for dataless classification scaling to a large number of unseen intents. We show competitive results and significant improvements (+6.12% Avg.) over strong zero-shot baselines, all without training on labelled or task-specific data. Furthermore, we provide qualitative error analysis of the shortfalls of this methodology to help guide future research in this area.

recent_papers.title: Systematic Literature Review: Computational Approaches for Humour Style Classification
recent_papers.abstract: Understanding various humour styles is essential for comprehending the multifaceted nature of humour and its impact on fields such as psychology and artificial intelligence. This understanding has revealed that humour, depending on the style employed, can either have therapeutic or detrimental effects on an individual's health and relationships. Although studies dedicated exclusively to computational-based humour style analysis remain somewhat rare, an expansive body of research thrives within related task, particularly binary humour and sarcasm recognition. In this systematic literature review (SLR), we survey the landscape of computational techniques applied to these related tasks and also uncover their fundamental relevance to humour style analysis. Through this study, we unveil common approaches, illuminate various datasets and evaluation metrics, and effectively navigate the complex terrain of humour research. Our efforts determine potential research gaps and outlined promising directions. Furthermore, the SLR identifies a range of features and computational models that can seamlessly transition from related tasks like binary humour and sarcasm detection to invigorate humour style classification. These features encompass incongruity, sentiment and polarity analysis, ambiguity detection, acoustic nuances, visual cues, contextual insights, and more. recent_papers.title: Improving Startup Success with Text Analysis
recent_papers.abstract: Investors are interested in predicting future success of startup companies, preferably using publicly available data which can be gathered using free online sources. Using public-only data has been shown to work, but there is still much room for improvement. Two of the best performing prediction experiments use 17 and 49 features respectively, mostly numeric and categorical in nature. In this paper, we significantly expand and diversify both the sources and the number of features (to 171) to achieve better prediction. Data collected from Crunchbase, the Google Search API, and Twitter (now X) are used to predict whether a company will raise a round of funding within a fixed time horizon. Much of the new features are textual and the Twitter subset include linguistic metrics such as measures of passive voice and parts-of-speech. A total of ten machine learning models are also evaluated for best performance. The adaptable model can be used to predict funding 1-5 years into the future, with a variable cutoff threshold to favor either precision or recall. Prediction with comparable assumptions generally achieves F scores above 0.730 which outperforms previous attempts in the literature (0.531), and does so with fewer examples. Furthermore, we find that the vast majority of the performance impact comes from the top 18 of 171 features which are mostly generic company observations, including the best performing individual feature which is the free-form text description of the company.

recent_papers.title: ChatGPT GameJam: Unleashing the power of Large Language Models for Game Jams
recent_papers.venue: International Conference on Game Jams, Hackathons, and Game Creation Events
recent_papers.abstract: Recently Large Language Models (LLMs) have captivated the imagination in many different fields. OpenAI’s ChatGPT, offered the first highly accessible LLM to the users and has seen phenomenal growth since its release in November 2022. While AI tools have always been part of game design and game development, now ChatGPT offers users perhaps for the first time, the ability to create a variety of novel games either entirely or partly by writing a text prompt. ChatGPT is a game jam that embraces and explores LLM-based game production while at the same time providing some insights about the current capabilities and shortcomings of the process. In this paper we report on the process and lessons learned from organizing the first ChatGPT Game Jam – held in May 2023.

pi_survey.research_philosophy: I believe that language and humor are windows into the human mind, and that computational methods should be built to genuinely understand these dimensions rather than treat them as simple classification tasks. My work on projects like 'MultiHuSE' and our systematic literature review on humour style classification reflects my conviction that rigorous dataset construction and transparent modeling are prerequisites for meaningful AI research. Especially in areas touching mental health, as in our XAI work on humour styles, clarity and accountability in our models are non-negotiable.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are curious about the intersection of natural language processing, psychology, and human behavior, and who are comfortable working across both technical and humanistic dimensions of a problem. Experience with machine learning, text embeddings, or multimodal data is a plus, but intellectual honesty and a willingness to question black-box approaches — as we do in our explainability work — matter more to me. If you find yourself drawn to questions like why a model classifies something as a particular humour style, rather than just whether it does so accurately, we'll get along well.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: Students in my group typically divide their time between reading and discussing recent literature, running and analyzing experiments, and writing — whether that's drafting a paper section or documenting results. Given the nature of our projects, like building datasets such as MultiHuSE or developing two-model pipelines for humour recognition, there is always a blend of data curation, modeling, and evaluation work happening simultaneously. We meet regularly to discuss progress and roadblocks, and I encourage students to present their findings clearly and early, even when the results are messy.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to mentor students toward intellectual independence by encouraging them to own their research questions from an early stage. I provide close guidance on methodology and writing, particularly around issues of rigor — for instance, making sure our classification approaches, like those in our dataless intent classification work, are properly benchmarked and honestly reported. I see my role as a collaborator who asks hard questions, not someone who simply hands students a predefined path.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I currently do not have active external grants, so students should be aware that funding arrangements may rely on teaching assistantships or internal university support. I am actively pursuing opportunities to support student research, and the applied nature of our work — touching mental health, explainability, and multimodal AI — positions us well for future grant applications. Prospective students should discuss funding specifics with me directly before committing.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read at least two or three of our recent papers — particularly the systematic literature review on humour style classification and our XAI work — and come with specific questions or critiques about them. I want to see that you can engage critically with research, not just summarize it. A strong application will articulate why computational humor or NLP transparency matters to you personally, and how your background positions you to contribute to this kind of work.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The vibe is collaborative but pretty quiet — it's mostly independent work punctuated by deep conversations about why a model is doing what it's doing, not just whether the numbers look good. There's a genuine intellectual seriousness about things like annotation decisions and explainability that I actually appreciate, even when it slows things down.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We meet one-on-one about once a week, and there are occasional group check-ins where everyone shares where they're stuck as much as where they're succeeding. Foaad is pretty available over email between meetings, which helps.
student_survey_responses.independence: He hands you a problem space pretty early and expects you to start shaping your own questions within it — that can feel unsettling at first if you're used to a more structured setup. But once I got used to owning my research direction, I found it genuinely motivating rather than just stressful.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: Funding is mostly TAships right now, and I wish I had been more direct about that in my initial conversations before accepting — it's not a dealbreaker, but it does shape your timeline and flexibility. Also, dataset construction takes way longer than any paper makes it look.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you're genuinely curious about the 'why' behind model behavior and don't mind reading across psychology and NLP literature in the same week, you'll fit in well. People who like honest, even awkward, conversations about model limitations do better here than people who just want clean results.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who need a lot of top-down structure or a clear checklist of tasks to feel productive can find the open-endedness draining. The lack of a large lab cohort also means you're not constantly bumping into peers who can troubleshoot things with you informally.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a smaller group so there isn't a big bustling lab room atmosphere, but that means you get real attention on your work rather than getting lost in someone else's agenda. The emphasis on things like transparent benchmarking and not overselling results creates a culture where intellectual honesty is actually practiced, not just talked about.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Regular weekly or biweekly meetings depending on where you are in a project cycle — he's serious about the 'present early even when results are messy' thing, so don't wait until everything is polished to bring something to the table. That norm took me a while to get comfortable with but it's actually pretty healthy.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to read, think, and come back with your own interpretation rather than waiting for him to tell you what a paper means for your work. He'll push back hard if he thinks your framing is sloppy, which is uncomfortable but has made me a noticeably better researcher.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The projects sound niche — computational humor, humour style classification — but the day-to-day work involves a lot of careful, sometimes tedious data curation and evaluation pipeline building. I came in thinking it would be more conceptually breezy and less grounded in grinding through annotation schemas.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who genuinely find something interesting in the intersection of language and human psychology, and who can hold technical and humanistic questions in their head at the same time, seem to do well. A tolerance for ambiguity and slow methodological progress before things click is basically required.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you're chasing publications on a fast timeline or need heavy external grant funding to feel secure, this might not be the right fit right now. And if you prefer a PI who lays out a predefined project plan and you just execute it, the expectation to co-own the research questions from early on can be genuinely frustrating.

name: Theresa Migler
institution: California Polytechnic State University, San Luis Obispo

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a collaborative, low-ego atmosphere where people genuinely share ideas across projects — it doesn't feel competitive internally. That said, because the group is relatively small, you have to be comfortable with a lot of self-directed momentum.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We do a one-on-one with Theresa roughly every week or two depending on where you are in your project cycle, plus occasional group check-ins. If you're stuck, she's reachable, but she won't micromanage your calendar.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own research questions pretty early — she'll help you sharpen an idea but won't hand you a pre-packaged problem. I appreciated that once I got my footing, but the first semester felt a bit unmoored.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much time goes into the graph theory and combinatorics background before you can really contribute meaningfully to the network science side of things. Coming in with stronger discrete math would have saved me months.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are genuinely curious about mathematical structures and comfortable sitting with open-ended problems for a while do really well here. If you like clean, self-contained puzzles with guaranteed answers, this might frustrate you.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need frequent external validation or a very structured research roadmap sometimes have a hard time — the mentorship style rewards initiative, and if you're waiting to be told what to do next, progress can stall.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a pretty relaxed environment — Theresa is approachable and there's no weird hierarchy where senior students gatekeep resources or attention. Collaboration with people outside the lab, including undergrads and folks in CS theory circles, happens organically.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Meetings happen regularly but they're working sessions, not status reports — you're expected to show up with something to discuss rather than just a progress update. I've found that rhythm really productive, though early on it added pressure.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a lot of autonomy, maybe more than I expected from an advisor relationship. She'll push back on your framing or methods when something seems off, but the intellectual ownership of the project stays with you, which I genuinely value.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I underestimated how much the research sits at a specific intersection — if you're expecting pure applied network analysis or pure theory, you'll need to stretch in both directions. It's rewarding but requires being flexible about your identity as a researcher.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People with a math-leaning CS background who are excited by questions around graph structure, algorithms, and real-world network data tend to find their niche quickly. Intellectual curiosity matters more than having a polished research background coming in.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you're hoping for a lab that has a big team with lots of built-in social scaffolding and daily peer interaction, the smaller size can feel isolating — you have to be proactive about building your own academic community beyond the lab.

name: Jonathan Ventura
institution: California Polytechnic State University, San Luis Obispo

recent_papers.title: Application of Deep Learning for Classification of Intertidal Eelgrass from Drone-Acquired Imagery
recent_papers.abstract: Shallow estuarine habitats are globally undergoing rapid changes due to climate change and anthropogenic influences, resulting in spatiotemporal shifts in distribution and habitat extent. Yet, scientists and managers do not always have rapidly available data to track habitat changes in real-time. In this study, we apply a novel and a state-of-the-art image segmentation machine learning technique (DeepLab) to two years of high-resolution drone-based imagery of a marine flowering plant species (eelgrass, a temperate seagrass). We apply the model to eelgrass (Zostera marina) meadows in the Morro Bay estuary, California, an estuary that has undergone large eelgrass declines and the subsequent recovery of seagrass meadows in the last decade. The model accurately classified eelgrass across a range of conditions and sizes from meadow-scale to small-scale patches that are less than a meter in size. The model recall, precision, and F1 scores were 0.954, 0.723, and 0.809, respectively, when using human-annotated training data and random assessment points. All our accuracy values were comparable to or demonstrated greater accuracy than other models for similar seagrass systems. This study demonstrates the potential for advanced image segmentation machine learning methods to accurately support the active monitoring and analysis of seagrass dynamics from drone-based images, a framework likely applicable to similar marine ecosystems globally, and one that can provide quantitative and accur

recent_papers.title: Localization and tracking of stationary users for augmented reality
recent_papers.abstract: In augmented reality applications it is essential to know the position and orientation of the user to correctly register virtual 3D content in the user’s field of view. For this purpose, visual tracking through simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) is often used. However, when applied to the commonly occurring situation where the users are mostly stationary, many methods presented in previous research have two key limitations. First, SLAM techniques alone do not address the problem of global localization with respect to prior models of the environment. Global localization is essential in many applications where multiple users are expected to track within a shared space, such as spectators at a sporting event. Secondly, these methods often assume significant translational movement to accurately reconstruct and track from a local model of the environment, causing challenges for many stationary applications. In this paper, we extend recent research on Spherical Localization and Tracking to support relocalization after tracking failure, as well as global localization in large shared environments, and optimize the method for operation on mobile hardware. We also evaluate various state-of-the-art localization approaches, the robustness of our visual tracking method, and demonstrate the effectiveness of our system in real-life scenarios.

In augmented reality applications it is essential to know the position and orientation of the user to correctly register virtual 3D content in the user’s field of view. For this purpose, visual tracking through simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) is often used. However, when applied to the commonly occurring situation where the users are mostly stationary, many methods presented in previous research have two key limitations. First, SLAM techniques alone do not address the problem of global localization with respect to prior models of the environment. Global localization is essential in many applications where multiple users are expected to track within a shared space, such as spectators at a sporting event. Secondly, these methods often assume significant translational movement to accurately reconstruct and track from a local model of the environment, causing challenges for many stationary applications. In this paper, we extend recent research on Spherical Localization and Tracking to support relocalization after tracking failure, as well as global localization in large shared environments, and optimize the method for operation on mobile hardware. We also evaluate various state-of-the-art localization approaches, the robustness of our visual tracking method, and demonstrate the effectiveness of our system in real-life scenarios.

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My research is fundamentally about making computers 'see' — understanding the 3D world from visual data. I'm especially drawn to problems at the intersection of computer vision and real-world impact, whether that's enabling precise localization for augmented reality, as in our work on 'Localization and tracking of stationary users for augmented reality,' or collaborating with ecologists to apply deep learning to environmental monitoring, as in our eelgrass classification work from drone imagery.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are curious, self-motivated, and excited about both the theoretical and applied sides of computer vision and machine learning. Prior experience with image processing, 3D geometry, or deep learning is a plus, but intellectual enthusiasm and a willingness to dig into hard problems matter most to me.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week involves a mix of reading recent literature, implementing and experimenting with algorithms, and meeting with me to discuss progress and next steps. Depending on the project, students might be training image segmentation models, working through geometric camera problems, or prototyping an AR demo — the work is hands-on and iterative.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to give students enough autonomy to develop their own research instincts while staying closely engaged so they don't get stuck. Cal Poly's learn-by-doing culture fits my style well — I'd rather work through a problem together at the whiteboard than just point someone to a paper.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: My work has previously been supported by the NSF, including an NSF CRII award and an NSF CAREER award, as well as the NIH. I do not currently have active external grants listed, so prospective students should discuss funding availability with me directly before applying.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Please note that Cal Poly SLO does not offer a Ph.D. in Computer Science — we offer an M.S., which is a great fit if you want rigorous research training in computer vision or AR/VR. Reach out by email with a brief description of your background and what problems excite you; a genuine connection to the research is far more compelling than a generic inquiry.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small, collaborative environment — probably three or four of us at any given time — which means you get a lot of face time with Ventura and your work actually matters to the group's direction. There's no toxic competition, but it's also not a hand-holding situation; you're expected to show up with initiative.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We meet one-on-one roughly once a week, sometimes more if something's breaking or we're close to a deadline. He's pretty responsive over email between meetings, which helps when you're stuck at 11pm debugging a PyTorch training loop.
student_survey_responses.independence: He gives you real ownership over your project, which is great but can also feel disorienting at first if you're used to more structured coursework. Once I got comfortable proposing my own next steps rather than waiting to be told, the dynamic clicked into place.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known that the MS here is genuinely research-focused and not just a coursework degree — I underestimated how deep you'd go into the literature and how much original experimentation is expected. Also, funding isn't guaranteed, so have that conversation early.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you like switching between reading a geometry paper in the morning and training a segmentation model in the afternoon, this is your place. People who are genuinely curious about the vision problems — not just chasing a credential — tend to get a lot out of the experience.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a lot of external structure or frequent milestones handed to you, the open-ended nature of the projects can be stressful. The iterative, whiteboard-style mentorship is great, but the path forward isn't always spelled out clearly in advance.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The vibe is pretty low-key and academically honest — Ventura doesn't hype things up, and he's upfront when an experiment didn't work out the way we hoped. I appreciated that he treats the MS students like actual researchers rather than course takers who happen to write a thesis.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Weekly group check-ins plus individual meetings when needed; he's not going to chase you down, but he's also never hard to reach. I found that coming in with a clear 'here's what I tried and here's where I'm confused' made those meetings way more useful than vague status updates.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a meaningful amount of autonomy, especially once you're a few months in and have established some trust. Early on I wished he'd narrow the problem space a bit more, but looking back, figuring out scope is part of the research training.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The interdisciplinary projects — like the ecological imaging work — are genuinely interesting but can require a surprising amount of domain reading outside CV, so budget time for that. I also didn't fully appreciate how much the learn-by-doing Cal Poly culture would shape the lab's expectations around prototyping quickly.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are comfortable with ambiguity and enjoy the applied side of things — building demos, running field experiments, working across domains — will find a lot to love here. The AR/3D localization work in particular has some really satisfying moments when geometry and real-world data align.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If your goal is a publication-first, high-output PhD pipeline, this is an MS program at a teaching-focused school, and that's worth sitting with honestly. The research is rigorous, but the publication pace and resource scale are different from an R1 CS PhD environment.

name: Alex Dekhtyar
institution: California Polytechnic State University, San Luis Obispo

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

recent_papers.title: Using Computer Vision to Annotate Video-Recoded Direct Observation of Physical Behavior
recent_papers.abstract: Direct observation is a ground-truth measure for physical behavior, but the high cost limits widespread use. The purpose of this study was to develop and test machine learning methods to recognize aspects of physical behavior and location from videos of human movement: Adults (N = 26, aged 18–59 y) were recorded in their natural environment for two, 2- to 3-h sessions. Trained research assistants annotated videos using commercially available software including the following taxonomies: (1) sedentary versus non-sedentary (two classes); (2) activity type (four classes: sedentary, walking, running, and mixed movement); and (3) activity intensity (four classes: sedentary, light, moderate, and vigorous). Four machine learning approaches were trained and evaluated for each taxonomy. Models were trained on 80% of the videos, validated on 10%, and final accuracy is reported on the remaining 10% of the videos not used in training. Overall accuracy was as follows: 87.4% for Taxonomy 1, 63.1% for Taxonomy 2, and 68.6% for Taxonomy 3. This study shows it is possible to use computer vision to annotate aspects of physical behavior, speeding up the time and reducing labor required for direct observation. Future research should test these machine learning models on larger, independent datasets and take advantage of analysis of video fragments, rather than individual still images.

Future research should test these machine learning models on larger, independent datasets and take advantage of analysis of video fragments, rather than individual still images.

pi_survey.research_philosophy: I'm drawn to problems where computational methods can address real-world measurement challenges that are otherwise too costly or impractical to scale. My recent work on using computer vision to annotate video-recorded direct observation of physical behavior reflects this philosophy — direct observation is a gold standard, but its expense limits how broadly it can be applied, and I believe machine learning can help close that gap.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are comfortable working at the intersection of computer science and applied domains, and who are motivated by problems that have tangible impact. An aptitude for empirical, data-driven research — including wrangling messy real-world datasets like annotated behavioral video — is especially valuable in my group.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week likely involves a mix of reading recent literature, implementing and evaluating machine learning models, and meeting to discuss results and next steps. Students working on projects like our physical behavior recognition work would spend considerable time on data processing, model development, and interpreting outputs in the context of the application domain.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to guide students toward developing independent research judgment while keeping them connected to the broader goals of a project. I believe in iterative feedback — working through experimental design, results, and writeups together — so that students build both technical skills and the ability to communicate their findings clearly.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I do not currently have active external grants to report, so prospective students should discuss funding arrangements with me directly and explore departmental fellowship and teaching assistantship opportunities as part of their planning.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: If my work on applying machine learning to behavioral science measurement resonates with you, reach out with a clear description of your technical background and what specifically attracts you to this kind of interdisciplinary problem. Demonstrating that you've thought carefully about the research challenges — not just the methods — will make your application stand out.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a pretty collaborative vibe even though most of us are working on separate projects — Alex genuinely wants you to understand the 'why' behind what you're building, not just crank out model benchmarks. There's a strong expectation that you care about the application domain, so conversations often drift into behavioral science territory, which I actually find refreshing.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: I meet one-on-one with Alex roughly every week or week and a half, though it can slip to biweekly when he has heavier service obligations. The meetings are substantive — he comes prepared and usually has specific comments on whatever I sent ahead of time, which keeps things from feeling like status updates.
student_survey_responses.independence: He gives you real autonomy, but it's the kind that can feel like being dropped in the deep end if you're not ready for it. Once you've established a direction together, he expects you to drive the day-to-day decisions and bring him results to react to rather than waiting for step-by-step instruction.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known upfront how much of the work is data pipeline and preprocessing — annotated behavioral video is genuinely messy, and I spent my first semester just getting the data into a usable state before any interesting modeling happened. It's not glamorous, but he does treat it as real intellectual work, which helps.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who are self-starters with a genuine curiosity about applied problems do really well here — if you find it satisfying to see your algorithm actually move the needle on something a public health researcher cares about, that motivation carries you through the grind. Strong Python/ML fundamentals also matter a lot from day one.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came in expecting a large, buzzy lab with lots of peer structure and social momentum, you might feel isolated — it's a smaller group and you have to be proactive about building your own scholarly community. Funding is also something you need to be clear-eyed about going in; TA lines exist but they're not guaranteed year over year.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a low-drama, heads-down kind of environment where the work is expected to speak for itself — Alex isn't the type to micromanage, and there isn't a lot of performative busy-ness. I'd describe it as intellectually serious without being cutthroat, though the flip side is that you need to generate your own energy and accountability.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We do regular one-on-ones and occasionally have broader check-ins when there's a paper deadline or a new dataset to discuss as a group. I've found that if I come in with concrete results or a specific question, the time is really productive; vague check-ins tend to be less useful with him.
student_survey_responses.independence: Alex is very much an 'iterative feedback' mentor in practice — he'll engage deeply on experimental design and writing, but between those touchpoints you're largely on your own to figure out implementation details. It took me a while to calibrate how much to struggle independently versus when to just ask.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I underestimated how much domain knowledge I'd need to absorb — you can't just treat behavioral annotation as a generic computer vision task because the ground truth labels have nuances that come from measurement theory in social science. Alex will push you to engage with that literature, which I now appreciate but initially felt like a detour.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are comfortable sitting at the boundary of CS and an applied field — and who don't need that boundary to be clean — tend to flourish. If you're energized by the fact that your model outputs will eventually be used by someone outside CS to make a scientific claim, this is a good fit.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: The funding situation is something applicants should ask about directly and not assume will sort itself out — I've seen people feel caught off guard mid-program, and it adds stress that affects research productivity. Also, if you need a lot of structured guidance on day-to-day tasks, the mentorship style here will probably feel under-scaffolded at first.

name: Pat Fidopiastis
institution: California Polytechnic State University, San Luis Obispo

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

recent_papers.title: Corrected and republished from: “Vibrio fischeri Possesses Xds and Dns Nucleases That Differentially Influence Phosphate Scavenging, Aggregation, Competence, and Symbiotic Colonization of Squid”
recent_papers.abstract: ABSTRACT Cells of Vibrio fischeri colonize the light organ of Euprymna scolopes, providing the squid bioluminescence in exchange for nutrients and protection. The bacteria encounter DNA-rich mucus throughout their transition to a symbiotic lifestyle, leading us to hypothesize a role for nuclease activity in the colonization process. In support of this, we detected abundant extracellular nuclease activity in growing cells of V. fischeri. To discover the gene(s) responsible for this activity, we screened a V. fischeri transposon mutant library for nuclease-deficient strains. Interestingly, only one strain, whose transposon insertion mapped to nuclease gene VF_1451, showed a complete loss of nuclease activity in our screens. A database search revealed that VF_1451 is homologous to the nuclease-encoding gene xds in Vibrio cholerae. However, V. fischeri strains lacking xds eventually revealed slight nuclease activity on plates upon prolonged incubation. This led us to hypothesize that a second secreted nuclease, identified through a database search as VF_0437, a homolog of V. cholerae dns, might be responsible for the residual nuclease activity. Here, we show that Xds and/or Dns are involved in essential aspects of V. fischeri biology, including natural transformation, aggregation, and phosphate scavenging. Furthermore, strains lacking either nuclease were outcompeted by the wild type for squid colonization. Understanding the specific role of nuclease activity in the squid coloniz

recent_papers.title: Microbiome composition and function within the Kellet’s whelk perivitelline fluid
recent_papers.abstract: ABSTRACT Microbiomes have gained significant attention in ecological research, owing to their diverse interactions and essential roles within different organismal ecosystems. Microorganisms, such as bacteria, archaea, and viruses, have profound impact on host health, influencing digestion, metabolism, immune function, tissue development, and behavior. This study investigates the microbiome diversity and function of Kellet’s whelk (Kelletia kelletii) perivitelline fluid (PVF), which sustains thousands of developing K. kelletii embryos within a polysaccharide and protein matrix. Our core microbiome analysis reveals a diverse range of bacteria, with the Roseobacter genus being the most abundant. Additionally, genes related to host-microbe interactions, symbiosis, and quorum sensing were detected, indicating a potential symbiotic relationship between the microbiome and Kellet’s whelk embryos. Furthermore, the microbiome exhibits gene expression related to antibiotic biosynthesis, suggesting a defensive role against pathogenic bacteria and potential discovery of novel antibiotics. Overall, this study sheds light on the microbiome’s role in Kellet’s whelk development, emphasizing the significance of host-microbe interactions in vulnerable life history stages. To our knowledge, ours is the first study to use 16S sequencing coupled with RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) to profile the microbiome of an invertebrate PVF. IMPORTANCE This study provides novel insight to an encapsulated system wit

ABSTRACT Microbiomes have gained significant attention in ecological research, owing to their diverse interactions and essential roles within different organismal ecosystems. Microorganisms, such as bacteria, archaea, and viruses, have profound impact on host health, influencing digestion, metabolism, immune function, tissue development, and behavior. This study investigates the microbiome diversity and function of Kellet’s whelk (Kelletia kelletii) perivitelline fluid (PVF), which sustains thousands of developing K. kelletii embryos within a polysaccharide and protein matrix. Our core microbiome analysis reveals a diverse range of bacteria, with the Roseobacter genus being the most abundant. Additionally, genes related to host-microbe interactions, symbiosis, and quorum sensing were detected, indicating a potential symbiotic relationship between the microbiome and Kellet’s whelk embryos. Furthermore, the microbiome exhibits gene expression related to antibiotic biosynthesis, suggesting a defensive role against pathogenic bacteria and potential discovery of novel antibiotics. Overall, this study sheds light on the microbiome’s role in Kellet’s whelk development, emphasizing the significance of host-microbe interactions in vulnerable life history stages. To our knowledge, ours is the first study to use 16S sequencing coupled with RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) to profile the microbiome of an invertebrate PVF. pi_survey.research_philosophy: My work centers on understanding how microorganisms shape the biology of their hosts, from the intimate bioluminescent symbiosis between Vibrio fischeri and the squid Euprymna scolopes to broader microbiome dynamics in marine invertebrates like Kellet's whelk. I believe that studying these host-microbe interactions across diverse ecological contexts reveals fundamental principles about how bacteria influence digestion, immunity, behavior, and development. Whether we're investigating nuclease activity during squid colonization or characterizing microbiome composition in perivitelline fluid, the goal is always to connect molecular mechanisms to ecological outcomes.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are genuinely curious about microbial ecology and are comfortable working across both molecular and organismal levels of biology. A strong candidate will be excited by the kind of integrative questions my lab pursues — for example, asking how Vibrio fischeri nucleases like Xds and Dns differentially shape competence and symbiotic colonization, or what functional roles microbiomes play in novel niches like whelk perivitelline fluid. Intellectual initiative and a willingness to tackle open-ended problems matter more to me than having a perfectly polished background.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: Students in my lab typically divide their time between bench work — microbial culturing, molecular assays, sequencing-based microbiome analyses — and reading and discussing the primary literature. Depending on the project, a given week might involve designing colonization experiments with squid or processing environmental samples from marine invertebrates and bioinformatically characterizing the microbial communities. Regular one-on-one meetings and lab discussions ensure that students stay connected to the bigger conceptual picture behind their day-to-day experiments.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to mentor students by helping them develop scientific independence while keeping them grounded in rigorous experimental design. I encourage students to take ownership of their projects early — asking their own questions and defending their interpretive choices — while making myself available as a resource and collaborator rather than a director. I find that students grow fastest when they feel trusted to make decisions and learn constructively from results that don't go as expected.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I want to be transparent that my lab does not currently have active external grant support, which is an important practical consideration for prospective students. I am actively working to develop new funding directions, and my ongoing research into host-microbe interactions in marine systems — including microbiome function in Kellet's whelk and the molecular basis of V. fischeri symbiosis — positions the lab well for future grant opportunities. Prospective students should discuss funding arrangements with me directly so we can identify the best path forward together.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: If you're interested in joining my lab, I'd encourage you to read our recent work — including the whelk perivitelline microbiome study and the V. fischeri nuclease paper — and come prepared to talk about what questions those papers raise for you. The strongest applicants are those who have thought carefully about why host-microbe interactions in marine invertebrates are an interesting system, not just those who are generally interested in microbiology. Reach out by email with a brief description of your background and what draws you to this kind of research.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small lab, which means you actually get to know everyone's project pretty well and there's genuine intellectual cross-talk between people working on squid colonization and people doing microbiome stuff with whelks. The vibe is collaborative but not hand-holdy — you're expected to show up with ideas, not just waiting to be told what to do next.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: One-on-ones happen pretty regularly, maybe every week or every other week depending on how much is going on, and Pat is pretty good about not canceling them even during busy periods. There are also lab meetings where you actually discuss papers, not just data updates, which I appreciate more than I expected to.
student_survey_responses.independence: The independence is real — like, uncomfortably real at first if you came from an undergrad lab where everything was scripted. Pat will ask what you think the next experiment should be before offering his own opinion, which felt strange at first but genuinely changed how I approach problems.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish someone had told me upfront how much the funding situation would affect day-to-day logistics — reagent ordering timelines, travel to conferences, that kind of thing. It's not a dealbreaker but it's something you have to mentally prepare for and factor into your planning.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who are genuinely obsessed with the biology — like, who stay late not because they have to but because they actually want to know why the colonization result came out weird — do really well here. If you find marine host-microbe systems inherently fascinating rather than just a vehicle for techniques, this lab will feel like a good fit.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a lot of external structure or a very defined project handed to you from day one, the open-endedness can be stressful rather than exciting. There are also stretches where you're troubleshooting the same assay for weeks and you need to be okay sitting with uncertainty.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Honestly the lab feels more like a reading group and experimental team combined — we spend a real amount of time actually discussing papers, not just treating lit review as something you do alone at home before moving on. It's small enough that there's no politics, which I've heard horror stories about from friends in bigger labs.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Pat holds individual meetings consistently and doesn't just reschedule them into oblivion like I've seen happen elsewhere. That said, between formal meetings you're pretty self-directed, so you need to be proactive about surfacing problems early rather than waiting for the next scheduled check-in.
student_survey_responses.independence: I've had to defend my experimental design choices in ways I didn't expect — Pat will genuinely push back and ask you to articulate your reasoning, not to be difficult, but because he actually wants you to own the logic. It took me a few months to see that as mentorship rather than criticism.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The bioinformatics side of the microbiome work has a steeper learning curve than I anticipated coming in with mostly bench experience — I wish I'd brushed up on sequencing analysis pipelines before starting, because that's a real part of the toolkit here. Also, having a frank early conversation about stipend and funding sources would have saved me some anxiety.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who can toggle between caring about a molecular mechanism and caring about what it means for a squid living in Hawaiian seawater — that dual focus is genuinely what the lab is about, and people who naturally think that way find the work satisfying rather than scattered.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: The lack of external grant funding creates some real uncertainty about long-term support, and if financial stability is a major source of stress for you, that's worth having an honest conversation about before committing. It's not unmanageable but it's a real factor.

name: Katherine Bouman

recent_papers.title: Mapping Dark-Matter Clusters via Physics-Guided Diffusion Models
recent_papers.abstract: Galaxy clusters are powerful probes of astrophysics and cosmology through gravitational lensing: the clusters'mass, dominated by 85% dark matter, distorts background light. Yet, mass reconstruction lacks the scalability and large-scale benchmarks to process the hundreds of thousands of clusters expected from forthcoming wide-field surveys. We introduce a fully automated method to reconstruct cluster surface mass density from photometry and gravitational lensing observables. Central to our approach is DarkClusters-15k, our new dataset of 15,000 simulated clusters with paired mass and photometry maps, the largest benchmark to date, spanning multiple redshifts and simulation frameworks. We train a plug-and-play diffusion prior on DarkClusters-15k that learns the statistical relationship between mass and light, and draw posterior samples constrained by weak- and strong-lensing observables; this yields principled reconstructions driven by explicit physics, alongside well-calibrated uncertainties. Our approach requires no expert tuning, runs in minutes rather than hours, achieves higher accuracy, and matches expertly-tuned reconstructions of the MACS 1206 cluster. We release our method and DarkClusters-15k to support development and benchmarking for upcoming wide-field cosmological surveys.

recent_papers.title: POLISH'ing the Sky: Wide-Field and High-Dynamic Range Interferometric Image Reconstruction with Application to Strong Lens Discovery
recent_papers.abstract: Radio interferometry enables high-resolution imaging of astronomical radio sources by synthesizing a large effective aperture from an array of antennas and solving a deconvolution problem to reconstruct the image. Deep learning has emerged as a promising solution to the imaging problem, reducing computational costs and enabling super-resolution. However, existing DL-based methods often fall short of the requirements for real-world deployment due to limitations in handling high dynamic range, large field of view, and mismatches between training and test conditions. In this work, we build upon and extend the POLISH framework, a recent DL model for radio interferometric imaging. We introduce key improvements to enable robust reconstruction and super-resolution under real-world conditions: (1) a patch-wise training and stitching strategy for scaling to wide-field imaging and (2) a nonlinear arcsinh-based intensity transformation to manage high dynamic range. We conduct comprehensive evaluations using the T-RECS simulation suite with realistic sky models and point spead functions (PSF), and demonstrate that our approach significantly improves reconstruction quality and robustness. We test the model on realistic simulated strong gravitational lenses and show that lens systems with Einstein radii near the PSF scale can be recovered after deconvolution with our POLISH model, potentially yielding 10$\times$ more galaxy-galaxy lensing systems from the Deep Synoptic Array (DSA) survey t

recent_papers.title: U-DAVI: Uncertainty-Aware Diffusion-Prior-Based Amortized Variational Inference for Image Reconstruction
recent_papers.venue: IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing
recent_papers.abstract: Ill-posed imaging inverse problems remain challenging due to the ambiguity in mapping degraded observations to clean images. Diffusion-based generative priors have recently shown promise, but typically rely on computationally intensive iterative sampling or per-instance optimization. Amortized variational inference frameworks address this inefficiency by learning a direct mapping from measurements to posteriors, enabling fast posterior sampling without requiring the optimization of a new posterior for every new set of measurements. However, they still struggle to reconstruct fine details and complex textures. To address this, we extend the amortized framework by injecting spatially adaptive perturbations to measurements during training, guided by uncertainty estimates, to emphasize learning in the most uncertain regions. Experiments on deblurring and super-resolution demonstrate that our method achieves superior or competitive performance to previous diffusion-based approaches, delivering more realistic reconstructions without the computational cost of iterative refinement.

recent_papers.title: Dynamic Black-hole Emission Tomography with Physics-informed Neural Fields
recent_papers.abstract: With the success of static black-hole imaging, the next frontier is the dynamic and 3D imaging of black holes. Recovering the dynamic 3D gas near a black hole would reveal previously-unseen parts of the universe and inform new physics models. However, only sparse radio measurements from a single viewpoint are possible, making the dynamic 3D reconstruction problem significantly ill-posed. Previously, BH-NeRF addressed the ill-posed problem by assuming Keplerian dynamics of the gas, but this assumption breaks down near the black hole, where the strong gravitational pull of the black hole and increased electromagnetic activity complicate fluid dynamics. To overcome the restrictive assumptions of BH-NeRF, we propose PI-DEF, a physics-informed approach that uses differentiable neural rendering to fit a 4D (time + 3D) emissivity field given EHT measurements. Our approach jointly reconstructs the 3D velocity field with the 4D emissivity field and enforces the velocity as a soft constraint on the dynamics of the emissivity. In experiments on simulated data, we find significantly improved reconstruction accuracy over both BH-NeRF and a physics-agnostic approach. We demonstrate how our method may be used to estimate other physics parameters of the black hole, such as its spin.

recent_papers.title: Locating the missing large-scale emission in the jet of M87* with short EHT baselines
recent_papers.abstract: In Very-Long Baseline Interferometric arrays, nearly co-located stations probe the largest scales and typically cannot resolve the observed source. In the absence of large-scale structure, closure phases constructed with these stations are zero and, since they are independent of station-based errors, they can be used to probe data issues. Here, we show with an expansion about co-located stations, how these trivial closure phases become non-zero with brightness distribution on smaller scales than their short baseline would suggest. When applied to sources that are made up of a bright compact and large-scale diffuse component, the trivial closure phases directly measure the centroid relative to the compact source and higher-order image moments. We present a technique to measure these image moments with minimal model assumptions and validate it on synthetic Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) data. We then apply this technique to 2017 and 2018 EHT observations of M87* and find a weak preference for extended emission in the direction of the large-scale jet. We also apply it to 2021 EHT data and measure the source centroid about 1 mas northwest of the compact ring, consistent with the jet observed at lower frequencies.

recent_papers.title: Ring Asymmetry and Spin in M87*
recent_papers.abstract: Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) images of the supermassive black hole M87* depict an asymmetric ring of emission. General relativistic magnetohydrodynamic (GRMHD) models of M87* and its accretion disk predict that the amplitude and location of the ring’s peak brightness asymmetry should fluctuate due to turbulence in the source plasma. We compare the observed distribution of brightness asymmetry amplitudes to the simulated distribution in GRMHD models, across varying black hole spin a*. We show that, for strongly magnetized (MAD) models, three epochs of EHT data marginally disfavor ∣a*∣ ≲ 0.2. This is consistent with the Blandford–Znajek model for M87’s jet, which predicts that M87* should have nonzero spin. We show quantitatively how future observations could improve spin constraints and discuss how improved spin constraints could distinguish between differing jet-launching mechanisms and black hole growth scenarios.

recent_papers.title: Probing jet base emission of M87* with the 2021 Event Horizon Telescope observations
recent_papers.abstract: We investigate the presence and spatial characteristics of the jet base emission in M87* at $230 GHz $, enabled by the significantly enhanced ̆v coverage in the 2021 Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) observations. The integration of the 12 Kitt Peak Telescope (USA) and NOEMA (France) stations into the array introduces two critical intermediate-length baselines to SMT (USA) and IRAM 30 (Spain), providing sensitivity to emission structures at spatial scales of ∼ 250 μ as and ∼ 2500 μ as (∼ 0.02 pc and ∼ 0.2 pc ). Without these new baselines, previous EHT observations of the source in 2017 and 2018 lacked the capability to constrain emission on large scales, where a ``missing flux" of order ∼ 1,Jy is expected to reside. To probe these scales, we analyzed closure phases---robust against station-based gain calibration errors---and model the jet base emission using a simple Gaussian component offset from the compact ring emission at spatial separations $> 100 μ as $. Our analysis revealed a Gaussian feature centered at (Δ R.A. ≈ 320 μ as Δ Dec. ≈ 60 μ as ), projected separation of ≈ 5500 AU with an estimated flux density of only ∼ 60 mJy implying that most of the missing flux identified in previous EHT studies had to originate from different, larger scales. Brighter emission at the relevant spatial scales is firmly ruled out, and the data do not favor more complex models. This component aligns with the inferred position of the large-scale jet and is therefore physically consistent with

recent_papers.title: Revealing Fine Structure in Protoplanetary Disks with Physics Constrained Neural Fields
recent_papers.abstract: Protoplanetary disks are the birthplaces of planets, and resolving their three-dimensional structure is key to understanding disk evolution. The unprecedented resolution of ALMA demands modeling approaches that capture features beyond the reach of traditional methods. We introduce a computational framework that integrates physics-constrained neural fields with differentiable rendering and present RadJAX, a GPU-accelerated, fully differentiable line radiative transfer solver achieving up to 10,000x speedups over conventional ray tracers, enabling previously intractable, high-dimensional neural reconstructions. Applied to ALMA CO observations of HD 163296, this framework recovers the vertical morphology of the CO-rich layer, revealing a pronounced narrowing and flattening of the emission surface beyond 400 au - a feature missed by existing approaches. Our work establish a new paradigm for extracting complex disk structure and advancing our understanding of protoplanetary evolution.

recent_abstracts: Galaxy clusters are powerful probes of astrophysics and cosmology through gravitational lensing: the clusters'mass, dominated by 85% dark matter, distorts background light. Yet, mass reconstruction lacks the scalability and large-scale benchmarks to process the hundreds of thousands of clusters expected from forthcoming wide-field surveys. We introduce a fully automated method to reconstruct cluster surface mass density from photometry and gravitational lensing observables. Central to our approach is DarkClusters-15k, our new dataset of 15,000 simulated clusters with paired mass and photometry maps, the largest benchmark to date, spanning multiple redshifts and simulation frameworks. We train a plug-and-play diffusion prior on DarkClusters-15k that learns the statistical relationship between mass and light, and draw posterior samples constrained by weak- and strong-lensing observables; this yields principled reconstructions driven by explicit physics, alongside well-calibrated uncertainties. Our approach requires no expert tuning, runs in minutes rather than hours, achieves higher accuracy, and matches expertly-tuned reconstructions of the MACS 1206 cluster. We release our method and DarkClusters-15k to support development and benchmarking for upcoming wide-field cosmological surveys.
