pi_survey.research_philosophy: My work sits at the intersection of computational imaging, machine learning, and astrophysics — I believe that the most exciting science happens when we push the boundaries of what's physically observable by rethinking how we reconstruct images from sparse, indirect measurements. Whether we're doing dynamic black-hole emission tomography with physics-informed neural fields or mapping dark-matter clusters via physics-guided diffusion models, the through-line is always: how do we extract the most faithful picture of the universe from severely ill-posed data?
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I'm looking for students who are genuinely curious about both the mathematical underpinnings of inverse problems and the astrophysical questions those problems can answer. Strong candidates tend to have some combination of signal processing, machine learning, or applied math backgrounds, and — critically — they aren't afraid to engage deeply with domain science like radio interferometry or gravitational lensing.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week involves a mix of algorithm development and experimentation — for example, adapting deep learning architectures for wide-field interferometric image reconstruction as in our POLISH work, or validating a new reconstruction against simulated Event Horizon Telescope data. Students also spend time reading recent literature, attending group meetings, and collaborating with astronomers and physicists who bring the domain expertise that keeps our methods grounded in real observational constraints.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to give students ownership of their projects early while staying closely involved in the conceptual framing and critical decision points. I think the best mentorship pairs intellectual freedom — letting a student find the angle on a problem that excites them — with structured feedback on writing, presentations, and the rigor of scientific claims, since our work ultimately has to speak to both the imaging and the astronomy communities.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I don't have active grants listed at the moment that I can speak to directly, but my group's research connects to high-priority areas in NSF and DOE portfolios — computational imaging for next-generation radio arrays, gravitational lensing analysis for upcoming wide-field surveys, and black-hole physics with the EHT — so I am actively pursuing support in those directions.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read a paper or two from the group before you reach out — something like our work on dynamic black-hole imaging or mapping dark-matter clusters with diffusion models — and tell me concretely what problem you'd want to tackle and why. The applicants who stand out are the ones who can articulate not just that they like machine learning or astronomy, but why the hardest inverse problems in observational astrophysics are the ones worth solving.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has this genuinely interdisciplinary energy — on any given day you might be debugging a neural field architecture and then pivoting to a call with radio astronomers about EHT data artifacts. It's intellectually stimulating but can feel scattered if you need tight thematic focus.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have a weekly group meeting that rotates who presents, plus Katherine does one-on-ones that run roughly biweekly — though during crunch periods around paper deadlines she'll carve out extra time without you having to ask.
student_survey_responses.independence: She hands you real ownership of a project early, which I appreciated, but that also means you have to self-direct a lot of the day-to-day problem framing. The conceptual check-ins are substantive when they happen, but you can't expect her to walk you through every algorithmic decision.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd been more honest with myself about how much astrophysics background I actually had — the domain science isn't optional decoration, it shapes what counts as a valid reconstruction, and catching up on radio interferometry while also doing ML research was a real time tax my first year.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who genuinely enjoy sitting at the boundary between a hard math problem and a hard physics question will love it here — the work feels consequential in a way that's rare, like you're not just benchmarking on CIFAR.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need highly structured milestones and frequent reassurance that you're on the right track, the environment can feel a bit open-ended; the freedom is real but it has weight to it.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: There's a collaborative undercurrent — people share code and simulation data pretty openly, and nobody hoards results — but the lab is small enough that your social world at Caltech extends well beyond it. Katherine sets a tone of rigorous but non-competitive science.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Group meetings happen weekly and are usually centered on a paper read or someone's work-in-progress; individual check-ins vary more by project phase, and I've gone through stretches where two weeks passed without a dedicated slot, which required me to be proactive.
student_survey_responses.independence: She's good at letting you find the angle on a problem that genuinely excites you rather than just handing you a pre-scoped task, but that gift comes with the expectation that you can handle ambiguity and self-motivate through the messy middle of a project.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't fully appreciate how much of the work lives in the validation side — running reconstructions against simulated EHT data, checking against observational constraints — it's methodical and slow and important, and papers that skip it don't land well with the astronomy community.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who've done some signal processing or applied math and are genuinely curious about what's physically happening in the images they're reconstructing — not just the loss curves — tend to produce the most interesting work here.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Funding conversations can be opaque from the student side, and if you're anxious about RA continuity you may find the uncertainty stressful; the research connects to high-profile funding areas but concrete guarantees aren't always available early on.

recruiting_status.rationale: active lab signals present (funding or publications) but no explicit recruitment statement

name: Yisong Yue

recent_papers.title: Learning Regions of Interest for Bayesian Optimization with Adaptive Level-Set Estimation
recent_papers.abstract: We study Bayesian optimization (BO) in high-dimensional and non-stationary scenarios. Existing algorithms for such scenarios typically require extensive hyperparameter tuning, which limits their practical effectiveness. We propose a framework, called BALLET, which adaptively filters for a high-confidence region of interest (ROI) as a superlevel-set of a nonparametric probabilistic model such as a Gaussian process (GP). Our approach is easy to tune, and is able to focus on local region of the optimization space that can be tackled by existing BO methods. The key idea is to use two probabilistic models: a coarse GP to identify the ROI, and a localized GP for optimization within the ROI. We show theoretically that BALLET can efficiently shrink the search space, and can exhibit a tighter regret bound than standard BO without ROI filtering. We demonstrate empirically the effectiveness of BALLET on both synthetic and real-world optimization tasks.

recent_papers.title: DeCOIL: Optimization of Degenerate Codon Libraries for Machine Learning-Assisted Protein Engineering
recent_papers.abstract: With advances in machine learning (ML)-assisted protein engineering, models based on data, biophysics, and natural evolution are being used to propose informed libraries of protein variants to explore. Synthesizing these libraries for experimental screens is a major bottleneck, as the cost of obtaining large numbers of exact gene sequences is often prohibitive. Degenerate codon (DC) libraries are a cost-effective alternative for generating combinatorial mutagenesis libraries where mutations are targeted to a handful of amino acid sites. However, existing computational methods to optimize DC libraries to include desired protein variants are not well suited to design libraries for ML-assisted protein engineering. To address these drawbacks, we present DEgenerate Codon Optimization for Informed Libraries (DeCOIL), a generalized method which directly optimizes DC libraries to be useful for protein engineering: to sample protein variants that are likely to have both high fitness and high diversity in the sequence search space. Using computational simulations and wet-lab experiments, we demonstrate that DeCOIL is effective across two specific case studies, with potential to be applied to many other use cases. DeCOIL offers several advantages over existing methods, as it is direct, easy-to-use, generalizable, and scalable. With accompanying software (https://github.com/jsunn-y/DeCOIL), DeCOIL can be readily implemented to generate desired informed libraries.

recent_papers.title: Conformal Generative Modeling on Triangulated Surfaces
recent_papers.abstract: We propose conformal generative modeling, a framework for generative modeling on 2D surfaces approximated by discrete triangle meshes. Our approach leverages advances in discrete conformal geometry to develop a map from a source triangle mesh to a target triangle mesh of a simple manifold such as a sphere. After accounting for errors due to the mesh discretization, we can use any generative modeling approach developed for simple manifolds as a plug-and-play subroutine. We demonstrate our framework on multiple complicated manifolds and multiple generative modeling subroutines, where we show that our approach can learn good estimates of distributions on meshes from samples, and can also learn simultaneously from multiple distinct meshes of the same underlying manifold.

recent_papers.title: Eventual Discounting Temporal Logic Counterfactual Experience Replay
recent_papers.abstract: Linear temporal logic (LTL) offers a simplified way of specifying tasks for policy optimization that may otherwise be difficult to describe with scalar reward functions. However, the standard RL framework can be too myopic to find maximally LTL satisfying policies. This paper makes two contributions. First, we develop a new value-function based proxy, using a technique we call eventual discounting, under which one can find policies that satisfy the LTL specification with highest achievable probability. Second, we develop a new experience replay method for generating off-policy data from on-policy rollouts via counterfactual reasoning on different ways of satisfying the LTL specification. Our experiments, conducted in both discrete and continuous state-action spaces, confirm the effectiveness of our counterfactual experience replay approach.

funding_sources.title: Collaborative Research: III: Medium: Interpretable Scientific Discovery with Foundation Model Programs

nsf_grants.title: Collaborative Research: III: Medium: Interpretable Scientific Discovery with Foundation Model Programs

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My research sits at the intersection of machine learning and its application to science, autonomy, and AI systems more broadly. I believe the most exciting work bridges rigorous core ML — whether that's Bayesian optimization, temporal logic, or generative modeling — with real-world domains like protein engineering, as in our DeCOIL work, or robotic control. I want to develop methods that are both theoretically grounded and genuinely useful in practice.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are intellectually curious across domains and comfortable operating at the boundary between theory and application. Strong mathematical foundations matter, but so does the drive to connect ideas — someone who can engage with a biology collaborator on protein library synthesis one week and derive convergence guarantees the next is exactly the kind of person who thrives in my group. Demonstrated ability to see a project through to a real result, whether a paper or working code, is a strong signal.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week involves a mix of individual research — running experiments, reading papers, writing — along with lab meetings where we discuss ongoing projects across our core AI, AI for Science, and AI for Autonomy threads. Students regularly interact with collaborators from other disciplines; for instance, projects like DeCOIL require tight coordination with protein engineers, so you should expect to spend meaningful time communicating across field boundaries. I also encourage students to engage with the broader community, including ICLR activities given my leadership involvement there.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to calibrate my involvement to where each student is in their development — earlier-stage students get more frequent check-ins and structured feedback, while more advanced students are pushed toward greater independence in framing problems and driving collaborations. I care deeply about mentorship: receiving the mentoring award from Caltech EAS's Grad Student Advisory Board is something I'm genuinely proud of, and I take it seriously. I want students to leave my group as confident, independent researchers who can both generate and communicate novel ideas.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: My group is actively funded, including through our NSF collaborative grant on 'Interpretable Scientific Discovery with Foundation Model Programs,' which runs through 2028. I also maintain several active industry advising relationships — with organizations like Lila Sciences, Latitude AI, and others — which can create additional research opportunities and internship pathways for students interested in applied work.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Be specific about why my group's work resonates with you — pointing to a paper like 'Learning Regions of Interest for Bayesian Optimization with Adaptive Level-Set Estimation' or our AI for Science projects shows you've engaged seriously with what we actually do. I'm drawn to applicants who have a concrete research question or domain they're excited about, not just a general interest in ML. If you're applying, think carefully about where your background intersects with our three research pillars — core AI, science, and autonomy — and make that connection explicit in your statement.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a genuinely interdisciplinary feel — in a single week I've had serious conversations with protein engineers about combinatorial library design and then sat in on a discussion about convergence proofs for Bayesian optimization. It can feel scattered at first, but once you find your footing you realize the cross-pollination is the whole point.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Yisong does individual check-ins, but the cadence really depends on where you are in your PhD — my first year I had pretty structured weekly one-on-ones, now as a third-year it's more like every two to three weeks unless I initiate more. Lab meetings happen regularly and span all three research threads, so you're always hearing about projects outside your own area.
student_survey_responses.independence: He gives you real freedom to define your research direction, which I love, but that also means you have to be proactive about asking for feedback — he won't micromanage you into a result. The expectation is that you're driving things, especially once you've shipped one paper and proven you can execute.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much time cross-disciplinary collaboration actually takes — coordinating with biology collaborators isn't just a footnote, it's hours of back-and-forth emails and calls to align on terminology before you even get to the science. It's genuinely interesting work, but budget for it in your schedule.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who are comfortable sitting with ambiguity and can get excited about a new domain quickly do really well — if you can pick up enough cell biology vocabulary to have a real conversation with a wet-lab collaborator, Yisong notices and it opens up more interesting projects. Strong self-starters who don't need external structure to stay productive seem happiest.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came in wanting to go deep on one narrow subfield of ML theory and nothing else, you might find the breadth expectation frustrating. The lab isn't a place where you can ignore the application side of a project and just focus on lemmas.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Honestly the vibe is pretty collegial — people share paper recommendations across totally different subfields and nobody makes you feel dumb for not knowing something outside your area. That said, it's Caltech, so the bar for rigor is high and hand-wavy arguments don't survive lab meeting for long.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Group lab meetings are consistent and Yisong is reliably there, which I appreciate — some advisors skip half of them. Individual meetings are less predictable timing-wise because he has a lot going on, especially with the ICLR organizational stuff, so you learn to front-load your questions and come prepared.
student_survey_responses.independence: The autonomy is real — by year two he's expecting you to bring him a problem framing, not just ask what to work on next. I've seen students struggle with this transition, but if you have opinions about research directions and can defend them, it's actually a great environment to develop as an independent thinker.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't fully appreciate how seriously he takes mentorship until I'd been here a while — he genuinely reads your drafts carefully and the feedback is substantive, not just 'looks good.' The Caltech mentoring award he got wasn't performative, it reflects something real about how he operates.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who can zoom out from their immediate experiment and articulate why it matters to someone outside ML — a biologist, a roboticist, a program officer — tend to get a lot out of working with Yisong. There are also real industry connection opportunities through his advising relationships if you're interested in applied work after graduation.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: The breadth of the group means you don't always have a labmate who's close enough to your specific problem to give you detailed technical help — the person next to you might be working on temporal logic for autonomy while you're doing protein fitness prediction, so you sometimes have to go outside the lab to find your real intellectual peer group.

recruiting_status.rationale: active lab signals present (funding or publications) but no explicit recruitment statement

name: Pietro Perona

recent_papers.title: Benchmarking Algorithmic Bias in Face Recognition: An Experimental Approach Using Synthetic Faces and Human Evaluation
recent_papers.venue: IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision
recent_papers.abstract: We propose an experimental method for measuring bias in face recognition systems. Existing methods to measure bias depend on benchmark datasets that are collected in the wild and annotated for protected (e.g., race, gender) and unprotected (e.g., pose, lighting) attributes. Such observational datasets only permit correlational conclusions, e.g., "Algorithm A’s accuracy is different on female and male faces in dataset X.". By contrast, experimental methods manipulate attributes individually and thus permit causal conclusions, e.g., "Algorithm A’s accuracy is affected by gender and skin color."Our method is based on generating synthetic faces using a neural face generator, where each attribute of interest is modified independently while leaving all other attributes constant. Human observers crucially provide the ground truth on perceptual identity similarity between synthetic image pairs. We validate our method quantitatively by evaluating race and gender biases of three research-grade face recognition models. Our synthetic pipeline reveals that for these algorithms, accuracy is lower for Black and East Asian population subgroups. Our method can also quantify how perceptual changes in attributes affect face identity distances reported by these models. Our large synthetic dataset, consisting of 48,000 synthetic face image pairs (10,200 unique synthetic faces) and 555,000 human annotations (individual attributes and pairwise identity comparisons) is available to researchers in this important area.

recent_papers.title: Confidence Intervals for Error Rates in 1:1 Matching Tasks: Critical Statistical Analysis and Recommendations
recent_papers.abstract: Matching algorithms predict relationships between items in a collection. For example, in 1:1 face verification, a matching algorithm predicts whether two face images depict the same person. Accurately assessing the uncertainty of the error rates of such algorithms can be challenging when test data are dependent and error rates are low, two aspects that have been often overlooked in the literature.In this work, we review methods for constructing confidence intervals for error rates in 1:1 matching tasks. We derive and examine the statistical properties of these methods, demonstrating how coverage and interval width vary with sample size, error rates, and degree of data dependence with experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets. Based on our findings, we provide recommendations for best practices for constructing confidence intervals for error rates in 1:1 matching tasks.l

recent_papers.title: Understanding Label Bias in Single Positive Multi-Label Learning
recent_papers.abstract: Annotating data for multi-label classification is prohibitively expensive because every category of interest must be confirmed to be present or absent. Recent work on single positive multi-label (SPML) learning shows that it is possible to train effective multi-label classifiers using only one positive label per image. However, the standard benchmarks for SPML are derived from traditional multi-label classification datasets by retaining one positive label for each training example (chosen uniformly at random) and discarding all other labels. In realistic settings it is not likely that positive labels are chosen uniformly at random. This work introduces protocols for studying label bias in SPML and provides new empirical results.

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My lab is driven by a fundamental question: what are the computational foundations of vision? We pursue this from both directions — building machine vision systems that can recognize frogs, cell phones, sailboats, and thousands of other categories in cluttered images, while also studying the human visual system through psychophysical experiments and computational modeling. Ultimately, I believe understanding how vision works in the brain and building machines that can see are deeply connected endeavors that push each other forward.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are genuinely curious about both the science and the engineering of vision — people who aren't satisfied with a system that works unless they understand *why* it works. I also value breadth of application; our work spans conservation biology, consumer products, manufacturing, and defense, so students who can connect computational ideas to real-world impact, as we did in 'Spatial Implicit Neural Representations for Global-Scale Species Mapping,' tend to thrive here.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week involves a mix of hands-on experimentation — implementing and evaluating models, often on large-scale visual datasets — alongside reading and discussing recent literature in lab meetings. Students are also expected to engage with collaborators from other disciplines, whether ecologists interested in species mapping or neuroscientists studying human perception, so communication skills get exercised regularly.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to give students enough independence to develop their own scientific voice while staying closely enough engaged that they don't spend months heading in an unproductive direction. As projects mature I step back further, but early on I prefer frequent, frank conversations about what the data are actually telling us versus what we hoped they would say.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I don't currently have active external grants to advertise, so prospective students should be aware that funding situations can vary and I encourage them to ask me directly about support when they reach out. That said, Caltech provides strong institutional support for PhD students, and our lab has historically been able to sustain research across a wide range of application areas.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Look carefully at our publications on Google Scholar and identify one or two specific threads of work that genuinely excite you — don't write a generic letter. If you're interested in visual recognition, large-scale species mapping, Visipedia, or computational models of human perception, say so concretely and explain what you would want to do next. A focused, intellectually honest application stands out far more than a broad one.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a genuinely interdisciplinary feel — one week you're debugging a recognition pipeline, the next you're on a call with an ecologist about species distribution data, and both feel equally legitimate as research. It's collaborative without being hand-holdy; people share code and ideas freely, but you're expected to show up having done the work.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Pietro meets with me roughly every two weeks one-on-one, though that was closer to weekly in my first year when I was still finding my footing. Lab meetings happen regularly and he's pretty direct about poking holes in your framing, which is uncomfortable but genuinely useful.
student_survey_responses.independence: You get real independence, but it's not the kind where you disappear for six months — he'll notice if your results stop making sense relative to your hypotheses and he'll say so plainly. I'd describe it as 'earned independence': prove you can run a clean experiment and he backs off; show signs of spinning your wheels and he'll re-engage fast.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much the funding situation can vary from year to year — there's no single big grant anchoring everything, so it pays to have that conversation with him early and honestly. Also, Caltech's PhD structure means you need to be comfortable advocating for your own resources rather than assuming they'll materialize.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who genuinely want to understand *why* a model works, not just get the benchmark number, tend to do really well here — Pietro has a low tolerance for results that are impressive but unexplained. If you also have even a passing interest in connecting your work to something like conservation biology or human perception, that intellectual range gets rewarded.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who want a tightly scoped, deliverable-driven project with clear milestones handed to them up front will find the open-endedness stressful. The dual focus on machine vision and human perception is exciting in theory but it means you sometimes have to work hard to figure out where your thesis is actually anchored.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a smaller, quieter lab than some of the big ML groups, which I actually appreciate — you get real face time with Pietro rather than being one of thirty students jostling for attention. The vibe is more 'curious scientists' than 'ship fast, publish faster,' which suits some people and frustrates others.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Group lab meetings are where a lot of the intellectual action happens; Pietro comes in with pointed questions about the papers we read and doesn't let hand-wavy answers slide. Individual check-ins are less structured — sometimes I wish there were a standing weekly slot rather than scheduling ad hoc, especially when I'm stuck.
student_survey_responses.independence: He's genuinely invested in you developing your own scientific voice, and by your third or fourth year you're mostly steering your own ship. Early on, though, the 'frank conversations about what the data are actually telling us' thing is real — he'll push back hard if he thinks you're fitting a narrative to results, which stings but has made me a much more careful thinker.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The breadth of application areas the lab touches sounds exciting in recruiting materials, and it is, but it also means there isn't always a senior student who's already solved your exact subproblem — you're often building from scratch. I'd also have front-loaded more of the psychophysics literature before arriving; the human perception side of the work has a steep reading curve if your background is pure ML.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you can hold a technical conversation with a computer vision researcher and then turn around and explain your work clearly to a biologist or a neuroscientist, you'll find that skill valued here in a way it might not be elsewhere. People who are energized by large-scale visual datasets and real-world deployment contexts — species mapping, Visipedia-style annotation — fit the lab's identity well.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came from a fast-paced industry internship expecting rapid iteration with lots of compute resources on tap, the pace and resource environment here can feel like a gear shift — this is academia and a smaller lab, so you have to be resourceful. And if you're not genuinely interested in the 'why does this work' question and just want to stack papers, the culture will feel like friction rather than support.

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

name: Lior Pachter

recent_papers.title: Uniform pre-processing of bacterial single-cell RNA-seq
recent_papers.abstract: Bacteria are highly heterogeneous, even under controlled conditions, making single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) essential for studying microbial diversity and symbiosis. Since its first application in 2015, bacterial scRNA-seq has expanded, but different assays depend on distinct, custom, in-house preprocessing making it difficult to analyze data as part of a unified workflow. The kallisto-bustools suite of tools has enabled uniform pre-processing of eukaryotic scRNA-seq while also reducing time and resource demands for pre-processing, but is not optimized for bacterial scRNA-seq. We adapt kallisto-bustools to be suitable for reads generated from operons, as well as for a much shorter gene length distribution, and show that it can efficiently and accurately quantify bacterial scRNA-seq. Our work provides a scalable foundation for uniform pre-processing of microbial single-cell transcriptomics.

recent_papers.title: Long-read sequencing transcriptome quantification with lr-kallisto
recent_papers.abstract: RNA abundance quantification has become routine and affordable thanks to high-throughput “short-read” technologies that provide accurate molecule counts at the gene level. Similarly accurate and affordable quantification of definitive full-length, transcript isoforms has remained a stubborn challenge, despite its obvious biological significance across a wide range of problems. “Long-read” sequencing platforms now produce data-types that can, in principle, drive routine definitive isoform quantification. However some particulars of contemporary long-read datatypes, together with isoform complexity and genetic variation, present bioinformatic challenges. We show here, using ONT data, that fast and accurate quantification of long-read data is possible and that it is improved by exome capture. To perform quantifications we developed lr-kallisto, which adapts the kallisto bulk and single-cell RNA-seq quantification methods for long-read technologies.

recent_papers.title: k -spaces: Mixtures of Gaussian latent variable models
recent_papers.abstract: Principal component analysis (PCA) and k-means clustering are two seemingly different methods for dimension reduction and clustering, respectively, but can be understood as special cases of inference in a Gaussian latent variable model framework. We leverage this insight to develop a probabilistic framework and methods for simultaneous dimension reduction, clustering, and latent space learning that are efficient and interpretable, and that can replace current ad hoc combinations of PCA and clustering. The algorithm, k-spaces, has broad applicability, which we demonstrate in several distinct genomic settings. In particular, we show how k-spaces can be used to model gene expression in quantitative hybridization chain reaction (qHCR) images, for inference in epigenomics, and for dimension reduction of single-cell RNA-sequencing data.

recent_papers.title: Biophysical constraints on mRNA decay rates shape macroevolutionary divergence in steady-state abundances
recent_papers.abstract: Evolutionary changes to gene expression are understood to be a major driver of phenotypic divergence between species. Researchers have investigated the drivers of this divergence by fitting evolutionary models to multi-species ‘omic’ datasets. It is now apparent that steady-state mRNA expression levels show patterns consistent with evolutionary constraints, likely as a consequence of stabilizing selection. However, as all previous work has used bulk RNA measurements, it has been impossible to determine which of the many cellular processes that contribute to steady-state abundances underlie the divergence between species. Here we develop a novel paradigm for addressing this open problem. Using multi-species single-cell expression data and biophysical models, we estimate mRNA transcriptional burst sizes, splicing rates and decay rates across multiple species. We then derive phylogenetic models that describe the divergence of these rates under alternative evolutionary scenarios and fit these to the comparative data. We find evidence for biophysical constraints on the rates of mRNA decay, such that macroevolutionary divergence in expression is primarily a consequence of variation in transcriptional bursting.

recent_papers.title: Determining gene specificity from multivariate single-cell RNA sequencing data
recent_papers.abstract: An important application of single-cell genomics experiments is to identify genes specific to biological categories or experimental conditions. Although numerous approaches have been proposed to identify such genes, we consider an axiomatic approach based on defining properties that a specificity measure should have. This leads us to develop ember (Entropy Metrics for Biological ExploRation), which we show is the only method satisfying four key desired properties for a specificity measure. Applying ember to eight tissues from eight founder mouse strains, we find that gene specificity is often unintuitive: canonical markers can be supplanted, housekeeping genes are context-dependent, and mouse strain can drive unexpected cell type switching. Unsupervised learning on entropy metrics uncovers shared genes specialized to male gonads and kidney, as well as genes specific to non-consecutive developmental stages in the kidney. To facilitate further exploration of gene specificity in mice, we have also developed a comprehensive specificity database, along with a web interface and API. Extending ember to a human PBMC dataset collected from 255 diverse individuals, we find that variation in PBMCs is largely localized to classical monocytes. We also find genes with unique specificity by sex, age and ancestral background. Together, these applications establish ember as a powerful tool and provide a roadmap for elucidating the impact of human genetic variation using the murine model.

recent_papers.title: The Rayleigh Quotient and Contrastive Principal Component Analysis I
recent_papers.abstract: Contrastive learning methods can be powerful tools for genomics, enabling the identification of signals in an experiment via dimension reduction while reducing noise using a control. One such popular approach is contrastive PCA, which, despite being used in a variety of settings, does not scale to large datasets. We show that the contrastive PCA objective is an approximation of a Rayleigh quotient, analogous in form to Fisher’s linear discriminant analysis and the common spatial patterns method. The Rayleigh quotient is ρPCA, satisfies numerous desirable properties, and provides an interpretable form of dimension reduction via generalized eigenvectors. We demonstrate that ρPCA is more accurate than contrastive PCA and much more efficient. We also show how it can be used not only for dimension reduction of data with respect to a control, but also for contrasting conditions via an analysis of single-nucleus transcriptomics data. Finally, we discuss probabilistic interpretations of ρPCA that provide further insight into its effective performance.

recent_papers.title: Extrinsic biological stochasticity and technical noise normalization of single-cell RNA sequencing data
recent_papers.abstract: The technical noise introduced during single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has led to the use of size factor normalization as a first step prior to data analysis. However, this scaling approach inherently affects extrinsic (between cell) variability of gene expression, which stems from both biological and technical factors. Based on previous models on biological and technical extrinsic noise, we propose a general extrinsic noise model for scRNA-seq to provide a theoretical basis for size factor normalization, thus providing a framework for estimating both biological and technical components of extrinsic noise. We highlight the relationship between normalized gene expression covariance, extrinsic noise, and overdispersion, showing that extrinsic noise explains the baseline overdispersion commonly observed in scRNA-seq data. We validated the technical model by testing the relationship on data from pooled RNA. Interestingly, our model accurately describes mature mRNA counts but not nascent mRNA counts, suggesting the need for an alternative technical model for data derived from nascent transcripts. Using single-cell RNA-seq data, we characterize both biological and technical extrinsic noise and cell size factors estimated using Poisson-like genes. Overall, our model helps clarify common misconceptions and provides insight into the role of extrinsic noise and size factor normalization in scRNA-seq data.

recent_papers.title: CTIM-07. A PHASE 1 STUDY OF INTRAVENTRICULARLY ADMINISTERED AUTOLOGOUS HER2-TARGETING CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T CELLS (HER2-CAR T CELLS), WITH AND WITHOUT LYMPHODEPLETION, IN HER2-POSITIVE BREAST CANCER PATIENTS WITH RECURRENT BRAIN AND/OR LEPTOMENINGEAL METASTASES
recent_papers.abstract:
Despite significant advances in the management of HER2-positive breast cancer, as many as 50% of patients will develop CNS metastases. We performed a first-in-human study of intraventricularly administering memory-enriched autologous HER2(EQ)BBζ/CD19t+ T cells in this patient population.
 
 
 
 Participants were treated on 1 of 3 HER2-CAR T cell dose levels: 22 million(M),110 M, or 220 M divided into 3 weekly doses during the dose-limiting toxicity evaluation period (DLT-EP). HER2-CAR T cells were administered via an intraventricular Rickham catheter. CSF samples were obtained before and 24 hours after each dose. After the first ten participants, the study was amended to include lymphodepletion (LD) with fludarabine/cyclophosphamide prior to CAR T cells.
 
 
 
 Twenty-three participants received HER2-CAR T cells; median number of doses was 5 (range 2-13). The most frequent CAR T cell associated adverse events were headaches, nausea/vomiting, and flu-like symptoms. Two DLTs occurred related to grade 3 headaches and other symptoms, which prevented two participants from receiving all the planned CAR T cells during the DLT-EP. Best response was stable disease (SD): median duration of SD for the CAR T only group was 56 days (range 50-136); LD+CAR T group was 56 days (range 44-134); 44% of CAR T only and 64% of LD+CAR T participants achieved SD.HER2-CAR T cell peak and persistence in CSF was relatively dose-dependent and higher with the addition of LD. Significant cytokine changes

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My lab sits at the intersection of computation and experiment — we develop both the mathematical foundations and the wet-lab methods needed to make sense of genomics data. Whether we're proving theorems about pseudoalignment, building tools like kallisto for uniform preprocessing of bacterial single-cell RNA-seq, or extending infrastructure like SpatialFeatureExperiment for spatial genomics, I care about doing things rigorously and making the results broadly useful through open-source software.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for people who are genuinely comfortable crossing disciplinary boundaries — students in my group come from biochemistry, computer science, mathematics, molecular biology, physics, and statistics, and that breadth is intentional. More than any single skill set, I value intellectual curiosity and the willingness to engage seriously with both the biology and the mathematics of a problem.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week involves a mix of coding, reading, and discussion — you might be working through a proof, debugging a pipeline, or analyzing a new single-cell dataset. We are an experimental lab as well as a computational one, so some students will also spend time at the bench developing or applying new assays.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to give students enough freedom to develop their own scientific voice while making sure they have concrete problems to make progress on. I expect people to engage deeply with the literature and to think carefully about whether their methods are solving real biological problems, as we did when designing pseudoassembly approaches to identifying cell-type-specific variants from single-cell RNA-seq data.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I currently have no active grants listed, so prospective students should discuss funding arrangements directly with me. Our lab has a strong track record of producing widely used software and high-impact work, and I actively work to support lab members through fellowships and other mechanisms.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read some of our recent papers — from uniform preprocessing of bacterial scRNA-seq to optimizing alluvial plots for multivariate visualization — to get a sense of how broadly we think about problems. If our style of combining algorithmic ideas, statistical theory, and biological application excites you, reach out with a specific explanation of why your background fits that approach.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab genuinely has people from math, CS, and wet-lab biology all talking to each other, and that's not just a talking point — I've had a statistician help me think through a sequencing artifact and a biochemist push back on one of my algorithmic assumptions in the same week. It can feel a little chaotic because there's no single dominant paradigm, but that's also where the interesting ideas come from.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: There are regular group meetings where someone presents work in progress, and Lior engages pretty deeply with the details — he'll push on the math even in what you thought was a biology-focused presentation. One-on-ones happen but you often have to be proactive about scheduling them; he's not going to check in on you every Tuesday by default.
student_survey_responses.independence: The freedom is real and I mean that in both the good and the slightly scary sense — you're expected to develop your own project direction fairly early on, and there isn't a rigid pipeline of 'here's your chapter one.' If you come in knowing exactly what you want to work on and can articulate why it matters biologically and computationally, you'll thrive; if you need more scaffolding, you may have to ask for it explicitly.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how seriously the lab takes open-source software as a research output — it's not just a byproduct, it's treated as first-class science, and that means time spent on documentation and usability actually matters here. Also, the funding situation is worth having a candid conversation about before you commit.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who genuinely like reading math papers and biology papers in the same sitting, and who get excited about a tool being used by thousands of people in the field, do really well here. If you have a side interest in information theory or combinatorics that you thought was unrelated to genomics, it will probably turn out to be relevant.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you want a clearly scoped thesis problem handed to you on day one, the ambiguity here can be genuinely stressful — some students take a long time to land on a coherent project arc. The breadth that makes the lab exciting also means there isn't always a senior grad student who's an expert in exactly what you're doing, so you may be navigating new territory with less peer mentorship than in more focused labs.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Honestly the culture rewards people who are intellectually aggressive in seminars and meetings — Lior models that style and the lab follows his lead, which I find energizing but I've seen it intimidate newer students who aren't used to having their assumptions challenged publicly. There's a real commitment to rigor, not just computational rigor but also asking whether the biology actually justifies the model.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Group meetings are substantive and happen regularly, and Lior is present and engaged, not just skimming his laptop — he'll remember a detail you mentioned three weeks ago and connect it to something you said today. Individual meetings are less structured; I'd say it averages out to roughly biweekly for most students, but it depends a lot on where you are in your project.
student_survey_responses.independence: You get a lot of rope, which I appreciate now but found disorienting in my first year. The expectation is that you're reading broadly enough to identify important open problems yourself, and he'll help you stress-test those ideas rather than just assigning you a safe question.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The lab does real wet-lab work, not just in name — I came in thinking I'd be purely computational and ended up spending meaningful time at the bench developing an assay, which I actually ended up loving, but it was a surprise. Also, engaging with the blog and public-facing work is quietly valued here more than I initially realized.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who can hold a probabilistic or information-theoretic lens and a molecular biology question in their head simultaneously and find that exciting rather than exhausting tend to produce the most interesting work. Being comfortable with your code being scrutinized and eventually open-sourced is also basically a prerequisite.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: The lack of a formalized structure around grants and funding checkpoints means you need to be your own advocate for fellowships and you have to ask direct questions about your financial situation rather than assuming it's sorted. Students who work best when they have a defined role within a larger funded project may find the open-endedness here more stressful than liberating.

recruiting_status.rationale: active lab signals present (funding or publications) but no explicit recruitment statement

name: Matt Thomson

recent_papers.title: Unraveling cell differentiation mechanisms through topological exploration of single-cell developmental trajectories
recent_papers.abstract: Understanding the circuits that control cell differentiation is a fundamental problem in developmental biology. Single-cell RNA sequencing has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating this problem. However, the reconstruction of developmental trajectories is based on the assumption that cell states traverse a tree-like structure, which may bias our understanding of critical developmental mechanisms. To address this limitation, we developed a framework, TopGen, that enables identifying topological signatures of functional biological circuits as persistent homology groups in transcriptome space. First, we show that TopGen can identify genetic drivers of topological structures in simulated datasets. We then applied our approach to more than ten single-cell developmental atlases and found that topological transcriptome spaces are predominantly path-connected and only sometimes simply connected. Finally, we applied TopGen to analyze gene expression patterns in topological loops representing stem-like, transdifferentiation, and convergent cell circuits, found in C. elegans, H. vulgaris, and N. vectensis, respectively. Our results show that some essential differentiation mechanisms use non-trivial topological motifs, and that these motifs can be conserved in a cell-type–specific manner. Thus, our approach to studying the topological properties of developmental transcriptome atlases opens new possibilities for understanding cell development and differentiation.

recent_papers.title: Theoretical limits of energy extraction in active fluids
recent_papers.abstract: Active materials form a class of far-from-equilibrium systems that are driven internally and exhibit self-organization, which can be harnessed to perform mechanical work. Inspired by experiments on synthetic active polymer networks, in this paper we examine limits of work extraction from an active viscoelastic medium by analyzing the transport of a particle. The active viscoelastic material possesses an equilibrium density where the active and passive forces are balanced out. In a one-dimensional system, a gliding activation front (AF), which converts a passive to an active medium, provides active energy at a constant rate, which is injected into the system at one end and propagates to the other. We demonstrate that there exists a maximum velocity of the AF, above which the activated region fails to deliver the transport power. We hypothesize and intuitively argue based on the limit cases that the feasibility and the velocity of transport of the particle can be interpreted in terms of the velocity of an equilibration domain wall of the field, which is set by two parameters: (i) a measure of activity, and (ii) the viscoelastic timescale. The phase diagram is divided into “transport” and “no-transport” sectors, namely, for any pair of the two parameters, there exists a threshold velocity of the AF above which the particle transport becomes impossible. recent_papers.title: Spin glasses, error correcting codes, and synchronization of human stem cell organoids.
recent_papers.abstract: Magnetic spins, pendulum clocks, and fireflies all self-organize into coherent collectives when arranged into groups of spatially coupled and interacting individuals. Ramanathan and colleagues demonstrate that spatial coupling of human stem cell organoids induces coherent progression through developmental transitions, allowing the dissection of molecular circuits underlying human development.

Single-cell RNA sequencing has emerged as a powerful tool for investigating this problem. However, the reconstruction of developmental trajectories is based on the assumption that cell states traverse a tree-like structure, which may bias our understanding of critical developmental mechanisms. To address this limitation, we developed a framework, TopGen, that enables identifying topological signatures of functional biological circuits as persistent homology groups in transcriptome space. First, we show that TopGen can identify genetic drivers of topological structures in simulated datasets. We then applied our approach to more than ten single-cell developmental atlases and found that topological transcriptome spaces are predominantly path-connected and only sometimes simply connected. Finally, we applied TopGen to analyze gene expression patterns in topological loops representing stem-like, transdifferentiation, and convergent cell circuits, found in C. elegans, H. vulgaris, and N. vectensis, respectively. Our results show that some essential differentiation mechanisms use non-trivial topological motifs, and that these motifs can be conserved in a cell-type–specific manner. Thus, our approach to studying the topological properties of developmental transcriptome atlases opens new possibilities for understanding cell development and differentiation.

Magnetic spins, pendulum clocks, and fireflies all self-organize into coherent collectives when arranged into groups of spatially coupled and interacting individuals. Ramanathan and colleagues demonstrate that spatial coupling of human stem cell organoids induces coherent progression through developmental transitions, allowing the dissection of molecular circuits underlying human development.

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My lab is driven by the conviction that the most profound properties of biological systems — from how cells differentiate to how neural circuits self-organize — emerge from collective interactions that span molecular, cellular, and organismal scales. We formulate predictive mathematical models grounded in high-dimensional data, and we push those models back into the lab to reprogram and engineer biological systems to achieve new behaviors. Whether we're probing the topological structure of single-cell developmental trajectories or examining the limits of work extraction in active viscoelastic materials, the unifying thread is a physics-inspired search for organizing principles.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are genuinely comfortable sitting at the intersection of disciplines — someone equally willing to run a biochemistry experiment, derive a statistical physics argument, or implement a machine learning pipeline. Because our work spans cytoskeletal active matter, single-cell genomics, and stem cell organoids, intellectual range and a tolerance for ambiguity matter more to me than a narrow technical specialty. I'm especially drawn to people who are motivated by big, open questions rather than incremental problems.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in the lab blends experimental and computational work — you might spend part of the week analyzing single-cell RNA-seq data to reconstruct developmental trajectories and another part building or running a physical active-matter assay. We hold regular group meetings where we discuss both our own results and papers from the broader biophysics and machine learning literature, so students should expect to read widely and present often. Collaboration is the norm: projects like our organoid synchronization work draw simultaneously on statistical physics intuitions and stem cell biology, so you'll routinely work across those boundaries.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to mentor students as junior colleagues rather than trainees — I want you developing your own scientific taste and learning to ask the questions, not just answer them. Early on I invest heavily in helping students build the quantitative and experimental toolkit they need, and as projects mature I push students toward increasing independence in experimental design and in communicating results. I think the best mentorship happens through close engagement with actual data and models together, so I make a point of staying technically involved in projects rather than managing from a distance.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: No active grants are listed in our current records, so I would encourage applicants to reach out to me directly for the most up-to-date picture of the lab's funding situation and available positions. Our research spans active matter, machine learning, and single-cell biology, and I actively pursue support through multiple channels to keep those programs moving. Prospective students should discuss funding and fellowship opportunities — including NSF and NIH predoctoral fellowships — with me during the application process.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read a couple of our recent papers carefully before you write to me — whether it's our work on topological analysis of developmental trajectories or the spin-glass framework we applied to organoid synchronization — and tell me concretely which scientific question excites you and why. The students who thrive here are those who can bridge quantitative modeling and experiment, so in your application highlight any experience you have in both domains, even if it's modest. Most importantly, come with genuine curiosity about how collective behavior gives rise to biological function; that intellectual drive is what the lab runs on.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab genuinely operates like a small research collective — people are expected to have opinions about each other's projects, and it's normal to get pulled into a conversation about someone else's single-cell trajectory analysis even when you came in to run a gel. It's intellectually stimulating but can feel like a lot if you're someone who needs clear project boundaries.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have a weekly group meeting that alternates between paper discussions and project updates, and Matt tends to assign pretty ambitious reading from biophysics, ML, and even condensed matter literature. One-on-ones are less structured — you kind of have to advocate for time with him when things get busy, which took me a semester to figure out.
student_survey_responses.independence: He gives you real independence earlier than I expected, which is exciting but occasionally disorienting — I once spent three weeks going in a direction before realizing I should have checked in sooner. That said, when you do sit down together over data or a model, he's genuinely technically engaged, not just nodding along.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I had known how much breadth the lab actually demands day-to-day — I came in strong on the computational side and assumed I could lean on that, but within a few months I was expected to also be doing physical active-matter assays, which required picking up wet lab skills from scratch. It's doable, but budget time for that learning curve.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who get restless on narrow problems and want to feel like they're working on something genuinely open-ended do really well here. If you can hold a statistical physics argument and a biological mechanism in your head at the same time and find that energizing rather than exhausting, this is probably a good fit.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who need a clearly scoped project with defined milestones early on can find the ambiguity stressful — the lab's philosophy is to chase organizing principles, which is exciting but means the goalposts can shift as the science evolves. Also, if you're not proactive about keeping Matt updated on your progress, you can drift for longer than is healthy.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small lab with a big-swing mentality — conversations at the whiteboard can jump from cytoskeletal rheology to information theory in about four minutes, and that's considered a normal Tuesday. The culture is collaborative and pretty non-hierarchical, but it also means there isn't a lot of hand-holding built into the structure.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Group meeting is a real commitment — Matt expects you to have read the assigned papers deeply enough to argue about them, not just summarize them. Beyond that, project-level check-ins depend on where you are in your PhD; early-stage students seem to get more frequent face time, but as you advance you're expected to drive the cadence yourself.
student_survey_responses.independence: The 'junior colleague' framing is real — by year two he's genuinely asking for your take on experimental design decisions rather than just assigning you tasks, which felt validating. The flip side is that if you're stuck, the expectation is that you've already tried several things before bringing it to him.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: Funding conversations should happen early and explicitly — I didn't push on that during recruitment and had a stressful few months in my second year sorting out fellowship options. He's supportive when you bring it up, but he won't necessarily volunteer the full picture unless you ask specific questions.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: The students I've seen do best are the ones who are genuinely motivated by the question rather than the technique — people who treat learning a new method (whether that's implementing a variational autoencoder or setting up an in vitro reconstitution assay) as exciting rather than as a detour. Curiosity really is the operative currency here.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you come in expecting a tight experimental system with a clear pipeline and a definable thesis by year three, the lab's sprawl across active matter, genomics, and organoid biology can feel unfocused. It also helps to be comfortable presenting work that's still rough — Matt expects students to put things in front of the group before they're polished, and not everyone finds that easy.

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

name: Anima Anandkumar

recent_papers.title: Neural operators for accelerating scientific simulations and design
recent_papers.abstract: Scientific discovery and engineering design are currently limited by the time and cost of physical experiments. Numerical simulations are an alternative approach but are usually intractable for complex real-world problems. Artificial intelligence promises a solution through fast data-driven surrogate models. In particular, neural operators present a principled framework for learning mappings between functions defined on continuous domains, such as spatiotemporal processes and partial differential equations. Neural operators can extrapolate and predict solutions at new locations unseen during training. They can be integrated with physics and other domain constraints enforced at finer resolutions to obtain high-fidelity solutions and good generalization. Neural operators are differentiable, so they can directly optimize parameters for inverse design and other inverse problems. Neural operators can therefore augment, or even replace, existing numerical simulators in many applications, such as computational fluid dynamics, weather forecasting and material modelling, providing speedups of four to five orders of magnitude. Neural operators learn mappings between functions on continuous domains, such as spatiotemporal processes and partial differential equations, offering a fast, data-driven surrogate model solution for otherwise intractable numerical simulations of complex real-world problems.

recent_papers.title: Near-term distributed quantum computation using mean-field corrections and auxiliary qubits
recent_papers.abstract: Distributed quantum computation is often proposed to increase the scalability of quantum hardware, as it reduces cooperative noise and requisite connectivity by sharing quantum information between distant quantum devices. However, such exchange of quantum information itself poses unique engineering challenges, requiring high gate fidelity and costly non-local operations. To mitigate this, we propose near-term distributed quantum computing, focusing on approximate approaches that involve limited information transfer and conservative entanglement production. We first devise an approximate distributed computing scheme for the time evolution of quantum systems split across any combination of classical and quantum devices. Our procedure harnesses mean-field corrections and auxiliary qubits to link two or more devices classically, optimally encoding the auxiliary qubits to both minimize short-time evolution error and extend the approximate scheme’s performance to longer evolution times. We then expand the scheme to include limited quantum information transfer through selective qubit shuffling or teleportation, broadening our method’s applicability and boosting its performance. Finally, we build upon these concepts to produce an approximate circuit-cutting technique for the fragmented pre-training of variational quantum algorithms. To characterize our technique, we introduce a non-linear perturbation theory that discerns the critical role of our mean-field corrections in optimizatio

recent_papers.title: Geometry-Informed Neural Operator for Large-Scale 3D PDEs
recent_papers.abstract: We propose the geometry-informed neural operator (GINO), a highly efficient approach to learning the solution operator of large-scale partial differential equations with varying geometries. GINO uses a signed distance function and point-cloud representations of the input shape and neural operators based on graph and Fourier architectures to learn the solution operator. The graph neural operator handles irregular grids and transforms them into and from regular latent grids on which Fourier neural operator can be efficiently applied. GINO is discretization-convergent, meaning the trained model can be applied to arbitrary discretization of the continuous domain and it converges to the continuum operator as the discretization is refined. To empirically validate the performance of our method on large-scale simulation, we generate the industry-standard aerodynamics dataset of 3D vehicle geometries with Reynolds numbers as high as five million. For this large-scale 3D fluid simulation, numerical methods are expensive to compute surface pressure. We successfully trained GINO to predict the pressure on car surfaces using only five hundred data points. The cost-accuracy experiments show a $26,000 \times$ speed-up compared to optimized GPU-based computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulators on computing the drag coefficient. When tested on new combinations of geometries and boundary conditions (inlet velocities), GINO obtains a one-fourth reduction in error rate compared to deep neural

recent_papers.title: Tipping Point Forecasting in Non-Stationary Dynamics on Function Spaces
recent_papers.abstract: Tipping points are abrupt, drastic, and often irreversible changes in the evolution of non-stationary and chaotic dynamical systems. For instance, increased greenhouse gas concentrations are predicted to lead to drastic decreases in low cloud cover, referred to as a climatological tipping point. In this paper, we learn the evolution of such non-stationary dynamical systems using a novel recurrent neural operator (RNO), which learns mappings between function spaces. After training RNO on only the pre-tipping dynamics, we employ it to detect future tipping points using an uncertainty-based approach. In particular, we propose a conformal prediction framework to forecast tipping points by monitoring deviations from physics constraints (such as conserved quantities and partial differential equations), enabling forecasting of these abrupt changes along with a rigorous measure of uncertainty. We illustrate our proposed methodology on non-stationary ordinary and partial differential equations, such as the Lorenz-63 and Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equations. We also apply our methods to forecast a climate tipping point in stratocumulus cloud cover and airfoil wake and stall transitions using only limited knowledge of the governing equations. For the latter, we show that our proposed method zero-shot generalizes to forecasting multiple future tipping points under varying Reynolds numbers. In our experiments, we demonstrate that even partial or approximate physics constraints can be used to ac

recent_papers.title: Incrementally-Computable Neural Networks: Efficient Inference for Dynamic Inputs
recent_papers.abstract: Deep learning often faces the challenge of efficiently processing dynamic inputs, such as sensor data or user inputs. For example, an AI writing assistant is required to update its suggestions in real time as a document is edited. Re-running the model each time is expensive, even with compression techniques like knowledge distillation, pruning, or quantization. Instead, we take an incremental computing approach, looking to reuse calculations as the inputs change. However, the dense connectivity of conventional architectures poses a major obstacle to incremental computation, as even minor input changes cascade through the network and restrict information reuse. To address this, we use vector quantization to discretize intermediate values in the network, which filters out noisy and unnecessary modifications to hidden neurons, facilitating the reuse of their values. We apply this approach to the transformers architecture, creating an efficient incremental inference algorithm with complexity proportional to the fraction of the modified inputs. Our experiments with adapting the OPT-125M pre-trained language model demonstrate comparable accuracy on document classification while requiring 12.1X (median) fewer operations for processing sequences of atomic edits.

recent_papers.title: Guaranteed Approximation Bounds for Mixed-Precision Neural Operators
recent_papers.abstract: Neural operators, such as Fourier Neural Operators (FNO), form a principled approach for learning solution operators for PDEs and other mappings between function spaces. However, many real-world problems require high-resolution training data, and the training time and limited GPU memory pose big barriers. One solution is to train neural operators in mixed precision to reduce the memory requirement and increase training speed. However, existing mixed-precision training techniques are designed for standard neural networks, and we find that their direct application to FNO leads to numerical overflow and poor memory efficiency. Further, at first glance, it may appear that mixed precision in FNO will lead to drastic accuracy degradation since reducing the precision of the Fourier transform yields poor results in classical numerical solvers. We show that this is not the case; in fact, we prove that reducing the precision in FNO still guarantees a good approximation bound, when done in a targeted manner. Specifically, we build on the intuition that neural operator learning inherently induces an approximation error, arising from discretizing the infinite-dimensional ground-truth input function, implying that training in full precision is not needed. We formalize this intuition by rigorously characterizing the approximation and precision errors of FNO and bounding these errors for general input functions. We prove that the precision error is asymptotically comparable to the approximat

recent_papers.title: Artificial Intelligence for Science in Quantum, Atomistic, and Continuum Systems
recent_papers.abstract: Advances in artificial intelligence (AI) are fueling a new paradigm of discoveries in natural sciences. Today, AI has started to advance natural sciences by improving, accelerating, and enabling our understanding of natural phenomena at a wide range of spatial and temporal scales, giving rise to a new area of research known as AI for science (AI4Science). Being an emerging research paradigm, AI4Science is unique in that it is an enormous and highly interdisciplinary area. Thus, a unified and technical treatment of this field is needed yet challenging. This work aims to provide a technically thorough account of a subarea of AI4Science; namely, AI for quantum, atomistic, and continuum systems. These areas aim at understanding the physical world from the subatomic (wavefunctions and electron density), atomic (molecules, proteins, materials, and interactions), to macro (fluids, climate, and subsurface) scales and form an important subarea of AI4Science. A unique advantage of focusing on these areas is that they largely share a common set of challenges, thereby allowing a unified and foundational treatment. A key common challenge is how to capture physics first principles, especially symmetries, in natural systems by deep learning methods. We provide an in-depth yet intuitive account of techniques to achieve equivariance to symmetry transformations. We also discuss other common technical challenges, including explainability, out-of-distribution generalization, knowledge transfer w

recent_papers.title: LeanDojo: Theorem Proving with Retrieval-Augmented Language Models
recent_papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in proving formal theorems using proof assistants such as Lean. However, existing methods are difficult to reproduce or build on, due to private code, data, and large compute requirements. This has created substantial barriers to research on machine learning methods for theorem proving. This paper removes these barriers by introducing LeanDojo: an open-source Lean playground consisting of toolkits, data, models, and benchmarks. LeanDojo extracts data from Lean and enables interaction with the proof environment programmatically. It contains fine-grained annotations of premises in proofs, providing valuable data for premise selection: a key bottleneck in theorem proving. Using this data, we develop ReProver (Retrieval-Augmented Prover): an LLM-based prover augmented with retrieval for selecting premises from a vast math library. It is inexpensive and needs only one GPU week of training. Our retriever leverages LeanDojo's program analysis capability to identify accessible premises and hard negative examples, which makes retrieval much more effective. Furthermore, we construct a new benchmark consisting of 98,734 theorems and proofs extracted from Lean's math library. It features challenging data split requiring the prover to generalize to theorems relying on novel premises that are never used in training. We use this benchmark for training and evaluation, and experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of ReProver over non-r
