pi_survey.research_philosophy: My research sits at the intersection of machine learning and scientific discovery — I believe that principled AI methods, grounded in physics and geometry, can fundamentally transform how we do science. Whether we're developing geometry-informed neural operators for large-scale 3D PDEs or building symmetry-informed representations for molecules and crystalline materials, the goal is always to create tools that respect the underlying structure of the problem rather than treating it as a black box.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are comfortable operating at the boundary between rigorous mathematics and real-world scientific applications — someone who can engage with both the theoretical underpinnings of a method like the Image-to-Image Schrödinger Bridge and its downstream impact in, say, image restoration or medical imaging. Strong foundations in either machine learning, physics, or applied mathematics are essential, and intellectual curiosity that cuts across disciplines is what really sets a candidate apart.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in my group involves a mix of deep technical work — implementing and iterating on models, running experiments, and engaging with the relevant scientific literature — alongside group meetings where we pressure-test ideas together. Students working on projects like AI-enabled multiscale modeling of the SARS-CoV-2 replication transcription complex, for instance, also spend time collaborating closely with domain scientists, so communication and cross-disciplinary engagement are built into the rhythm of the lab.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to mentor students toward increasing independence, starting with well-scoped problems and gradually opening up the research question as they build confidence and expertise. I value direct, substantive feedback and aim to help each student develop their own scientific voice — whether they're working on efficient inference methods like incrementally-computable neural networks or on foundational representation learning for scientific domains.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I don't have specific grants to point to at the moment, but my group is actively engaged in high-impact research areas — AI for scientific simulation, generative modeling, and neural operators — that are attracting significant attention from both academic and industry partners. I am committed to ensuring that students have the computational resources and support they need to pursue ambitious projects.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read our recent papers carefully — pieces like the Geometry-Informed Neural Operator (GINO) or our work on symmetry-informed geometric representations — and think about what open questions they raise for you. In your application, show me not just that you understand the work but that you have genuine ideas about where it could go next; that intellectual initiative is what I find most compelling in a prospective student.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a genuinely interdisciplinary feel — on any given day you might be debugging a neural operator implementation alongside someone whose background is computational chemistry or fluid dynamics, and that cross-pollination is intellectually energizing. It can feel a bit diffuse at times, since people are working on quite different scientific domains, but the group meetings do a reasonable job of creating shared vocabulary.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have a weekly group meeting that rotates who presents, and Anima is usually present and engaged — she asks sharp questions that have a way of surfacing assumptions you didn't know you were making. One-on-ones are less frequent and you often need to be proactive about scheduling them, especially when she's traveling for conferences or industry commitments.
student_survey_responses.independence: The trajectory is real: early projects are fairly well-scoped and you get concrete feedback, but by year two or three the expectation shifts toward you driving the research direction. I personally found that transition a bit abrupt — there's not always a lot of scaffolding for the middle stage — but if you're self-motivated it becomes genuinely freeing.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how important it is to have at least one foot in a scientific domain before joining, not just strong ML chops. The work on things like multiscale molecular modeling or PDE solvers really does require you to communicate fluently with domain scientists, and ramping up on that while also doing ML research is harder than I anticipated.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who can context-switch between theory and implementation thrive — someone who can read a paper on Schrödinger bridges in the morning, write clean PyTorch code in the afternoon, and then explain the physical intuition to a biologist the next day will genuinely love this environment. Intellectual curiosity across disciplines isn't just a slogan here; it's basically a survival skill.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need very structured, frequent mentorship or a single tightly-defined project to feel secure, you may find the setup stressful. The lab's ambitions are broad and Anima's attention is spread across many projects, so students who struggle with ambiguity or who wait to be told what to do next can find themselves adrift.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: There's a high-energy, high-expectation atmosphere — people are working on genuinely hard problems and there's a shared sense that the research matters, which I find motivating most of the time. The flip side is that imposter syndrome is pretty common; the bar for what counts as an interesting result feels high, and early-stage work can feel perpetually provisional.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Group meetings happen weekly and they're substantive — Anima pushes back on methodology and framing in ways that have made my work significantly sharper. Individual check-ins are more variable; I'd say plan on one substantive meeting every two to three weeks and use Slack for async feedback in between.
student_survey_responses.independence: She's genuinely trying to build you into an independent researcher, not a cog executing her vision, and I respect that about the mentorship model. That said, 'incremental independence' in practice means you have to be comfortable with some stretches of uncertainty about whether you're working on the right thing.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd understood that reading papers like GINO or the symmetry-informed representation work before joining isn't just a signal for the application — it's actually the bare minimum baseline assumed once you're here. Joining without deep familiarity with the core methodological threads means a steep catching-up period that overlaps awkwardly with coursework.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who have a strong point of view — who come in with opinions about where a method falls short and what they'd do differently — seem to get the most out of Anima's mentorship style. She responds well to intellectual initiative, and those students tend to get more of her time and more latitude on project direction.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: The computational resource situation can be uneven; access to large-scale compute sometimes depends on timing and external collaborations rather than a predictable allocation, which can bottleneck experiments at frustrating moments. If your research requires frequent large runs, it's worth asking detailed questions about this before committing.

name: Adam Wierman

recent_papers.title: Learning-Augmented Energy-Aware List Scheduling for Precedence-Constrained Tasks
recent_papers.venue: ACM Transactions on Modeling and Performance Evaluation of Computing Systems
recent_papers.abstract: We study the problem of scheduling precedence-constrained tasks to balance between performance and energy consumption. We consider a system with multiple servers capable of speed scaling and seek to schedule precedence-constrained tasks to minimize a linear combination of performance and energy consumption. Inspired by the single-server setting, we propose the concept of pseudo-size for individual tasks, which is a measure of the externalities of a task in the precedence graph and is learned from historical workload data. We then propose a two-stage scheduling framework that uses a learned pseudo-size approximation and achieves a provable approximation bound on the linear combination of performance and energy consumption for both makespan and total weighted completion time, where the quality of the bound depends on the approximation quality of pseudo-sizes. We show experimentally that learning-based approaches consistently perform near optimally.

recent_papers.title: Beyond Black-Box Advice: Learning-Augmented Algorithms for MDPs with Q-Value Predictions
recent_papers.abstract: We study the tradeoff between consistency and robustness in the context of a single-trajectory time-varying Markov Decision Process (MDP) with untrusted machine-learned advice. Our work departs from the typical approach of treating advice as coming from black-box sources by instead considering a setting where additional information about how the advice is generated is available. We prove a first-of-its-kind consistency and robustness tradeoff given Q-value advice under a general MDP model that includes both continuous and discrete state/action spaces. Our results highlight that utilizing Q-value advice enables dynamic pursuit of the better of machine-learned advice and a robust baseline, thus result in near-optimal performance guarantees, which provably improves what can be obtained solely with black-box advice.

recent_papers.title: Online Learning for Robust Voltage Control Under Uncertain Grid Topology
recent_papers.abstract: Voltage control generally requires accurate information about the grid’s topology in order to guarantee network stability. However, accurate topology identification is challenging for existing methods, especially as the grid is subject to increasingly frequent reconfiguration due to the adoption of renewable energy. In this work, we combine a nested convex body chasing algorithm with a robust predictive controller to achieve provably finite-time convergence to safe voltage limits in the online setting where there is uncertainty in both the network topology as well as load and generation variations. In an online fashion, our algorithm narrows down the set of possible grid models that are consistent with observations and adjusts reactive power generation accordingly to keep voltages within desired safety limits. Our approach can also incorporate existing partial knowledge of the network to improve voltage control performance. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in a case study on a Southern California Edison 56-bus distribution system. Our experiments show that in practical settings, the controller is indeed able to narrow the set of consistent topologies quickly enough to make control decisions that ensure stability in both linearized and realistic non-linear models of the distribution grid.

recent_papers.title: Towards Environmentally Equitable AI via Geographical Load Balancing
recent_papers.abstract: Fueled by the soaring popularity of foundation models, the accelerated growth of artificial intelligence (AI) models’ enormous environmental footprint has come under increased scrutiny. While many approaches have been proposed to make AI more energy-efficient and environmentally friendly, environmental inequity — the fact that AI’s environmental footprint can be disproportionately higher in certain regions than in others — has emerged, raising social-ecological justice concerns. This paper takes a first step toward addressing AI’s environmental inequity by fairly balancing its regional environmental impact. Concretely, we focus on the carbon and water footprints of AI model inference and propose equity-aware geographical load balancing (eGLB) to explicitly minimize AI’s highest environmental cost across all the regions. The consideration of environmental equity creates substantial algorithmic challenges as the optimal GLB decisions require complete offline information that is lacking practice. To address the challenges, we introduce auxiliary variables and optimize GLB decisions online based on dual mirror descent. In addition to analyzing the performance of eGLB theoretically, we run trace-based empirical simulations by considering a set of geographically distributed data centers that serve inference requests for a large language AI model. The results demonstrate that existing GLB approaches may amplify environmental inequity while eGLB can significantly reduce the regional

recent_papers.title: Minimization Fractional Prophet Inequalities for Sequential Procurement
recent_papers.abstract: We consider a minimization variant on the classical prophet inequality with monomial cost functions. A firm would like to procure some fixed amount of a divisible commodity from sellers that arrive sequentially. Whenever a seller arrives, the seller’s cost function is revealed, and the firm chooses how much of the commodity to buy. We first show that if one restricts the set of distributions for the coefficients to a family of natural distributions that include, for example, the uniform and truncated normal distributions, then there is a thresholding policy that is asymptotically optimal in the number of sellers. We then compare two scenarios based on whether the firm has in-house production capabilities or not. We precisely compute the optimal algorithm’s competitive ratio when in-house production capabilities exist and for a special case when they do not. We show that the main advantage of the ability to produce the commodity in house is that it shields the firm from price spikes in worst-case scenarios. Funding: This work was supported by NSF Grants [CNS-2146814, CPS-2136197, CNS-2106403, NGSDI-2105648].

recent_papers.title: Adversarial Attacks on Online Learning to Rank with Click Feedback
recent_papers.abstract: Online learning to rank (OLTR) is a sequential decision-making problem where a learning agent selects an ordered list of items and receives feedback through user clicks. Although potential attacks against OLTR algorithms may cause serious losses in real-world applications, little is known about adversarial attacks on OLTR. This paper studies attack strategies against multiple variants of OLTR. Our first result provides an attack strategy against the UCB algorithm on classical stochastic bandits with binary feedback, which solves the key issues caused by bounded and discrete feedback that previous works can not handle. Building on this result, we design attack algorithms against UCB-based OLTR algorithms in position-based and cascade models. Finally, we propose a general attack strategy against any algorithm under the general click model. Each attack algorithm manipulates the learning agent into choosing the target attack item $T-o(T)$ times, incurring a cumulative cost of $o(T)$. Experiments on synthetic and real data further validate the effectiveness of our proposed attack algorithms.

recent_papers.title: Online Adversarial Stabilization of Unknown Linear Time-Varying Systems
recent_papers.venue: IEEE Conference on Decision and Control
recent_papers.abstract: This paper studies the problem of online stabilization of an unknown discrete-time linear time-varying (LTV) system under bounded non-stochastic (potentially adversarial) disturbances. We propose a novel algorithm based on convex body chasing (CBC). Under the assumption of infrequently changing or slowly drifting dynamics, the algorithm guarantees bounded-input-bounded-output stability in the closed loop. Our approach avoids system identification and applies, with minimal disturbance assumptions, to a variety of LTV systems of practical importance. We demonstrate the algorithm numerically on examples of LTV systems including Markov linear jump systems with finitely many jumps.

recent_papers.title: Contextual Combinatorial Bandits with Probabilistically Triggered Arms
recent_papers.abstract: We study contextual combinatorial bandits with probabilistically triggered arms (C$^2$MAB-T) under a variety of smoothness conditions that capture a wide range of applications, such as contextual cascading bandits and contextual influence maximization bandits. Under the triggering probability modulated (TPM) condition, we devise the C$^2$-UCB-T algorithm and propose a novel analysis that achieves an $\tilde{O}(d\sqrt{KT})$ regret bound, removing a potentially exponentially large factor $O(1/p_{\min})$, where $d$ is the dimension of contexts, $p_{\min}$ is the minimum positive probability that any arm can be triggered, and batch-size $K$ is the maximum number of arms that can be triggered per round. Under the variance modulated (VM) or triggering probability and variance modulated (TPVM) conditions, we propose a new variance-adaptive algorithm VAC$^2$-UCB and derive a regret bound $\tilde{O}(d\sqrt{T})$, which is independent of the batch-size $K$. As a valuable by-product, our analysis technique and variance-adaptive algorithm can be applied to the CMAB-T and C$^2$MAB setting, improving existing results there as well. We also include experiments that demonstrate the improved performance of our algorithms compared with benchmark algorithms on synthetic and real-world datasets.

funding_sources.title: Collaborative Research: National Science Foundation Expeditions in Computing: Carbon Connect -- An Ecosystem for Sustainable Computing

nsf_grants.title: Collaborative Research: National Science Foundation Expeditions in Computing: Carbon Connect -- An Ecosystem for Sustainable Computing

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My work sits at the intersection of theory and real-world impact — I want the algorithms we design to come with rigorous guarantees but also to tackle problems that genuinely matter, from sustainable computing to grid stability. A thread running through projects like 'Learning-Augmented Energy-Aware List Scheduling for Precedence-Constrained Tasks' and 'Online Learning for Robust Voltage Control Under Uncertain Grid Topology' is the conviction that machine learning and classical algorithmic theory are stronger together than apart. I'm particularly excited by settings where uncertainty is unavoidable and we need to be honest about what we can and cannot certify.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are comfortable moving between mathematical rigor and systems-level thinking — someone who can prove a theorem in the morning and think carefully about what it means for a real data center or power grid in the afternoon. Intellectual curiosity and the willingness to push into genuinely open problems are more important to me than having a perfectly polished background. A demonstrated interest in areas like online algorithms, control, or the sustainability of computing is a strong signal.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: Most weeks involve a mix of individual research time, one-on-one meetings with me, and broader group discussions where we workshop each other's ideas and proofs. Students working on projects like our geographical load balancing work for AI equity or our learning-augmented control papers are expected to engage with both the theoretical and applied sides of their problem — reading relevant systems papers one day and working through proof details the next. Collaboration across groups and with external partners is common, especially on our NSF Expeditions Carbon Connect project.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to calibrate my involvement to where a student is in their development — early on I'll work closely to help identify tractable but meaningful research directions, and I gradually step back as students build their own scientific voice. I think honest, direct feedback delivered with genuine care is the most useful thing I can offer, and I take seriously the responsibility to help students grow not just as researchers but as communicators and independent thinkers. I also try to connect students with collaborators and opportunities that match their longer-term goals.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: My group is well-supported through 2029 via the NSF Expeditions in Computing grant, Carbon Connect — An Ecosystem for Sustainable Computing, which provides a rich collaborative environment for students interested in sustainable and equitable computing. This grant funds work across multiple fronts, including the environmental equity questions raised in our geographical load balancing paper, so there is genuine room for students to find their own niche within a larger funded agenda. I actively pursue additional funding and collaborations to keep the group's research portfolio broad.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read a couple of our recent papers — something like 'Certifying Black-Box Policies With Stability for Nonlinear Control' or 'Beyond Black-Box Advice: Learning-Augmented Algorithms for MDPs with Q-Value Predictions' — and think about what questions they leave open for you. In your application, be specific about what draws you to this kind of work rather than writing generically about machine learning or theory; I want to see that you've thought carefully about the problems, not just the field. Reaching out briefly with a concrete, focused question is welcome, but the most important thing you can do is submit a strong application that shows your thinking.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The group has a genuine workshop culture — when someone is stuck on a proof or a model isn't behaving, people will actually sit down together and dig into it rather than just offering vague encouragement. There's a mix of theory-heavy folks and people who care a lot about the systems side, and those conversations across that divide are where a lot of the interesting ideas come from.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: I have a weekly one-on-one with Adam that's usually 30–45 minutes, and there's a group meeting that rotates between paper readings and in-progress work presentations. Early in my PhD he was pretty hands-on — we'd sometimes add an extra check-in if I was spinning my wheels — but that's tapered off as I've gotten more independent.
student_survey_responses.independence: You get real ownership over your direction, but it doesn't feel like being dropped in the ocean — more like being given a map and then asked to find your own route. The Carbon Connect project gives a broad framework, so there's structure, but within that you're expected to identify the actual research questions yourself, which was harder than I anticipated at first.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd understood earlier that 'moving between theory and systems' is not just a slogan — Adam will genuinely push you to engage with both, and if you're someone who instinctively wants to just stay in the math, that friction is real and you should prepare for it. It's ultimately made me a better researcher, but the adjustment took longer than I expected.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you like problems where the stakes feel tangible — like, the grid actually has to stay stable, the data center actually consumes real energy — and you want the rigor of a theory group without losing sight of that, this is a great fit. Students who are comfortable holding ambiguity and iterating on half-formed ideas out loud in group meeting do well.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need very tight, pre-specified project scopes tend to feel unmoored, because Adam's style is to help you find the right question more than to hand you one. Also, if your background is entirely systems or entirely pure math with no appetite to stretch, the expectation to engage across that boundary can be genuinely uncomfortable.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's collaborative but not cozy in a hand-holdy way — people are friendly and will read your drafts, but the expectation is that you come to discussions having actually thought hard about the problem, not just hoping someone else will solve it with you. The external collaborations through NSF Carbon Connect mean you're often interacting with people outside Caltech, which keeps things from feeling insular.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Weekly one-on-ones are the norm, and Adam is pretty consistent about keeping them even during busy periods, which I appreciate. The group meeting cadence is roughly biweekly, and honestly the peer feedback there has been some of the most useful input I've gotten — sometimes more actionable than the one-on-ones because lab mates are closer to the same technical trench you're in.
student_survey_responses.independence: Adam explicitly said he'd step back as I developed my own voice, and that's accurate — by year three I was mostly steering my own project, with him acting more as a sounding board. The early phase involves more joint direction-setting, and the pace of that transition depends a lot on how proactively you drive the conversation.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The learning-augmented algorithms framing that runs through a lot of the lab's work has a specific aesthetic — you need to care about worst-case guarantees AND average performance AND practical relevance simultaneously — and internalizing what that actually means for how you design and evaluate algorithms takes time. I spent a semester producing work that felt theoretically fine but didn't satisfy that full standard.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who read broadly and get genuinely curious about open problems — rather than just trying to produce a paper — seem to flourish, because Adam responds well to intellectual enthusiasm and will invest more when he sees it. If you have some background in online algorithms, control theory, or sustainability-adjacent systems work, there's a ready-made community to plug into.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you're primarily motivated by publishing quickly and you're not yet comfortable with the uncertainty of open-ended problems, the early years can feel frustrating because the problems are hard and the feedback loop is slow. The direct feedback style is also not for everyone — Adam means it caringly, but he won't soften a critique if he thinks your argument has a gap.

recruiting_status.rationale: active lab signals present (funding or publications) but no explicit recruitment statement

name: David Van Valen

recent_papers.title: CellSAM: a foundation model for cell segmentation
recent_papers.abstract: Cells are a fundamental unit of biological organization, and identifying them in imaging data—cell segmentation—is a critical task for various cellular imaging experiments. Although deep learning methods have led to substantial progress on this problem, most models are specialist models that work well for specific domains but cannot be applied across domains or scale well with large amounts of data. Here we present CellSAM, a universal model for cell segmentation that generalizes across diverse cellular imaging data. CellSAM builds on top of the Segment Anything Model (SAM) by developing a prompt engineering approach for mask generation. We train an object detector, CellFinder, to automatically detect cells and prompt SAM to generate segmentations. We show that this approach allows a single model to achieve human-level performance for segmenting images of mammalian cells, yeast and bacteria collected across various imaging modalities. We show that CellSAM has strong zero-shot performance and can be improved with a few examples via few-shot learning. Additionally, we demonstrate how CellSAM can be applied across diverse bioimage analysis workflows. A deployed version of CellSAM is available at https://cellsam.deepcell.org/. CellSAM uses an object detector, CellFinder, to detect cells and prompt the Segment Anything Model (SAM) to generate segmentations. This universal model achieves human-level performance across a range of bioimaging data encompassing mammalian cells, yeast a

recent_papers.title: Uncovering the molecular basis of kinase activity and substrate recognition with phospho-PCA
recent_papers.abstract: Protein kinases relay information to various cellular processes, and their dysregulation underlies numerous human diseases. Despite their importance, our understanding of how kinase domains and their variants impact protein stability, catalytic activity, and substrate recognition is incomplete. In this work, we develop the phosphorylation protein complementation assay (phospho-PCA), which enables quantitative measurements of kinase-substrate interactions by coupling them to the growth of bud-ding yeast, thereby enabling deep mutational scanning of kinase domains. When combined with deep mutational scans targeting folding stability and Bayesian modeling, phospho-PCA can disentangle the relative impact of mutations on kinase domain stability, catalytic activity, and substrate specificity. We demonstrate the accuracy and breadth of phospho-PCA, showing its applicability to both tyrosine and serine/threonine kinase domains. We then apply our method to three closely related protein kinases with distinct substrate preferences, evaluating over 15,000 kinase variants against a panel of three substrates for both catalytic activity and substrate specificity. The resulting dataset constitutes the largest and most detailed variant-to-function map assembled for this enzyme family to date, revealing numerous mutations that alter kinase activity and substrate specificity. Physics-based modeling reveals how these mutations operate through diverse mechanisms, including long-range allosteric c

recent_papers.title: Flow Autoencoders are Effective Protein Tokenizers
recent_papers.abstract: Protein structure tokenizers enable the creation of multimodal models of protein structure, sequence, and function. Current approaches to protein structure tok-enization rely on bespoke components that are invariant to spatial symmetries, but that are challenging to optimize and scale. We present Kanzi, a flow-based tokenizer for tokenization and generation of protein structures. Kanzi consists of a diffusion autoencoder trained with a flow matching loss. We show that this approach simplifies several aspects of protein structure tokenizers: frame-based representations can be replaced with global coordinates, complex losses are replaced with a single flow matching loss, and SE(3)-invariant attention operations can be replaced with standard attention. We find that these changes stabilize the training of parameter-efficient models that outperform existing to- kenizers on reconstruction metrics at a fraction of the model size and training cost. An autoregressive model trained with Kanzi outperforms similar generative models that operate over tokens, although it does not yet match the performance of state-of-the-art continuous diffusion models. Code is available here: https://github.com/rdilip/kanzi/.

recent_papers.title: Modulation of bacterial cell size and growth rate via activation of a cell envelope stress response
recent_papers.abstract: ABSTRACT Cells mount elaborate responses to environmental stresses across multiple scales. In enterobacteria, the Rcs phosphorelay is activated by cell-envelope damage and by alterations to periplasmic thickness and cell width. To explore Rcs activation in the absence of external stresses, we investigated the physiological and morphological consequences of Rcs activation in Escherichia coli. We used an inducible variant of the lipoprotein RcsF that mislocalizes to the inner membrane (RcsFIM) and constitutively activates the Rcs system. RcsFIM expression slowed growth and reduced the cell length added per cell cycle, independent of Rcs-regulated capsule production. Concurrently, the concentration of the division protein FtsZ increased, and division rings formed closer together in filamentous cells. Depletion of the Rcs negative regulator IgaA also slowed growth and elevated FtsZ levels, indicating that IgaA is essential due to growth inhibition in its absence. In contrast, treatment with the Rcs-activating antibiotic A22 did not affect growth rate or FtsZ concentration and restored normal growth in cells in which the Rcs system was pre-induced with RcsFIM expression. Hence, the cellular response to A22 buffers the Rcs-dependent effect on cell growth. Together, our findings suggest that Rcs modulates cell division indirectly through growth-rate changes and that activation of the same pathway in different contexts can produce distinct physiological outcomes. IMPORTANCE Bacteria

recent_papers.title: Cellular behavior analysis from live-cell imaging of TCR T cell–cancer cell interactions
recent_papers.abstract: T cell therapies, such as chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells and T cell receptor (TCR) T cells, are a growing class of anti-cancer treatments. However, expansion to novel indications and beyond last-line treatment requires engineering cells’ dynamic population behaviors. Here we develop the tools for cellular behavior analysis of T cells from live-cell imaging, a common and inexpensive experimental setup used to evaluate engineered T cells. We first develop a state-of-the-art segmentation and tracking pipeline, Caliban, based on human-in-the-loop deep learning. We then build the Occident pipeline to collect a catalog of phenotypes that characterize cell populations, morphology, movement, and interactions in co-cultures of modified T cells and antigen-presenting tumor cells. We use Caliban and Occident to interrogate how interactions between T cells and cancer cells differ when beneficial knock-outs of RASA2 and CUL5 are introduced into TCR T cells. We apply spatiotemporal models to quantify T cell recruitment and proliferation after interactions with cancer cells. We discover that, compared to a safe harbor knockout control, RASA2 knockout T cells have longer interaction times with cancer cells leading to greater T cell activation and killing efficacy, while CUL5 knockout T cells have increased proliferation rates leading to greater numbers of T cells for hunting. Together, segmentation and tracking from Caliban and phenotype quantification from Occident enable cellular

recent_papers.title: Generalized cell phenotyping for spatial proteomics with language-informed vision models
recent_papers.abstract: We present DeepCell Types, a novel approach to cell phenotyping for spatial proteomics that addresses the challenge of generalization across diverse datasets with varying marker panels collected across different platforms. Our approach utilizes a transformer with channel-wise attention to create a language-informed vision model; this model’s semantic understanding of the underlying marker panel enables it to learn from and adapt to heterogeneous datasets. Leveraging a curated, diverse dataset named Expanded TissueNet with cell type labels spanning the literature and the NIH Human BioMolecular Atlas Program (HuBMAP) consortium, our model demonstrates robust performance across various cell types, tissues, and imaging modalities. Comprehensive benchmarking shows superior accuracy and generalizability of our method compared to existing methods. This work significantly advances automated spatial proteomics analysis, offering a generalizable and scalable solution for cell phenotyping that meets the demands of multiplexed imaging data.

recent_papers.title: Automated classification of cellular expression in multiplexed imaging data with Nimbus
recent_papers.abstract: Multiplexed imaging offers a powerful approach to characterize the spatial topography of tissues in both health and disease. To analyze such data, the specific combination of markers that are present in each cell must be enumerated to enable accurate phenotyping, a process that often relies on unsupervised clustering. We constructed the Pan-Multiplex (Pan-M) dataset containing 197 million distinct annotations of marker expression across 15 different cell types. We used Pan-M to create Nimbus, a deep learning model to predict marker positivity from multiplexed image data. Nimbus is a pre-trained model that uses the underlying images to classify marker expression across distinct cell types, from different tissues, acquired using different microscope platforms, without requiring any retraining. We demonstrate that Nimbus predictions capture the underlying staining patterns of the full diversity of markers present in Pan-M. We then show how Nimbus predictions can be integrated with downstream clustering algorithms to robustly identify cell subtypes in image data. We have open-sourced Nimbus and Pan-M to enable community use at https://github.com/angelolab/Nimbus-Inference.

recent_papers.title: Accurate single-molecule spot detection for image-based spatial transcriptomics with weakly supervised deep learning.
recent_papers.abstract: Image-based spatial transcriptomics methods enable transcriptome-scale gene expression measurements with spatial information but require complex, manually tuned analysis pipelines. We present Polaris, an analysis pipeline for image-based spatial transcriptomics that combines deep-learning models for cell segmentation and spot detection with a probabilistic gene decoder to quantify single-cell gene expression accurately. Polaris offers a unifying, turnkey solution for analyzing spatial transcriptomics data from multiplexed error-robust FISH (MERFISH), sequential fluorescence in situ hybridization (seqFISH), or in situ RNA sequencing (ISS) experiments. Polaris is available through the DeepCell software library (https://github.com/vanvalenlab/deepcell-spots) and https://www.deepcell.org.

Protein kinases relay information to various cellular processes, and their dysregulation underlies numerous human diseases. Despite their importance, our understanding of how kinase domains and their variants impact protein stability, catalytic activity, and substrate recognition is incomplete. In this work, we develop the phosphorylation protein complementation assay (phospho-PCA), which enables quantitative measurements of kinase-substrate interactions by coupling them to the growth of bud-ding yeast, thereby enabling deep mutational scanning of kinase domains. When combined with deep mutational scans targeting folding stability and Bayesian modeling, phospho-PCA can disentangle the relative impact of mutations on kinase domain stability, catalytic activity, and substrate specificity. We demonstrate the accuracy and breadth of phospho-PCA, showing its applicability to both tyrosine and serine/threonine kinase domains. We then apply our method to three closely related protein kinases with distinct substrate preferences, evaluating over 15,000 kinase variants against a panel of three substrates for both catalytic activity and substrate specificity. The resulting dataset constitutes the largest and most detailed variant-to-function map assembled for this enzyme family to date, revealing numerous mutations that alter kinase activity and substrate specificity. Physics-based modeling reveals how these mutations operate through diverse mechanisms, including long-range allosteric c

Protein structure tokenizers enable the creation of multimodal models of protein structure, sequence, and function. Current approaches to protein structure tok-enization rely on bespoke components that are invariant to spatial symmetries, but that are challenging to optimize and scale. We present Kanzi, a flow-based tokenizer for tokenization and generation of protein structures. Kanzi consists of a diffusion autoencoder trained with a flow matching loss. We show that this approach simplifies several aspects of protein structure tokenizers: frame-based representations can be replaced with global coordinates, complex losses are replaced with a single flow matching loss, and SE(3)-invariant attention operations can be replaced with standard attention. We find that these changes stabilize the training of parameter-efficient models that outperform existing to- kenizers on reconstruction metrics at a fraction of the model size and training cost. An autoregressive model trained with Kanzi outperforms similar generative models that operate over tokens, although it does not yet match the performance of state-of-the-art continuous diffusion models. Code is available here: https://github.com/rdilip/kanzi/.

ABSTRACT Cells mount elaborate responses to environmental stresses across multiple scales. In enterobacteria, the Rcs phosphorelay is activated by cell-envelope damage and by alterations to periplasmic thickness and cell width. To explore Rcs activation in the absence of external stresses, we investigated the physiological and morphological consequences of Rcs activation in Escherichia coli. We used an inducible variant of the lipoprotein RcsF that mislocalizes to the inner membrane (RcsFIM) and constitutively activates the Rcs system. RcsFIM expression slowed growth and reduced the cell length added per cell cycle, independent of Rcs-regulated capsule production. Concurrently, the concentration of the division protein FtsZ increased, and division rings formed closer together in filamentous cells. Depletion of the Rcs negative regulator IgaA also slowed growth and elevated FtsZ levels, indicating that IgaA is essential due to growth inhibition in its absence. In contrast, treatment with the Rcs-activating antibiotic A22 did not affect growth rate or FtsZ concentration and restored normal growth in cells in which the Rcs system was pre-induced with RcsFIM expression. Hence, the cellular response to A22 buffers the Rcs-dependent effect on cell growth. Together, our findings suggest that Rcs modulates cell division indirectly through growth-rate changes and that activation of the same pathway in different contexts can produce distinct physiological outcomes. IMPORTANCE Bacteria

T cell therapies, such as chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells and T cell receptor (TCR) T cells, are a growing class of anti-cancer treatments. However, expansion to novel indications and beyond last-line treatment requires engineering cells’ dynamic population behaviors. Here we develop the tools for cellular behavior analysis of T cells from live-cell imaging, a common and inexpensive experimental setup used to evaluate engineered T cells. We first develop a state-of-the-art segmentation and tracking pipeline, Caliban, based on human-in-the-loop deep learning. We then build the Occident pipeline to collect a catalog of phenotypes that characterize cell populations, morphology, movement, and interactions in co-cultures of modified T cells and antigen-presenting tumor cells. We use Caliban and Occident to interrogate how interactions between T cells and cancer cells differ when beneficial knock-outs of RASA2 and CUL5 are introduced into TCR T cells. We apply spatiotemporal models to quantify T cell recruitment and proliferation after interactions with cancer cells. We discover that, compared to a safe harbor knockout control, RASA2 knockout T cells have longer interaction times with cancer cells leading to greater T cell activation and killing efficacy, while CUL5 knockout T cells have increased proliferation rates leading to greater numbers of T cells for hunting. Together, segmentation and tracking from Caliban and phenotype quantification from Occident enable cellular

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My lab's driving question is how living systems and their viruses process information to understand their internal state and the world around them. To tackle this, we integrate imaging, machine learning, and genomics — building tools like CellSAM, a foundation model for cell segmentation across virtually any imaging domain, and Nimbus, which automates cellular phenotyping in multiplexed imaging data. I believe that transformative biological insight increasingly comes from building scalable, generalizable computational infrastructure rather than one-off specialist solutions.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are genuinely excited about sitting at the intersection of quantitative methods and cell biology — people comfortable picking up machine learning, microscopy, or genomics as the problem demands. Because our work spans protein tokenization (Kanzi), cell segmentation foundation models (CellSAM), and functional assays like phospho-PCA, intellectual versatility and a willingness to learn unfamiliar techniques matter more to me than a single narrow specialty. Curiosity, rigor, and the drive to build things that other scientists will actually use are the traits I value most.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week blends wet-lab or computational work with regular check-ins — students might be training or evaluating a deep learning model one day and discussing biological results from a bacterial morphology experiment the next, since projects like our Rcs phosphorelay study combine quantitative imaging with microbiology. We hold lab meetings where both works-in-progress and new literature get scrutinized, and I expect students to be active contributors to those discussions. Collaboration is built into the culture, so you'll also spend time interfacing with teammates whose expertise complements yours.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to give students enough autonomy to develop their own scientific voice while staying closely engaged, especially early in their training when framing the right question is the hardest part. Recognition like the NIH Director's New Innovator Award and the HHMI Freeman Hrabowski Scholar program reflects commitments I've made to supporting trainees from diverse backgrounds, and I take that responsibility seriously. My goal is for every student to leave the lab not just with a set of results but with the independent judgment to lead their own research program.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: The lab is fortunate to have active support from multiple sources, including the NIH Director's New Innovator Award, the NIH HuBMAP program, the Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation, the Rita Allen Foundation, the Rothenberg Innovation Initiative at Caltech, and the Susan E. Riley Foundation. This diversity of funding lets us pursue high-risk, high-reward directions — like developing protein structure tokenizers or broad cell segmentation foundation models — without being locked into a single narrow deliverable. I am actively working to maintain and grow this portfolio to keep students well-supported throughout their PhDs.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read our recent papers — CellSAM, Nimbus, and Kanzi — and ask yourself which problems in that space genuinely excite you, because the best conversations I have with prospective students start from a specific scientific question rather than a general interest in 'AI and biology.' Be honest in your application about where your current skills sit and what you want to learn; I care far more about trajectory and intellectual hunger than a perfectly polished CV. Reach out with a concrete question or observation about our work — it's the clearest signal that you've engaged seriously with what we do.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab genuinely runs on collaboration — I'll be deep in PyTorch one afternoon and then troubleshooting a bacterial imaging protocol with a labmate the next morning, which keeps things intellectually alive but can also feel scattered if you're not good at context-switching. There's a real shared pride in building tools that outside labs actually adopt, like when we see groups using CellSAM on data we never anticipated.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have weekly lab meetings that rotate between paper discussions and works-in-progress, and Dave participates actively rather than just showing up — he'll push back on your framing or suggest a connection to something like the Nimbus phenotyping pipeline that you hadn't considered. One-on-ones happen but aren't always at a fixed cadence, so you have to be proactive about booking time when you need it.
student_survey_responses.independence: Dave gives you real latitude to pursue directions that interest you, which I genuinely appreciate now that I'm further along, but the first year felt a little unmoored because the lab spans so many modalities — segmentation models, genomics, microbiology — and figuring out where to plant your flag takes longer than I expected. Once you've identified your niche he becomes a very engaged thought partner, especially around how to frame the scientific question rather than just the technical execution.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known going in that 'comfortable picking up machine learning or microscopy as needed' really means you should expect to become functional in both, not choose one — the projects genuinely demand it, and if you resist the wet-lab or the ML side you'll hit walls. Also, Caltech is small and Pasadena is not a major city, so factor in the lifestyle honestly before committing.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who get energized by infrastructure-level thinking — the kind of person who finds it satisfying to build something robust enough that strangers can use it — fit this lab really well, and so do people who are comfortable with ambiguity in the early stages of a project.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need very tight daily structure or a single well-defined thesis project handed to you from day one, the breadth of the lab's directions can feel overwhelming rather than exciting. Students who are narrowly specialized and reluctant to engage outside their lane also tend to feel out of place given how cross-disciplinary the day-to-day collaboration is.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small enough lab that everyone knows what everyone else is working on, which creates genuine intellectual cross-pollination — conversations about protein tokenization (Kanzi) have actually shaped how I think about my own cell segmentation work, which surprised me. The flip side is that there's not much place to hide if you're not making progress; it's a transparent environment, for better or worse.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Lab meetings are weekly and Dave takes them seriously — he reads things in advance and arrives with specific questions, which sets a tone that makes people prepare rather than wing it. Individual check-ins are less formal; I'd say most students aim for biweekly with Dave, but he's accessible over Slack for quick questions more often than I expected from a PI at his career stage.
student_survey_responses.independence: The autonomy is high even by PhD standards, which I've come to value, though early on I occasionally wished for more explicit milestones — you have to be self-directing enough to impose structure on yourself. Dave's mentorship strength is really in helping you figure out whether you're asking the right question, not in telling you what experiment to run next.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The funding situation is genuinely diverse and stable, which matters more than I realized as a prospective student — seeing the Moore Foundation, NIH HuBMAP, and the New Innovator Award all active at once means you're not watching Dave scramble every grant cycle, and that changes the lab's emotional climate in subtle but real ways. What I underestimated is how much time goes into making tools usable by the broader community; if you want to publish a result and move on, the 'build things other scientists will actually use' ethos means you'll spend more time on robustness and documentation than a more traditional computational biology lab might.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Intellectually restless people who genuinely like learning a new technique when the problem calls for it will find this place stimulating — I've picked up skills in multiplexed imaging analysis I never planned to acquire, and they've turned out to be central to my thesis. People who are motivated by real-world impact of their tools, not just publications, also seem to find the lab's mission energizing.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If your goal is very deep, narrow expertise in a single method — say, purely theoretical ML — this probably isn't the right environment, because the biological grounding is always expected. Students who struggle with ambiguity or who need frequent reassurance about their project direction also tend to find the independence more stressful than freeing.

recruiting_status.rationale: active lab signals present (funding or publications) but no explicit recruitment statement

name: Mikhail Shapiro

recent_papers.title: Population coding of predator imminence in the hypothalamus.
recent_papers.abstract: Hypothalamic VMHdmSF1 neurons are activated by predator cues and are necessary and sufficient for instinctive defensive responses. However, such data do not distinguish which features of a predator encounter are encoded by VMHdmSF1 neural activity. To address this issue, we imaged VMHdmSF1 neurons at single-cell resolution in freely behaving mice exposed to a natural predator in varying contexts. Our results reveal that VMHdmSF1 neurons do not encode different defensive behaviors but rather represent predator identity and multiple predator-evoked internal states, including threat-evoked fear/anxiety, arousal or neophobia, predator imminence, and safety. Notably, threat and safety are encoded bi-directionally by anti-correlated subpopulations. Strikingly, individual differences in predator defensiveness are correlated with individual differences in VMHdmSF1 response dynamics. Thus, different threat-related internal state variables are encoded by distinct neuronal subpopulations within a genetically defined, anatomically restricted hypothalamic cell class.

recent_papers.title: Imaging-guided bioresorbable acoustic hydrogel microrobots
recent_papers.abstract: Micro- and nanorobots excel in navigating the intricate and often inaccessible areas of the human body, offering immense potential for applications such as disease diagnosis, precision drug delivery, detoxification, and minimally invasive surgery. Despite their promise, practical deployment faces hurdles, including achieving stable propulsion in complex in vivo biological environments, real-time imaging and localization through deep tissue, and precise remote control for targeted therapy and ensuring high therapeutic efficacy. To overcome these obstacles, we introduce a hydrogel-based, imaging-guided, bioresorbable acoustic microrobot (BAM) designed to navigate the human body with high stability. Constructed using two-photon polymerization, a BAM comprises magnetic nanoparticles and therapeutic agents integrated into its hydrogel matrix for precision control and drug delivery. The microrobot features an optimized surface chemistry with a hydrophobic inner layer to substantially enhance microbubble retention in biofluids with multiday functionality and a hydrophilic outer layer to minimize aggregation and promote timely degradation. The dual-opening bubble-trapping cavity design enables a BAM to maintain consistent and efficient acoustic propulsion across a range of biological fluids. Under focused ultrasound stimulation, the entrapped microbubbles oscillate and enhance the contrast for real-time ultrasound imaging, facilitating precise tracking and control of BAM movement thr

recent_papers.title: Pressure estimation of ultra-high frequency ultrasound using gas vesicles.
recent_papers.venue: Journal of the Acoustical Society of America
recent_papers.abstract: Acoustic microscopy uses ultra-high frequency (UHF) ultrasound transducers over 80 MHz to perform high-resolution imaging. The pressure output of these transducers is unknown, as commercial calibrated hydrophones can measure pressure for transducers with frequencies only up to 80 MHz. This study used gas vesicle nanostructures (GVs) that collapse at 571 kPa to estimate the pressure of UHF transducers at 40, 80, 200, and 375 MHz. Agarose phantoms containing GVs were made, and a baseline ultrasound image was performed at low pressure to prevent GV collapse. Sections within the phantom were scanned at varying voltage to determine the GV collapse threshold. The pressure at full driving voltage was then calculated, assuming a linear relation between transducer voltage and pressure. The pressure calculated for the 40 MHz transducer was 2.2 ± 0.1 MPa at 21 °C. Using a hydrophone, the measured pressure was 2.1 ± 0.3 MPa, a difference of <2%, validating the method at this frequency. The pressure calculated for the other transducers was 2.0 ± 0.1 MPa (80 MHz), 1.2 ± 0.1 (200 MHz), and 1.05 ± 0.17 (375 MHz at 37 °C). This study addresses the challenge of estimating pressure output from UHF ultrasound transducers, demonstrating that the pressure output in the 40-400 MHz frequency range can be quantified.

recent_papers.title: Harmonic imaging for nonlinear detection of acoustic biomolecules
recent_papers.abstract: Gas vesicles (GVs) based on acoustic reporter genes have emerged as potent contrast agents for cellular and molecular ultrasound imaging. These air-filled, genetically encoded protein nanostructures can be expressed in a variety of cell types in vivo to visualize cell location and activity or injected systemically to label and monitor tissue function. Distinguishing GV signal from tissue deep inside intact organisms requires imaging approaches such as amplitude modulation (AM) or collapse-based pulse sequences. However, these approaches have limitations either in sensitivity or require the destruction of GVs, restricting the imaging of dynamic cellular processes. To address these limitations, we developed harmonic imaging to enhance the sensitivity of nondestructive GV imaging. We hypothesized that harmonic imaging, integrated with AM, could significantly elevate GV detection sensitivity by leveraging the nonlinear acoustic response of GVs. We tested this hypothesis by imaging tissue-mimicking phantoms embedded with purified GVs, mammalian cells genetically modified to express GVs, and mice liver in vivo post-systemic infusion of GVs. Our findings reveal that harmonic cross-propagating wave AM (HxAM) imaging markedly surpasses traditional xAM in isolating GVs' nonlinear acoustic signature, demonstrating significant (p < 0.05) enhancements in imaging performance. HxAM imaging improves detection of GV producing cells up to three folds in vitro, enhances in vivo imaging performa

recent_papers.title: Multiplexed ultrasound imaging of gene expression
recent_papers.abstract: Acoustic reporter genes (ARGs) have enabled the imaging of gene expression with ultrasound, which provides high-resolution access to deep, optically opaque living tissues. However, unlike their fluorescent counterparts, ARGs have so far been limited to a single “color,” preventing multiplexed imaging of cellular states or populations. Here, we use rational protein design and directed evolution to develop two novel ARGs that can be distinguished from each other based on their acoustic pressure response profiles, enabling “two-color” ultrasound imaging of gene expression. We demonstrate the utility of multiplexed ARGs for delineating bacterial cell species and cell states in vitro, and then apply them towards imaging distinct subpopulations of probiotics in the mouse gastrointestinal tract and in tumor-colonizing bacterial agents in vivo. Just as the first wavelength-shifted derivatives of fluorescent proteins opened a vivid world for optical microscopy, our next-generation acoustic proteins set the stage for a richer symphony of ultrasound signals from living subjects.

recent_papers.title: Directed Evolution of Acoustic Reporter Genes Using High-Throughput Acoustic Screening
recent_papers.abstract: A major challenge in the fields of biological imaging and synthetic biology is noninvasively visualizing the functions of natural and engineered cells inside opaque samples such as living animals. One promising technology that addresses this limitation is ultrasound (US), with its penetration depth of several cm and spatial resolution on the order of 100 μm. Within the past decade, reporter genes for US have been introduced and engineered to link cellular functions to US signals via heterologous expression in commensal bacteria and mammalian cells. These acoustic reporter genes (ARGs) represent a novel class of genetically encoded US contrast agent, and are based on air-filled protein nanostructures called gas vesicles (GVs). Just as the discovery of fluorescent proteins was followed by the improvement and diversification of their optical properties through directed evolution, here we describe the evolution of GVs as acoustic reporters. To accomplish this task, we establish high-throughput, semiautomated acoustic screening of ARGs in bacterial cultures and use it to screen mutant libraries for variants with increased nonlinear US scattering. Starting with scanning site saturation libraries for two homologues of the primary GV structural protein, GvpA/B, two rounds of evolution resulted in GV variants with 5- and 14-fold stronger acoustic signals than the parent proteins. We anticipate that this and similar approaches will help high-throughput protein engineering play as large

recent_papers.title: SEMPER: Stoichiometric expression of mRNA polycistrons by eukaryotic ribosomes for compact, ratio-tunable multi-gene expression.
recent_papers.abstract: Here, we present a method for expressing multiple open reading frames (ORFs) from single transcripts using the leaky scanning model of translation initiation. In this approach termed "stoichiometric expression of mRNA polycistrons by eukaryotic ribosomes" (SEMPER), adjacent ORFs are translated from a single mRNA at tunable ratios determined by their order in the sequence and the strength of their translation initiation sites. We validate this approach by expressing up to three fluorescent proteins from one plasmid in two different cell lines. We then use it to encode a stoichiometrically tuned polycistronic construct encoding gas vesicle acoustic reporter genes that enables efficient formation of the multi-protein complex while minimizing cellular toxicity. We also demonstrate that SEMPER enables polycistronic expression of recombinant monoclonal antibodies from plasmid DNA and of two fluorescent proteins from single mRNAs made through in vitro transcription. Finally, we provide a probabilistic model to elucidate the mechanisms underlying SEMPER. A record of this paper's transparent peer review process is included in the supplemental information.

recent_papers.title: An implantable piezoelectric ultrasound stimulator (ImPULS) for deep brain activation
recent_papers.abstract: Precise neurostimulation can revolutionize therapies for neurological disorders. Electrode-based stimulation devices face challenges in achieving precise and consistent targeting due to the immune response and the limited penetration of electrical fields. Ultrasound can aid in energy propagation, but transcranial ultrasound stimulation in the deep brain has limited spatial resolution caused by bone and tissue scattering. Here, we report an implantable piezoelectric ultrasound stimulator (ImPULS) that generates an ultrasonic focal pressure of 100 kPa to modulate the activity of neurons. ImPULS is a fully-encapsulated, flexible piezoelectric micromachined ultrasound transducer that incorporates a biocompatible piezoceramic, potassium sodium niobate [(K,Na)NbO3]. The absence of electrochemically active elements poses a new strategy for achieving long-term stability. We demonstrated that ImPULS can i) excite neurons in a mouse hippocampal slice ex vivo, ii) activate cells in the hippocampus of an anesthetized mouse to induce expression of activity-dependent gene c-Fos, and iii) stimulate dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta to elicit time-locked modulation of nigrostriatal dopamine release. This work introduces a non-genetic ultrasound platform for spatially-localized neural stimulation and exploration of basic functions in the deep brain. Ultrasound neuromodulation overcomes limitations of electrode-based stimulation through improved targeting and long-term

To address this issue, we imaged VMHdmSF1 neurons at single-cell resolution in freely behaving mice exposed to a natural predator in varying contexts. Our results reveal that VMHdmSF1 neurons do not encode different defensive behaviors but rather represent predator identity and multiple predator-evoked internal states, including threat-evoked fear/anxiety, arousal or neophobia, predator imminence, and safety. funding_sources.ic: National Institute of Biomedical Imaging and Bioengineering
funding_sources.title: Biogenic Gas Nanostructures as Molecular Imaging Reporters for Ultrasound
funding_sources.abstract:
SUMMARY
Ultrasound is among the world’s most widely used biomedical imaging technologies due to its low cost and ability
to visualize deep tissues with high spatial and temporal resolution. However, ultrasound has had a relatively
small role in molecular and cellular imaging due to a lack of contrast agents and reporter genes connected to
specific aspects of cellular function such as gene expression and intracellular signaling. To address this
limitation, we are developing acoustic biomolecules – proteins that can be imaged with ultrasound. These
constructs are based on gas vesicles (GVs) – a unique class of air-filled protein nanostructures from buoyant
photosynthetic microbes, which we introduced as imaging agents for ultrasound in 2014 (Nature Nano. 9:311)
and as acoustic reporter genes for commensal microbes in 2018 (Nature 554:86). Since our last renewal, we
took the next major step of demonstrating that GVs can function as reporter genes in mammalian cells (Science
365:1469, 2019 and Nature Biotech. 2023), learned how to turn GVs into dynamic biosensors of intracellular
protease activity (Nature Chem. Biol. 16:988, 2020), developed methods to detect GV-expressing cells down to
single-cell sensitivity (Nature Methods 18:945, 2021), demonstrated their utility as injectable contrast agents in
a disease context (ACS Nano 14:12210, 2020), and made several other advances in understanding and
engineering GVs and accompanying ultrasound methods and applications. Much work remai

funding_sources.ic: National Institute of Biomedical Imaging and Bioengineering
funding_sources.title: Sonogenetic Remote Control of Cellular Function
funding_sources.abstract:
SUMMARY
The discovery and development of fluorescent proteins and optogenetics revolutionized biology by making it
possible to image and control specific cellular processes with visible light. While these tools have enabled
countless biological discoveries, the poor penetration of light into living tissue makes it difficult to use optical
techniques in intact animals. As a result, biological phenomena ranging from the signaling of neurons in deep-
brain regions, to the infiltration of immune cells into tumors, to the microbial colonization of the GI tract, are
challenging to study within their natural in vivo context. If instead of light it were possible to visualize and
manipulate cellular function using a more penetrant form of energy such as ultrasound, this would open
previously inaccessible domains of in vivo biology to direct investigation. In addition, it would enhance the
development of cell-based therapies by allowing cellular agents to be seen and controlled after administration
into the human body. The physics of ultrasound make it an ideal modality for deep-tissue cellular communication.
Sound waves in the MHz range are weakly scattered by tissue and can therefore penetrate several cm into the
body. With wavelengths on the order of 100 µm and travel times < 1 ms, ultrasound can access many key
structures and processes. When focused, sound waves can deliver mechanical and thermal energy to precise

funding_sources.ic: National Institute of Biomedical Imaging and Bioengineering
funding_sources.title: Sonogenetic Remote Control of Cellular Function
funding_sources.abstract:
SUMMARY
The discovery and development of fluorescent proteins and optogenetics revolutionized biology by making it
possible to image and control specific cellular processes with visible light. While these tools have enabled
countless biological discoveries, the poor penetration of light into living tissue makes it difficult to use optical
techniques in intact animals. As a result, biological phenomena ranging from the signaling of neurons in deep-
brain regions, to the infiltration of immune cells into tumors, to the microbial colonization of the GI tract, are
challenging to study within their natural in vivo context. If instead of light it were possible to visualize and
manipulate cellular function using a more penetrant form of energy such as ultrasound, this would open
previously inaccessible domains of in vivo biology to direct investigation. In addition, it would enhance the
development of cell-based therapies by allowing cellular agents to be seen and controlled after administration
into the human body. The physics of ultrasound make it an ideal modality for deep-tissue cellular communication.
Sound waves in the MHz range are weakly scattered by tissue and can therefore penetrate several cm into the
body. With wavelengths on the order of 100 µm and travel times < 1 ms, ultrasound can access many key
structures and processes. When focused, sound waves can deliver mechanical and thermal energy to precise
anatomical locations. These properties have already made ultrasound

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My lab is driven by a simple but ambitious idea: that we can develop technologies to image and control the function of cells deep inside the living body by harnessing biomolecules with unusual physical properties. Gas vesicles, for example, are air-filled protein nanostructures that interact with sound waves in remarkable ways — we've shown they can serve as genetically encoded contrast agents for ultrasound imaging and, as described in our work on acoustic biomolecular actuators, as 'acoustic antennae' that let us selectively push, trap, and sort specific cells using ultrasound gradients. We apply these tools across synthetic biology, neuroscience, cancer, immunology, and the microbiome, always asking how deep-tissue biology can be made visible and controllable.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for people who are genuinely excited about working at the intersection of multiple disciplines — someone comfortable thinking about biophysics one day and molecular engineering the next. The breadth of our projects, from imaging hypothalamic VMHdmSF1 neurons during predator encounters to developing implantable piezoelectric ultrasound stimulators (ImPULS) for deep brain activation, means that intellectual flexibility and curiosity matter as much as any single technical skill. A strong quantitative or engineering background is often helpful, but what I care most about is a candidate's drive to ask and answer questions that don't fit neatly into one field.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in the lab involves a mix of hands-on experiments and collaborative discussion — you might spend days building and testing a new gas vesicle construct using a tool like SEMPER for stoichiometric multi-gene expression, and then gather with the broader team to analyze imaging data or troubleshoot an ultrasound stimulation protocol. We have regular one-on-one meetings to keep projects moving, and students are expected to engage with literature across acoustics, molecular biology, and neuroscience. There's also real opportunity to collaborate with groups outside Caltech, as our functional ultrasound brain imaging work with partners at USC and in Paris illustrates.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to give students enough independence to develop their own scientific voice while staying closely engaged, especially in the early years when research instincts are still forming. I think of mentorship as an evolving relationship — early on I'm more hands-on in shaping experimental design and interpretation, but as students mature I step back and let them drive their own directions. I'm genuinely invested in helping people build careers, whether that's in academia, industry, or elsewhere, and I try to make sure students get visibility through publications and presentations.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: While I'm not able to point to specific active grants at the moment, our lab's research programs — spanning ultrasound bioacoustics, biomagnetism, and neural circuit control — have historically attracted support because they address fundamental unmet needs in biomedical imaging and neuromodulation. Our published advances, including the acoustic cranial window for human brain imaging and the ImPULS deep brain stimulator, demonstrate the translational relevance of what we do, which tends to resonate with both fundamental science and applied funding agencies. I work to maintain a portfolio of projects at different stages so that students are supported throughout their PhD.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read broadly across our published work before reaching out — papers like our harmonic imaging study for nonlinear gas vesicle detection and the bioorthogonal labeling approach for in situ GV fluorescence imaging give you a real sense of how we think about problems. Come with genuine scientific questions of your own, even if they're half-formed, because that tells me far more than a polished application statement alone. The students who thrive here are those who are excited by the idea that entirely new biology becomes accessible when you give cells the ability to interact with sound and magnetic fields.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab genuinely feels collaborative rather than competitive — people will drop what they're doing to help you troubleshoot a gas vesicle construct or debug an imaging protocol, and there's a real mix of biologists, engineers, and physicists who all think differently. That said, the breadth can be disorienting at first; your labmates' projects can feel almost unrelated to yours, which is energizing on good days and isolating on bad ones.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: One-on-ones with Mikhail happen weekly when things are moving, though they can slip to biweekly during busy grant or conference seasons — he's genuinely present in those meetings, not just checking boxes. There are also group meetings where you're expected to engage with work pretty far outside your immediate project, which I've come to appreciate even when it's a lot.
student_survey_responses.independence: In my first year he was very much in the weeds with me on experimental design, which I needed more than I expected. By year three I'm largely driving my own direction, but he's still reachable when I hit a conceptual wall — it's not the kind of independence where you're just left alone.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much of my time would be spent learning adjacent literatures from scratch — one week you're reading acoustics papers, the next you're deep in synthetic biology or neuroscience, and none of my coursework really prepared me for that pace of context-switching. It's genuinely exciting in retrospect, but the first semester felt like drinking from several firehoses simultaneously.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who are intrinsically motivated by open-ended questions and don't need a narrow, fully defined project from day one do really well here. If you're the type who gets excited reading a paper in a field you've never touched and immediately starts thinking about how it connects to your work, this place will feel electric.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a lot of external structure or a very clear experimental roadmap handed to you, you'll find the early years stressful — the project scope is wide enough that it falls on you to narrow it, and not everyone is ready for that when they arrive.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a high-energy environment where ambition is the baseline — people here genuinely believe they're building tools that didn't exist before, and that belief is contagious. The flip side is that the bar for what counts as 'good progress' is set pretty high, so you have to be comfortable with the feeling that there's always more to do.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Mikhail does weekly one-on-ones and takes them seriously — he reads what you send him beforehand and comes in with real questions, not just encouragement. Group meetings are substantive and cover everything from acoustic physics to immunology depending on who's presenting, so you genuinely have to stay curious across the whole lab.
student_survey_responses.independence: The independence ramps up in a way that feels deliberate rather than accidental — early on he'll push back on your experimental logic and make you defend your choices, which is uncomfortable but useful. By later years you're the one setting the agenda and he's more of a sounding board, which is what I wanted from a PhD but took some adjustment to actually handle.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I underestimated how much the collaboration with outside groups — USC, the Paris fUS team, others — would shape my timeline and require me to coordinate across institutions with different norms and schedules. It's great for your network and the science, but nobody tells you upfront that cross-institutional projects move at their own pace.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Quantitatively minded people who are also genuinely curious about biology — not just tolerant of it — tend to hit their stride here faster than anyone else. There's also a lot of room to push a project toward translation if that's your interest, which keeps things from feeling purely academic.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came in wanting to go deep on one very specific technique or model system and stay there, the lab's philosophy will feel at odds with your instincts — Mikhail really does value breadth, and projects tend to evolve in directions you didn't anticipate when you started.
