name: Long Cai

recent_papers.title: Birth Outcomes of Pregnant Women Infected with COVID-19 in Highland Areas of China from 2020 to 2022: A Retrospective Analysis
recent_papers.abstract: Purpose To explore the effect of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) infection on neonates in plateau regions. Methods Cases of newborns born to pregnant women infected with COVID-19 who received prenatal care or treatment at the Women and Children’s Hospital of the Tibet Autonomous Region and the Lhasa People’s Hospital between January 2020 and December 2022 (infected group) and newborns born to healthy pregnant women (non-infected group) who were included by age, underlying disease and length of hospital stay were retrospectively collected. According to the inclusion and exclusion criteria, 381 patients in the infected group and 314 patients in the non-infected group were included in the study. Results The results of multivariate analysis showed that admission to the neonatal intensive care unit (OR = 3.342, 95% CI = 1.564–6.764), shortness of breath (OR = 2.853, 95% CI = 1.789–3.154), irregular breathing (OR = 2.465, 95% CI = 1.879–4.112) and neonatal jaundice (OR = 2.324, 95% CI = 1.989–2.445) were the factors influencing the low Apgar scores of neonates in the infected group (all P < 0.05). Conclusion Neonates born to pregnant women infected with COVID-19 had lower Apgar scores and higher incidences of complications, such as shortness of breath, groaning, irregular breathing and neonatal jaundice, than newborns born to pregnant women not infected with COVID-19.

recent_papers.title: Effect of COVID-19 infection on pregnant women in plateau regions.
recent_papers.abstract:
OBJECTIVE
The present study aims to explore the effect of COVID-19 infection on pregnant women in plateau regions.


STUDY DESIGN
Data from 381 pregnant women infected with COVID-19 who underwent prenatal examination or treatment at Women and Children's Hospital of Tibet Autonomous Region between January 2020 and December 2022 and 314 pregnant women not infected with COVID-19 were retrospectively collected.


METHODS
The study participants were divided into an infected and non-infected group according to whether they were infected with COVID-19. Basic information (ethnicity, age, body mass index and gestational age [GA]), vaccination status, intensive care unit (ICU) admission and delivery outcomes were compared. Binary logistic regression was used to analyse the influencing factors of ICU admission.


RESULTS
The results revealed significant differences in the GA, vaccination rate, blood pressure, partial pressure of oxygen, white blood cell (WBC) count, ICU admission rate, preeclampsia rate, forearm presentation rate, thrombocytopenia rate, syphilis infection rate and placental abruption rate between the two groups (P < 0.05). A univariate analysis showed that COVID-19 infection, hepatitis B virus infection, the WBC count and hypoproteinaemia were risk factors for ICU admission. recent_papers.title: Prevalence of Pelvic Floor Disorders in Parous Women from the Tibet Autonomous Region: A Cross-Sectional Study
recent_papers.abstract: Purpose Pelvic floor disorder (PFD) seriously affects the everyday life of women. This cross-sectional study aimed to evaluate the prevalence and risk factors for postpartum PFD in women living in the Tibet Autonomous Region (TAR). Methods Parous women who attended the outpatient gynaecology clinic at our hospital between June 2022 and August 2022 were screened in this study. The demographic and clinical data of these women were collected. Their pelvic floor functions were evaluated via a pelvic organ prolapse (POP) quantification examination, the Pelvic Floor Distress Inventory Questionnaire-20 (PFDI-20) and the Overactive Bladder Symptom Score (OABSS). Results A total of 201 women were included in this study, of whom 81.09% (163/201) were Tibetan. Twenty-seven women (13.43%) were diagnosed with POP stage ≥2 and 27 women (13.43%) with an OABSS score ≥3. The median PFDI-20 total score was 4.17 (range 0–43.75). Han women (n = 38) in the TAR had much lower PFDI-20 total scores, compared with Tibetan women (n = 163) (p < 0.05). The results of the multiple linear regression models showed that the PFDI-20 scores obtained from women living in the TAR were closely related to parity, history of heavy weight lifting, age, history of instrumental deliveries, ethnicity and number of caesarean sections. Conclusion Pelvic floor disorder is common among parous women living in the TAR. Ethnicity, parity, history of heavy weight lifting, age, history of instrumental deliveries and number of

funding_sources.title: Understanding the role of tumor microenvironment in low grade glioma progression to malignancy
funding_sources.abstract: ABSTRACT. Our PCA research center brings together the UCLA Neurosurgery team with the Caltech spatial single cell team to construct a comparative spatial atlas of low-grade gliomas. The collaboration between the three components of the PCA research center has already generated preliminary data in several glioma samples. We will expand this effort to generate a comparative atlas of gliomas with distinct progression outcomes using integrated spatial transcriptomics, proteomics, and chromosome profiling. By comparing the low-grade gliomas that eventually transform with ones that stay indolent or do not recur, and with IDH-mutant high-grade gliomas, we aim to understand the molecular and cellular mechanisms at the low-grade stage that are predictive of malignant transformation (MT) and to suggest intervention strategies to prevent MT. The comparative analysis will examine three types of changes in low-grade gliomas with different outcomes: cell type composition, tumor microenvironment, and pathway specific gene expression. From UCLA’s Brain Tumor Translation Resource (BTTR) center, we have already collected 99 fresh-frozen low-grade glioma samples and will collect approximately an additional 100 samples of low-grade glioma with different outcomes (MT, indolent, and no-recurrence). We will then generate an integrated multi-modal spatial atlas targeting 2500 mRNAs, 10 proteins and 10 DNA CNVs and translocations. From the high sensitivity and multiplexed RNA seqFISH assays, we will

funding_sources.ic: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke
funding_sources.title: Single cell analysis of the kinome
funding_sources.abstract:
Project Summary
Profiling kinases and their activities in single cells will enable exploration of cell type specific
functional and biochemical pathways. While we have learned a great deal about transcriptional
states of different cell types in the past decade through single cell genomics, we still understand
little about proteins and in particular kinases networks in diverse cell populations. This is largely
because single-cell kinome analysis faces major technical challenges. Unlike nucleic acids,
proteins cannot be amplified, making detection of minute quantities from single cells difficult. We
recently demonstrated a proof-of-principle experiment to accurately detect protein PTM isoforms
in single cells. Here we propose to scale this method up to profile kinases at the global level in
single cells in both cell culture and in tissues. We will integrate the single cell kinome analysis
with RNA measurements to identify cell type specific kinase profiles. Ultimately, we will map
the kinome with spatial context in the mouse and human brain to examine the kinase pathways
involved in aging and neurodegeneration which could identify druggable targets. This project is a
major departure from our current research. Its goals are ambitious but achievable. The project
will leverage our expertise in single cell technology development and in application of new
technology to diverse biological systems.

pi_survey.research_philosophy: Biology is fundamentally defined by spatial organization across scales — from the meter-scale of the human body down to the nanometer-scale of individual molecules. In my lab at Caltech, we believe that to truly understand biology you have to observe molecules in their native spatial context, which is why we pioneered seqFISH, a technology that lets us simultaneously visualize tens of thousands of different molecules within a single tissue sample. We apply this single-cell spatial genomics framework to some of the most challenging problems in developmental biology, immunology, neuroscience, cancer biology, and microbiology.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for scientists with genuine curiosity and the drive to push into technically demanding, high-risk territory — the kind of work recognized by the NIH Director's Pioneer Award we received in 2022. Strong candidates typically bring a combination of quantitative or engineering skills and a deep interest in fundamental biological questions, since our work requires integrating fluorescence microscopy, DNA nanotechnology, custom fluidics, and advanced image processing. Intellectual range and the willingness to collaborate across disciplines, as we do with the Elowitz Lab on projects like MEMOIR, are qualities I value highly.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in the Cai Lab involves hands-on bench work developing or applying seqFISH — designing imaging experiments, running fluid-handling protocols, and processing large multiplexed image datasets. Students also attend lab meetings where we discuss both our own results and the broader spatial genomics literature, and they have regular one-on-one check-ins with me to align on experimental priorities. Because our projects span so many biological domains, students often spend time collaborating with specialists in neuroscience, immunology, or developmental biology both within Caltech and at partner institutions.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: My goal is to help each student develop into an independent scientist who can both conceive of and execute breakthrough technology-driven research. I try to give lab members enough creative latitude to pursue their own ideas while providing close guidance on the technical rigor that is essential when you are building novel imaging platforms like seqFISH or recording systems like MEMOIR. I see mentorship as an evolving relationship — early on I am more hands-on, but as students mature I step back and let them drive their own projects and collaborations.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: Although no specific active grants are listed in our current records, our lab received the prestigious NIH Director's Pioneer Award in 2022, which is part of the High-Risk, High-Reward Research Program and provides substantial support for our most ambitious directions in single-cell spatial genomics. We continue to pursue funding commensurate with the scope of our technology development and biological applications across developmental biology, neuroscience, immunology, and cancer biology.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Before applying, I encourage you to read deeply into our seqFISH papers and think carefully about which biological problems most excite you — we are most energized by candidates who arrive with specific questions they want to answer using spatial genomics tools, not just a general interest in imaging. Reach out with a concise message explaining how your background in quantitative methods, biology, or engineering connects to what we do, and be honest about both your strengths and the skills you are eager to develop. The best fit is someone who is energized rather than intimidated by the challenge of working at the frontier of a rapidly evolving field.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a genuinely collaborative atmosphere — people share reagents, debugging sessions, and image analysis scripts without too much territorial behavior, which I didn't expect given how competitive Caltech can feel. That said, everyone is moving fast, so you have to be proactive about asking for help rather than waiting for someone to notice you're stuck.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: One-on-ones with Long happen roughly weekly early in your PhD and maybe biweekly once you're more established — he's pretty accessible by Slack for quick questions in between. Lab meeting is once a week and is a real mix of internal progress talks and journal club on the latest spatial genomics papers, which keeps you honest about the broader field.
student_survey_responses.independence: By your second or third year you're largely designing your own experiments and chasing down your own biological question, which is exciting but can also feel untethered if you're not a self-starter. Long will push you toward a direction early on, but once the technical foundation is there he really does step back and let you run with it.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I had known how much of the work is image analysis and data wrangling — I came in thinking it would be mostly bench biology, and the coding load surprised me in the first year. Getting comfortable with Python and some image processing basics before rotating would have saved me months of catching up.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who are genuinely excited by both the technology and the biology — not one or the other — do really well here because the projects demand both fluency. If you find it fun to iterate on a fluidics protocol at midnight and then spend the next morning arguing about what the expression pattern means developmentally, this is your place.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who need a tight, pre-defined project handed to them at the start tend to feel lost, because the frontier-y nature of seqFISH means failure modes are real and timelines shift. It can also be hard if you're attached to a single biological model organism, since the collaborations pull you in many directions at once.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: There's a high-energy, startup-ish vibe — people are genuinely proud of seqFISH and talk about it with the kind of enthusiasm that makes you want to push harder on your own project. The flip side is that the bar for technical rigor is high, and if your imaging data looks sloppy it will be called out at lab meeting, kindly but clearly.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Long holds individual check-ins that are usually 30–45 minutes and focused mostly on experimental priorities and troubleshooting; he's not the type to do a lot of career-mentoring chat in those slots unless you steer the conversation there. Lab meetings run about 90 minutes and are substantive — expect to be asked hard questions about controls when you present.
student_survey_responses.independence: The independence ramp is real: first year you're essentially apprenticing on someone else's pipeline, and by third year you're expected to be proposing your own directions and reaching out to collaborators yourself. Some people find that transition exhilarating, others find it jarring, so it's worth knowing yourself before you join.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wished someone had told me how physically demanding the experiments can be — seqFISH runs are long, multi-day protocols and you're spending serious hours at the microscope or babysitting the fluidics rig. Planning personal time requires actual scheduling or it quietly disappears.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Quantitatively inclined biologists who have enough engineering curiosity to enjoy building and debugging custom instruments rather than just using them off the shelf will absolutely thrive. Collaborative people who don't mind pivoting their biological question when a new technology capability opens up a more interesting angle also fit the culture well.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came to grad school specifically to do classical genetics or pure computational work without wet-lab components, the seqFISH-centric workflow will feel like a poor fit — nearly everyone ends up touching the bench in some capacity. People who want frequent, structured feedback on their career development beyond the science itself may also find they have to advocate loudly for that kind of mentorship.

no lab text, funding, or publication data available

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a collaborative, low-ego vibe — people share code and paper drafts pretty openly, and there's no obvious hierarchy between senior and junior students. It can feel a little informal at times, which is great when you're stuck on something but occasionally means accountability slips.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We do a weekly group meeting where someone presents work-in-progress, and Erik usually does one-on-ones every other week unless a deadline is coming up. When crunch time hits, he's pretty available over Slack, which I appreciate.
student_survey_responses.independence: He gives you a lot of rope early on, which I think is intentional — you're expected to find your own footing with the literature before he starts shaping your direction. If you need hand-holding on reading papers or scoping a project, that adjustment period can be uncomfortable.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I had understood earlier that 'freedom to explore' here really means you have to proactively surface problems; Erik won't necessarily notice if you're quietly spinning your wheels for a few weeks. Building the habit of flagging blockers early made a huge difference for me.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who have genuine curiosity about applying machine learning to messy real-world domains — especially anything touching software engineering or educational data — tend to find a lot of natural traction with the kind of problems the lab works on. Self-starters who like setting their own weekly agenda do really well.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came in expecting a tightly scoped dissertation topic handed to you on day one, the open-endedness can feel destabilizing for longer than it should. People who need frequent structured feedback on their writing also sometimes find the review cycles slower than they'd like.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small group, so you end up knowing everyone's project pretty well, which is genuinely useful when your work touches multiple subfields — I've gotten some of my best methodology feedback from labmates who aren't even in my area. The downside is there's not a huge internal audience if your work gets niche.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Group meetings happen weekly and rotate who's presenting, so you might only be in the hot seat every month or so — which keeps the pressure manageable but also means you can coast if you're not careful. One-on-ones feel more ad hoc than scheduled, so you kind of have to advocate for your own face time.
student_survey_responses.independence: Erik seems to trust students to own their projects pretty fully once a direction is set, and he tends to engage most deeply at the 'big picture' level rather than line-editing your experiments. That's energizing if you're confident in your methods, but it does mean you need to build your own external networks for technical mentorship.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't realize how much of the work here sits at the intersection of CS and education or human factors, so if you're purely a deep-learning-for-its-own-sake person, some of the application constraints can feel limiting at first. Leaning into the domain questions earlier would have saved me a semester of mild confusion about what 'good' results even look like.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People with a background or genuine interest in software systems, human-computer interaction, or learning analytics find the lab's problem space naturally motivating rather than something they have to talk themselves into. Strong self-regulators who can turn ambiguous feedback into a concrete next experiment are the ones who seem to publish consistently.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a lot of emotional check-ins or explicit validation to stay motivated, the communication style here might leave you feeling a bit adrift — it's not that anyone is unkind, it's just that the mentorship leans practical rather than pastoral. Grant and funding timelines can also feel uncertain depending on where the lab's project cycle is, so it's worth asking about that directly before you commit.

recent_papers.title: Monitoring Koyna Dam Displacements Using Persistent Scatterer Interferometry
recent_papers.abstract: Monitoring dam stability is critical to ensure structural safety and operational reliability. This study integrates Persistent Scatterer Interferometry (PSI) based on Sentinel-1 SAR imagery (2020–2023) with Finite Element Method (FEM) simulations to assess the behavior of the Koyna Dam in India. PSI detected crest displacements between −1.0 and −1.8 mm yr−1, while FEM simulations predicted a maximum vertical displacement of approximately −3.2 mm at the crest. Although these results represent different quantities (time-averaged displacement rates versus peak static displacement), both approaches indicate millimeter-scale deformation and a consistent pattern of settlement at the dam crest, supporting the interpretation of hydrologically driven structural response. The observed differences are primarily attributed to differences in spatial resolution and methodology between point-based FEM outputs and pixel-averaged satellite observations. The study demonstrates that combining satellite-based monitoring with numerical simulations provides a robust and cost-effective framework for dam safety assessment. This integrated approach supports improved interpretation of deformation behavior and offers practical value in extreme conditions, such as during flood events or climate-driven hydrological changes. recent_papers.title: Assessing Meteorological Impacts on Hydrological Switches in the CONUS
recent_papers.venue: IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium
recent_papers.abstract: Hydrological switches, defined as rapid transitions between extreme meteorological events such as droughts and floods, are becoming increasingly frequent across the contiguous United States (CONUS) due to climate variability. This study analyzes the spatial and temporal patterns of these hydrological switches and their correlation with large-scale meteorological indices, such as the Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI) and Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI). Streamflow data from the US Geological Survey (USGS) is analyzed to investigate the impact of meteorological drivers on hydrological variability. Results indicate that regions dependent on snowmelt exhibit delayed hydrological responses to climatic conditions, while areas in the Eastern and Southern U.S. respond quickly to precipitation events. These findings underscore the need for region-specific water management strategies to mitigate the risks posed by increasing hydroclimatic extremes.

recent_papers.title: Wildfires Classification in Canadian Boreal Forest: A Comparative Study of Logistic Regression and XGBoost Models
recent_papers.venue: IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium
recent_papers.abstract: In recent years, Canada has faced a growing number of wildfires. These events have devastated ecosystems, displaced communities, and posed severe health risks. To minimize the damage caused by such disasters, this study aims to develop an early warning system that predicts wildfire occurrences. Two machine learning models for binary classification of wildfire occurrence in Canadian wild forests, Logistic regression and XGBoost, will be compared and evaluated. The models are used to predict the likelihood of wildfire events based on various environmental and climatic factors. The models are evaluated using a 70-30 split validation approach and their performance is assessed through confusion matrices. Our research contributes to the understanding and prediction of wildfire events.

recent_papers.title: Multi-Crop Systems and Crop-Switching Strategies to Enhance Water Use Efficiency and Climate Resilience in Arid Agricultural Regions
recent_papers.venue: IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium
recent_papers.abstract: Agriculture in the Lower Colorado River (LCR) region faces mounting challenges from climate change, arid conditions, and water scarcity. This study evaluates water use efficiency (WUEc) and crop-switching strategies under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 scenarios for 2025–2049, 2050–2074, and 2075–2099. Using historical data, climatic drivers such as temperature and precipitation were analyzed for their influence on key crops, including durum wheat, winter wheat, and corn. Results show SSP2-4.5 supports water use reductions up to 16%, stable profits (80–90%), and modest calorie increases (up to 15%), while SSP5-8.5 poses severe challenges, with water use reductions of 2–5%, profits dropping to around 20%, and minimal calorie gains (2%). The study highlights the vulnerability of durum wheat and resilience of winter wheat and corn, advocating for crop-switching and resource-efficient practices to sustain agriculture in arid regions like the LCR.

recent_papers.title: Decoding Teleconnection Impacts on Hydrological Switches in the Conus Using Wavelet Analysis
recent_papers.venue: IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium
recent_papers.abstract: Hydrometeorological teleconnections are key drivers of hydrological processes, representing the influence of large-scale atmospheric circulation patterns on regional climates. Understanding these teleconnections provides crucial insights into the mechanisms underlying hydrometeorological phenomena, particularly hydrological switches—rapid transitions between extreme events such as droughts and floods. These switches have become increasingly prevalent across the contiguous United States (CONUS), fueled by climate variability and evolving atmospheric patterns.This study utilizes cross-wavelet transform analysis to examine the spatial and temporal dynamics of hydrological switches and their correlations with major teleconnection indices, including NAO, ONI, WP, PDO, PNA, and QBO. The findings indicate significant coherence between hydrological switches and teleconnection indices across different temporal scales, with ONI and QBO showing dominant correlations at frequencies of 16–32 months across all regions from 1951 to 2023. Phase analysis reveals a mix of synchronized and lagged impacts, highlighting the intricate interactions between teleconnections and hydrological extremes. Additionally, other indices such as NAO, WP, PDO, and PNA exhibit region-specific correlations, with values peaking at frequencies of 64–256 months during notable periods, underscoring the complex temporal and spatial variability of these influences.

recent_papers.title: Insights from Swot Data on Transboundary Upstream-Downstream Impacts in The Nile Basin
recent_papers.venue: IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium
recent_papers.abstract: This study utilizes high-resolution data from NASA's Surface Water and Ocean Topography (SWOT) mission to investigate water dynamics and upstream-downstream impacts across key reservoirs in the Nile Basin. Focusing on the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam (GERD), Rosaries Dam, Merowe Dam, and the Aswan High Dam, the analysis spans 15 months (August 2023 to October 2024). By systematically selecting 30 points across each reservoir, monthly boxplots of surface water elevation were generated, revealing significant temporal and spatial variability. The results show that GERD’s filling phase led to a steady increase in water levels (peaking at ~615 meters from June to October 2024), while downstream reservoirs experienced sharp declines. Rosaries and Merowe recorded their lowest levels in July 2024, with delayed recovery starting in September 2024, while the Aswan High Dam showed a gradual decline from April to August 2024, reflecting reduced upstream flows.The findings highlight the hydrological interdependence of Nile Basin reservoirs, where upstream water management, particularly GERD’s operations, has cascading effects on downstream regions. This study demonstrates the value of SWOT satellite data in capturing transboundary water dynamics and emphasizes the need for cooperative water governance strategies, including coordinated reservoir operations and data-sharing mechanisms, to address downstream water stress. By offering actionable data for sustainable resource management, th

recent_papers.title: Assessing Coastal Vulnerability and Climate-Driven Migration Risk in West Africa
recent_papers.venue: IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium
recent_papers.abstract: This study presents a GIS-based multi-criteria decision-making framework to assess climate-induced migration risk along the West African coast. We developed a comprehensive risk index that integrates environmental hazards such as flood frequency and socio-economic vulnerability indicators, including poverty levels, population density, and adaptive capacity. By utilizing datasets such as the Geocoded Disasters (GDIS) dataset, Social Vulnerability Index (SVI), Poverty and Adaptive Capacity Index (PACI), and the Population Exposure Index (PEI), the study identifies regions most susceptible to displacement. Results reveal that areas like Benin’s Abomey-Calavi, Cotonou, and Akpo-Misserete are especially vulnerable due to high disaster frequency, substantial population exposure, and limited adaptive capacity. These findings offer vital insights for policymakers and humanitarian organizations to prioritize climate adaptation strategies and mitigate the risks of climate-induced migration in West Africa.

recent_papers.title: Multisensor Data Fusion and GIS-DRASTIC Integration for Groundwater Vulnerability Assessment With Rainfall Consideration
recent_papers.venue: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
recent_papers.abstract: In many areas of the world, particularly in arid and semi-arid regions, groundwater is the primary source of fresh water, and it supplies around one-third of the world's fresh water. Agriculture is the primary economic sector on the coast in the southern district (Nowshera). More food productivity is required due to the expanding population and diminishing agricultural lands, which increases the use of chemical pesticides and fertilizers in farming. The current study was conducted in northwestern parts of Pakistan to evaluate the impacts of the frequent use of pesticides and fertilizers in agricultural fields. Nine hydrogeological parameters were considered, and the GIS-based DRASTIC index was used to generate the final groundwater vulnerability map. The index map (ranging from 220 to 1980) was further classified into five classes based on index vulnerability: very low (220–345), low (346–670), moderate (671–730), high (731–1239), and very high (1240–1980). Nitrate and TDS, the two reliable and recognized scientific water quality measurements, have been used to validate the model. By regulating and controlling anthropogenic and agricultural pollution, the danger of contamination can be decreased. This research will aid in understanding the possible dangers and risks related to the usage of pesticides in agriculture and other industries. Furthermore, it will help identify the specific pesticides causing the contamination, assess the extent and severity of the contamination, an

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My work is driven by the belief that pressing environmental challenges — from dam safety and groundwater vulnerability to wildfires and hydrological extremes — demand integrated, multi-sensor approaches. I combine satellite remote sensing, machine learning, and physics-based modeling, as reflected in studies like our Persistent Scatterer Interferometry analysis of Koyna Dam and our GIS-DRASTIC groundwater vulnerability assessments, because no single tool captures the full complexity of Earth's systems.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are comfortable working across disciplinary boundaries — someone equally at home processing Sentinel-1 SAR imagery and interpreting climate indices or running Finite Element simulations. A genuine curiosity about how environmental stressors interact, whether that's drought-to-flood hydrological switches across the CONUS or wildfire risk in Canadian boreal forests, is more important to me than a perfectly polished résumé.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in my group involves a mix of data acquisition and processing — pulling satellite imagery, running classification models like the logistic regression and XGBoost pipelines we developed for wildfire prediction — alongside reading current literature and contributing to manuscript drafts. Students also spend time in collaborative discussions where we connect findings from one project, say water use efficiency under SSP scenarios in the Lower Colorado River region, to broader climate resilience questions.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I aim to guide students from conceptual framing through to publication, giving them enough independence to develop their own scientific voice while staying closely engaged with their analytical choices. I encourage students to take ownership of methodological decisions, as our work on multisensor data fusion and crop-switching strategies illustrates — these projects require students to make real trade-offs and defend them rigorously.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I do not currently have active external grants to report, so prospective students should discuss funding arrangements directly with me and explore fellowship opportunities through Chapman University and external agencies such as NASA, NOAA, or NSF, whose thematic priorities align closely with our remote sensing and climate resilience research portfolio.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Reach out with a concrete statement of which research thread in my group excites you most — whether it's SAR-based infrastructure monitoring, machine learning for hazard prediction, or arid-region water sustainability — and explain why your background positions you to contribute to it. Vague expressions of interest in 'remote sensing in general' are far less compelling than a focused connection between your prior experience and a specific problem we are actively working on.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a genuinely collaborative feel — people are pulling from totally different toolkits, SAR processing one day, XGBoost pipelines the next, and somehow it works because everyone is expected to bridge those gaps. It can feel a bit eclectic if you come from a narrow background, but that's kind of the point.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We meet one-on-one roughly every week or two, though it can slip when everyone is heads-down on manuscript revisions or data crunches. Group check-ins happen less formally than I expected — a lot of coordination happens over email or shared docs.
student_survey_responses.independence: Hesham gives you real latitude on methodological decisions, which I appreciated once I found my footing but found disorienting in the first semester when I was still learning what questions were even worth asking. He'll push back hard in manuscript review, so you need to be able to defend your choices with evidence, not just intuition.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I had known upfront how fluid the funding situation is — there's no big standing grant that covers everyone, so you're expected to actively chase fellowship applications through NASA or NSF fairly early on. It's a good skill to build, but it was stressful to figure that out mid-first-year.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who genuinely like hopping between problem domains do really well here — if you can go from reading a paper on Persistent Scatterer Interferometry for dam monitoring to drafting a section on groundwater DRASTIC indices without losing momentum, you'll fit right in.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need a tightly scoped, handed-down dissertation topic tend to struggle because projects here are often still being shaped as you join them. If you need a lot of external structure to stay productive, that mismatch shows up fast.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The vibe is small and international — most of the grad students have pretty different geographic and technical backgrounds, which makes conversations interesting but means there's no single 'standard workflow' everyone defaults to. You learn a lot from each other, but you also have to be comfortable being the person figuring something out for the first time.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Hesham is pretty accessible by email and will sit with you to work through an analytical problem if you ask, but formal scheduled meetings are not rigidly structured — I'd say substantive one-on-ones happen maybe twice a month on average, more when a deadline is approaching.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a lot of ownership expected from early on; he pointed me toward wildfire risk modeling and the literature and then essentially said 'tell me what you find.' That's exciting if you're self-directed, but the feedback loop can feel long if you're used to a more hands-on advising style.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The projects span a huge range — hydrology, infrastructure monitoring, arid-region water sustainability — and while that breadth looks great on a CV, it took me a while to figure out how to frame a coherent dissertation narrative from it. I'd advise incoming students to have that conversation with Hesham explicitly in the first semester.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you come in with a specific itch — say, you're obsessed with how drought and flood cycles interact across large watersheds, or you want to apply ML to hazard prediction — and you can connect that to his active work, you'll thrive because he'll invest real intellectual energy in you.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who assumed the remote sensing angle meant mostly fieldwork were caught off guard — this is almost entirely computational and literature-heavy, and if you're not comfortable spending long stretches debugging satellite image preprocessing pipelines or tuning classification models, the day-to-day can feel grinding.

recruiting_status.rationale: lab page exists but no funding, publication, or recruitment signals

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My work is driven by the goal of building machine learning algorithms that actually work at scale on real-world social media data, including text in languages other than English that most of the research community overlooks. I believe good algorithms should be grounded in solid mathematical foundations — my earlier work on algebraic structures like monoids and groups for machine learning reflects that conviction. Practical impact and theoretical rigor aren't opposites; the best research achieves both.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I want students who are genuinely curious and self-directed — people who can pick up a messy real-world problem and figure out how to make progress on it without needing every step spelled out. A strong mathematical background and comfort with programming are essential, and any prior exposure to NLP or large-scale data systems is a real plus. I also appreciate students who care about the broader social implications of the technology they build.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week involves a mix of reading current literature, writing and debugging code for ongoing experiments, and meeting with me to discuss results and next steps. Because my lab focuses on large-scale social media problems, students spend meaningful time wrangling real, noisy datasets rather than working only on clean benchmarks. I encourage students to blog or otherwise communicate their work publicly, as I do myself on my own site.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to give students a lot of ownership over their projects while staying close enough to catch problems early. I came to academia from a non-traditional background — I was a naval officer before becoming a conscientious objector — and that shapes how I mentor: I respect independence and unconventional thinking. My goal is for every student to leave the lab capable of defining and executing their own research agenda.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I don't have large external grants to report at the moment, so prospective students should have an honest conversation with me about funding availability before applying. Claremont McKenna College is a liberal arts institution, which means the research environment is different from a large R1 university, and that context matters for planning. I'm committed to being transparent with students about resources so they can make well-informed decisions.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Reach out directly at mike@izbicki.me — I mean it when I say I can be contacted for just about anything. Tell me specifically why large-scale social media ML or non-English NLP interests you, and point me to something you've built or written that shows how you think. A genuine intellectual connection and a willingness to engage with hard, open-ended problems will matter far more to me than a perfect GPA.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a pretty small group, which means you get a lot of direct face time with Mike and your work actually matters to the lab's direction — there's no hiding in a crowd of twenty grad students. The vibe is collaborative but not hand-holdy; people share papers and code pretty openly, and Mike genuinely seems to enjoy the weird rabbit holes that come up with real social media data.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We meet one-on-one roughly once a week, sometimes more if something is breaking or a deadline is close. The meetings are pretty focused — he'll ask what you tried, what broke, and what you think the next move is, so you need to show up with actual progress or at least a clear picture of where you're stuck.
student_survey_responses.independence: Mike gives you a lot of rope, which is great if you're the type who likes owning a problem end-to-end, but it can feel disorienting at first if you're used to structured coursework telling you what to do next. I've found that the projects that go well are the ones where I came in with my own angle on the problem rather than waiting for him to define everything.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish someone had told me upfront how different the funding situation is here compared to a big R1 — there's no sprawling lab budget to throw compute at everything, so you learn to be resourceful fast. It's not a dealbreaker but it changes what kinds of experiments are practical, and knowing that going in would have saved me some frustration early on.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you genuinely find non-English social media data interesting — not just as a résumé line but as an actual puzzle — you'll have a great time because the problems are legitimately messy and understudied. People who like the math side of ML and also don't mind getting their hands dirty cleaning horrible real-world text fit in really well.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who need a lot of external structure or frequent validation tend to flounder a bit, because Mike's style is to push you toward self-sufficiency pretty quickly. Also, if your heart is set on a very large-scale compute environment or a giant collaborative team, the liberal arts college setting is going to feel like a mismatch.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab is small enough that it barely feels like a 'lab' in the traditional sense — it's more like a tight reading group where everyone is also running experiments. Mike brings a pretty unconventional energy to everything, whether that's the North Korea blog posts or the way he talks about the ethics of the technology we build, which keeps things from feeling purely transactional.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Meetings happen weekly as a baseline, but they're not super ceremonial — sometimes it's a quick thirty-minute check-in, sometimes it turns into a two-hour whiteboard session if we're working through something mathematically tricky. He's generally pretty responsive over email between meetings if you hit a real blocker.
student_survey_responses.independence: You are expected to drive your own project pretty much from day one, which I think is honestly the right way to do research but can feel like being thrown in the deep end. Mike will push back on your ideas and ask hard questions, but he's not going to tell you 'do this experiment next' — figuring out what's worth doing is half the job.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't fully appreciate how much time goes into just wrangling the data before you can do any interesting modeling — social media text in low-resource languages is a disaster of encoding issues, slang, and missing context, and that's genuinely most of the work. Also, the public blogging thing he encourages is actually useful for your own thinking, even if it feels awkward at first.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who have a strong math background but are also comfortable writing a thousand lines of messy data pipeline code without complaining seem to do really well. There's also a certain kind of person who's motivated by working on problems that most of the NLP community has ignored, and if that's you, this environment is surprisingly energizing.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you're primarily motivated by publishing quickly on trendy benchmarks or you need the prestige signal of a large well-funded lab on your CV, this probably isn't the right fit. The pace can also feel slow when you're stuck in a data-cleaning phase for weeks, and not everyone handles that ambiguity gracefully.

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a collegial but quietly competitive atmosphere — people are generally willing to help each other troubleshoot, but there's an unspoken expectation that you're keeping pace with everyone else. It's not cutthroat, but you'll notice pretty quickly who's publishing and who isn't.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have a full lab meeting once a week that runs about 90 minutes, and then one-on-ones with John that happen roughly every two weeks, though they can slip to monthly when he's traveling or grant season hits. I've learned to be proactive about scheduling those check-ins or they quietly disappear.
student_survey_responses.independence: John gives you a lot of rope — maybe more than I expected coming straight from undergrad. He'll point you toward a problem and then largely expect you to design your own approach, which is exciting in year three but genuinely disorienting in year one.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much the day-to-day culture depends on which senior grad students happen to be in the lab when you arrive — my cohort got lucky, but I've heard it can feel isolating when the senior students are heads-down on dissertations. Also, get comfortable with ambiguity early; there's no hand-holding on project scoping.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who are self-directed, can tolerate uncertainty for long stretches, and genuinely enjoy reading broadly outside their immediate project do really well here. If you have an intrinsic drive to follow a question wherever it leads, John will support that and give you room to run.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who need frequent structured feedback or clear short-term milestones to stay motivated tend to feel adrift. If you're someone who needs external accountability to write or to make decisions, you'll have to build that scaffolding yourself because it's not baked into the lab's rhythms.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small lab, which means there's real camaraderie and you actually get to know everyone, but it also means when interpersonal tension pops up there's nowhere to hide. On balance I prefer it — we share equipment time without drama and people actually show up to each other's practice talks.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Lab meetings rotate so each of us presents every six weeks or so, plus there are informal journal club sessions that are technically optional but you'd feel weird skipping. One-on-ones with John are supposed to be biweekly but realistically I'd say monthly is the average, and they're fairly short — twenty to forty minutes.
student_survey_responses.independence: John trusts you to own your project, which I've come to appreciate, but the transition from coursework to that level of independence was rougher than I anticipated. He's very available for big-picture conversations but he's not going to remind you to run your next control experiment.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish someone had told me that email is genuinely not his preferred medium — you'll get a reply eventually, but if something is time-sensitive you need to catch him in person or you'll wait a week. Also, the lab doesn't have a ton of administrative support, so budget a surprising amount of time for ordering and compliance paperwork yourself.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students with a genuine intellectual curiosity that goes beyond their dissertation chapter — people who come to lab meeting excited about someone else's data — fit in really well here. John seems to light up around students who push back on his ideas respectfully, so don't be afraid to disagree with him if you have evidence.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you're hoping for a mentor who will co-write your first paper with you or who will check in proactively when you go quiet for a few weeks, you may feel unsupported. The lab rewards initiative so heavily that students who are struggling sometimes become invisible rather than getting pulled back in.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab is pretty small, which means you get a lot of direct face time with Dr. Panangadan but also means there's not a big cohort of senior grad students to lean on for day-to-day troubleshooting. It's collaborative when everyone's timelines align, but mostly you're heads-down on your own project.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We do one-on-ones roughly every week or two, though it can slip to biweekly when he's deep in service commitments. There's no formal group meeting, which I miss because I don't always know what my labmates are working on.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own research direction pretty early, which I found exciting but also a little disorienting in my first year when I didn't yet know what 'good enough' looked like for a result. He'll redirect you if you're way off track, but he won't hand you a roadmap.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd understood that feedback cycles can be slow, so you need to build in extra lead time before conference deadlines or you'll be scrambling alone. Coming in with strong technical writing habits would have saved me months of frustration.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are genuinely self-motivated and comfortable sitting with ambiguity tend to do well here — if you need constant check-ins to feel productive, this environment will feel isolating. A background in applied math or data-heavy systems work also gives you a real running start.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who expect a structured onboarding or a clear project handed to them on day one tend to feel lost for longer than is healthy. Funding conversations can also feel opaque, so if financial stability is a major stressor for you, it's worth having that conversation explicitly upfront.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a low-drama, heads-down kind of environment — people are friendly but not super socially tight, and most collaboration happens organically around shared datasets rather than through organized lab events. Dr. Panangadan sets a calm, professional tone, which I appreciate even if the lab doesn't have a lot of energy or buzz.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Meetings happen but aren't always consistent; I've gone three weeks without a substantive check-in during busy semesters, which can feel like you're drifting. When we do meet, the conversations are focused and useful, so quality is good even if cadence is unpredictable.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a real expectation that you'll identify interesting problems yourself rather than waiting for assignments, which has genuinely made me a stronger researcher over time. That said, early on I made some costly detours that a bit more structured mentorship might have prevented.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't realize how important it would be to proactively seek out external collaborators and reading groups outside the lab to fill in intellectual community. Also, getting familiar with the specific application domains Dr. Panangadan works in before you arrive will make your first semester much less of a scramble.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who have a specific problem they care deeply about and want dedicated time and reasonable autonomy to pursue it will find this a good setup. If you're the type who can self-regulate your schedule and push yourself without external deadlines, you'll flourish.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need frequent encouragement or structured milestones to stay motivated, the loose cadence here can quietly derail you — I've seen labmates hit rough patches that lingered longer than they should have simply because nobody noticed the drift. The publication pipeline can also feel slower than in larger, more resource-rich labs, which matters if you're on a tight job-market timeline.

name: Kanika Sood

recent_papers.title: Application of AI on Age Longevity and Relevance of Feature Selection on Smaller Datasets
recent_papers.venue: Ubiquitous Computing, Electronics & Mobile Communication Conference
recent_papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of age longevity across the global community remains an elusive and complex challenge, chiefly due to a plethora of socioeconomic and health-related factors involved. One critical barrier to improving prediction is through the quantity and quality of available data, the likes of which are directly responsible for any model's capacity to learn and predict. This study hypothesizes that while sample size may significantly influence machine learning models' predictive accuracy and generalizability, precise data preparation and preprocessing could mitigate, if not negate, the risks and detriments of underfitting and sampling error. Using a globally sourced dataset encompassing demographic and national indicators from multiple countries, four predictive models - k-Nearest Neighbors, Decision Trees, Random Forest, Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, and Gradient Boosting Regression - are developed and evaluated to test the effectiveness of thorough preprocessing on a small, yet high-dimensional, dataset. The investigation highlights how different levels of data completeness and rigorousness of preprocessing impact model performance, especially in scenarios with limited sample sizes. Results indicate that, while careful preprocessing can mitigate some undersampling issues, critically small datasets inherently limit predictive accuracy regardless of the model or preparation involved.

recent_papers.title: NLP-Driven Emotion Recognition for Individuals with Impaired Speech
recent_papers.abstract: Recent advancements in Natural Language Processing (NLP) and speech recognition have significantly enhanced human-computer interaction. However, these technologies often fails to serve people with speech impairments, creating persistent barriers to communication, education, and inclusion. The re-search introduces a humanitarian-focused NLP system designed to bridge this gap by integrating emotional recognition into the visual text processing pipeline. This system extracts text from images using Optical Character Recognition (OCR), classifies the emotional tone of the extracted content using sentiment analysis, and converts it into audible speech using Text-to-Speech (TTS) technology. This dual-layer processing particularly supports speech-impaired individuals by enabling them to access not only content but also the emotional tone, bridging criti-cal communication gaps. This project brings forward a more meaningful interactions with written content, particularly for those with impairments affecting literacy, vision, or cognitive processing.

recent_papers.title: Machine Learning Meets the Subconscious: Can AI Decode Dreams?
recent_papers.venue: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
recent_papers.abstract: This study examines the relationship between the structured features of dream narratives and the presence of negative emotional content. We extract and engineer features that include demographic indicators (e.g., gender), character composition (e.g., proportions of family, friends, animals, dead or imaginary figures) and emotional behavior indices (Aggression/Friendliness). To model the relationship between dream characteristics and negative emotions, we implement a classification algorithm, k-nearest neighbors (KNN) and multiple regression algorithms: linear regression, k-nearest neighbors (KNN), support vector machine (SVM), random forest, and extreme gradient boost (XGBoost), and evaluate performance using multiple evaluation metrics such as Mean Squared Error (MSE). XGBoost achieved the lowest MSE (0.2065), outperforming other models in capturing non-linear patterns. Among the features, Aggression/Friendliness demonstrate the strongest associations with negative emotions, while Friendliness and sexuality show moderate correlations. Gender has minimal impact, suggesting that dream content may be largely independent of the dreamer’s gender. Although the overall predictive performance of the models is modest, the findings provide insight into the dream elements that can influence the emotional tone. This work highlights both the promise and limitations of using machine learning for effective dream analysis and suggests future directions that integrate psychological or contex

recent_papers.title: Using Machine Learning to Predict Hypertension from Lifestyle Factors: A Study Using NHANES Data
recent_papers.venue: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
recent_papers.abstract: Hypertension, a leading global health concern, often goes undetected until it manifests itself as severe cardiovascular complications. Although prior research has demonstrated the utility of machine learning in hypertension prediction, many models rely on clinical or laboratory-only variables that limit generalizability and accessibility in the real world. This study addresses this gap by exploring the application of machine learning models to predict hypertension using publicly available survey data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES). We develop and evaluate predictive models employing random forest, gradient boosting, bagging decision trees, and Logistic Regression using lifestyle, demographic, and medical history variables. A complete feature set with 56 features and a reduced set with 20 features are analyzed to assess the impact of feature selection on model performance. The results indicate that gradient boosting with reduced features achieve the highest accuracy (73.78%), demonstrating the potential of optimized feature selection to enhance prediction efficiency. These findings highlight the utility of machine learning in enabling cost-effective and early risk assessment tools for hypertension, paving the way for more personalized and preventive healthcare strategies.

recent_papers.title: Analyzing Effects of Parenting Style on Child Development Using Machine Learning
recent_papers.venue: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Machine Learning and Data Science (AMLDS)
recent_papers.abstract: Child development is the process of a child developing the foundation of their health, learning capabilities, and behavioral characteristics. Each individual undergoes this development as a child, and it’s essential to understand how parental parenting habits impact their child. Parents and children from primary to secondary schools were part of a study that answered questionnaires about parenting styles and their children’s habits and characteristics. We use different classification techniques, such as KNN, Random Forest, SVM, and AdaBoost to create a predictive model to assign different areas of child development with a ranking—from 1 (high) to 4 (low). Our results demonstrate that the best performance is obtained from Random Forest and a child’s development can be well predicted based on parenting habits.

recent_papers.title: Duplicate and Similar Questions Pair Detection using Natural Language Processing
recent_papers.venue: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Machine Learning and Data Science (AMLDS)
recent_papers.abstract: The proliferation of user-generated content on the internet, particularly in the form of questions on platforms such as Stack Overflow and Quora, presents a significant challenge in identifying duplicate or near-duplicate queries. Research indicates that up to 40 percent of questions on Quora and 30 percent on Stack Overflow are duplicates or similar, highlighting the need for effective methods to manage this issue. Duplicate questions can lead to redundant information, complicate search engine results, and degrade the user experience, making it difficult to find specific, accurate answers. In response to this challenge, natural language processing (NLP) techniques have been employed to detect duplicate questions within datasets. The application of NLP algorithms for duplicate question detection not only enhances dataset quality and user experience on knowledge-sharing platforms but also has broader implications for improving search engine results and minimizing dataset redundancy.

recent_papers.title: Leveraging Machine Learning Models for Climate Data Interpretation and Forecasting
recent_papers.venue: 2025 International Conference on Advanced Machine Learning and Data Science (AMLDS)
recent_papers.abstract: Climate change poses significant challenges for ecosystems, agriculture, and societal resilience, driven by rising global temperatures and shifting climate patterns. This work leverages historical climate data from the Berkeley Earth Surface Temperature repository to analyze and predict global warming trends using machine learning techniques. Key features such as temporal components (Year, Month) and spatial coordinates (Latitude, Longitude) are processed to train four machine learning algorithms: Linear Regression, Random Forest, Support Vector Regression (SVR), and K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN). Among these, the Random Forest Regressor demonstrated the highest predictive accuracy with an R2 score of 0.9857, followed closely by KNN at 0.9706. These results underscore the capability of machine learning to uncover meaningful trends in climate data and enhance our understanding of global warming, enabling more informed analyses of its impacts.

recent_papers.title: Predicting Firewall Actions for Anomaly Detection Using Machine Learning
recent_papers.venue: 2025 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Energy Technologies (ICECET)
recent_papers.abstract: Firewalls play a vital role in network security. To adapt to new threats, and make our current means of detecting anomalies more efficient, we can use machine learning to analyze network data and predict the approach a firewall should take. By classifying network packets into action categories - Allow, Drop, Deny and ResetBoth - the proposed system aims to enhance the firewall decision-making process, thus strengthening overall network security and reducing the incidence of false positives and negatives. This work analyzes several machine learning algorithms such as SVM, K-nearest neighbor, logistic regression, random forest, and decision trees to determine the best predictor of firewall actions. With random forest, we achieve around 99% precision and 99.2% recall.
