pi_survey.research_philosophy: My work sits at the intersection of machine learning and real-world human concerns — from decoding dream narratives and predicting infant mortality to understanding how parenting styles shape child development. I believe AI is most valuable when it addresses questions that genuinely matter to people's lives, and my recent papers reflect that commitment to socially grounded, data-driven inquiry.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are curious about applying machine learning to messy, human-centered datasets — the kind where class imbalance, small sample sizes, and feature engineering challenges are the norm rather than the exception. If you're excited by problems like using NLP to support individuals with speech impairments or modeling socioeconomic factors in longevity prediction, we'll likely work well together.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: Students in my group typically spend time on data preprocessing and feature engineering, experimenting with classification and prediction models, and writing up findings for publication — all five of my 2025 papers involved student collaborators working through these exact steps. Expect regular check-ins to discuss results, troubleshoot models, and refine research questions.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I mentor students hands-on through the full research cycle, from formulating a hypothesis — as we did in our age longevity study — to preparing a manuscript. I emphasize that good research requires both technical rigor and clear communication of why the problem matters to a broader audience.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I currently do not have external grant funding, so students should be aware that research assistantships may be limited. That said, working within the Department of Computer Science at CSUF provides access to departmental resources and a collaborative faculty community, and I'm actively building a portfolio of published work that can support future funding applications.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Reach out with a specific paper of mine that resonates with you — whether it's the NLP-driven emotion recognition work or the infant mortality prediction study — and tell me concretely what draws you to that problem. Genuine intellectual curiosity about human-centered AI applications matters far more to me than a perfect GPA.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a pretty small, tight-knit feel — there aren't dozens of grad students competing for attention, which means you actually get real face time with Dr. Sood. The flip side is that there's no senior PhD student culture to absorb from; you figure a lot out alongside each other.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We do regular one-on-ones, usually weekly or close to it, and she genuinely comes prepared with feedback on whatever model results or writeup you shared beforehand. It's not the kind of meeting where you show up and she's clearly forgotten where you left off.
student_survey_responses.independence: She'll push you to own the research direction more than I expected — she'll ask 'why does this problem matter to someone outside CS?' pretty early on, which forces you to think beyond just getting accuracy numbers up. That said, she's very reachable when you hit a wall on something like handling severe class imbalance in a small health dataset.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known upfront that RA funding is genuinely uncertain here — it's not a complaint, she's transparent about it, but I came in hoping for stable support and had to patch things together with TAships. Plan your finances accordingly before you commit.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you actually care about the application domain — like, if you find yourself reading about infant health outcomes or speech impairments outside of work — you'll fit in naturally because that enthusiasm shapes how the research questions get framed. Pure methods people who just want a benchmark to beat will feel a little lost.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who need a large peer cohort for motivation or want to learn by osmosis from a bustling lab environment will find it harder here; the group is small enough that you have to be fairly self-directed between check-ins.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's collaborative in the sense that papers genuinely involve students at every step — I have my name on work I actually contributed to rather than just being thanked in an acknowledgments section. But the vibe is more 'serious working group' than social lab family, which suits me fine.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Check-ins happen consistently, and she's good about not canceling last-minute, which I've heard horror stories about from friends in other labs. Sometimes I wished we had a longer group meeting format to hear what other students are wrestling with, since we mostly meet one-on-one.
student_survey_responses.independence: The balance leans toward guided independence — she'll hand you a dataset and a research question she cares about, but then expects you to drive the feature engineering and modeling decisions yourself and defend your choices. It took me a semester to calibrate to that expectation.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The datasets we work with are genuinely messy in ways that textbook ML courses don't prepare you for — small N, weird missingness patterns, sensitive variables — so if your background is mostly clean Kaggle-style problems, budget real time for that learning curve early on.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who like writing and can articulate why a technical result matters to a non-CS audience will get a lot out of the mentorship; she pushes hard on the 'so what' framing in every manuscript, and that skill has made me a better communicator overall.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you're hoping to pivot toward deep learning infrastructure or large-scale systems work, this probably isn't the right fit — the research is very much applied ML on human-centered tabular and text data, and the scope of projects reflects that focus.

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab is fairly small and technically focused, which means you get a lot of direct exposure to the actual research problems without a lot of bureaucratic noise. It can feel a bit quiet socially — there aren't many group outings or traditions — but people are generally collegial and willing to help when you're stuck.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have one-on-one meetings roughly every week or two, depending on how active things are with a project. Group meetings happen but not on a rigid schedule, so you have to be proactive about flagging blockers rather than waiting for the next formal check-in.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own work pretty quickly — within the first year you're largely setting your own weekly agenda. This is great if you're self-motivated, but if you need a lot of hand-holding to figure out what to read or what experiment to run next, it can feel isolating.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd come in with stronger foundations in applied cryptography and systems security, because the learning curve in the first semester was steeper than I expected and there wasn't a structured onboarding curriculum. Having a paper or two in the area before you join would make a real difference.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who have a genuine curiosity about privacy-preserving computation and can sit with ambiguous, open-ended problems do really well here. If you enjoy reading dense crypto or systems papers and translating them into something implementable, you'll find the work satisfying.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Anyone expecting frequent, detailed feedback on writing or a clear milestone checklist will probably feel adrift. The mentorship style leans more toward high-level direction than granular day-to-day coaching, so some students have had to seek writing feedback elsewhere.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The vibe is more 'quiet workshop' than 'bustling lab' — most people are heads-down on their own projects, but there's genuine mutual respect and no toxic competitiveness. I appreciate that there's no pressure to perform for an audience; you're just expected to produce good work.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Meetings are fairly infrequent by default — maybe biweekly if things are moving, sometimes longer gaps. On one hand this keeps you from feeling micromanaged; on the other hand, if you're going in a wrong direction you might not find out for a while unless you proactively seek input.
student_survey_responses.independence: Research independence ramps up fast, which I've personally found motivating even when it's uncomfortable. By my second year I was fully owning my project direction, including deciding which conferences to target, which felt like real professional development.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't realize how much time would go into systems implementation work versus purely theoretical contributions — a lot of the research involves actually building and benchmarking prototypes, so solid programming skills matter more than the formal coursework suggested they would.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who can balance theoretical rigor with hands-on implementation tend to flourish here. If you're comfortable context-switching between reading a security proof and debugging a C++ prototype in the same week, this is a good environment.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came from a background where your advisor was your primary social anchor in grad school, this might feel lonely — the lab doesn't have a strong communal identity, and you'll need to build your own support network through other channels like department reading groups or collaborators outside the lab.

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a pretty collaborative feel — people share datasets and talk through methods openly, which I didn't expect going in. It's small enough that you actually know what everyone else is working on, which I personally like.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We meet one-on-one roughly every two weeks, sometimes stretching to three if things are busy on either end. There's also a group meeting that happens monthly where everyone presents progress or a paper they've been reading.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own project pretty early, which was a steep curve for me as a first-year. Once you hit your stride it feels empowering, but the initial months can feel like you're figuring things out alone.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known that having a strong statistics background would matter as much as it does — a lot of the applied work ends up leaning heavily on modeling choices. Brushing up on probability theory before arriving would have saved me real headaches.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are self-motivated and can tolerate ambiguity in their research questions do really well here. If you like being handed a defined problem with clear milestones, this setup will frustrate you.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need a lot of structured feedback on a tight schedule can find the pacing inconsistent. It's not that support isn't there, but you have to ask for it deliberately rather than waiting for it to come to you.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Honestly the vibe is pretty low-drama, which I appreciate after hearing horror stories from friends in other labs. There's an implicit expectation that you're serious about the work, but nobody is performatively grinding or making you feel bad for leaving at a reasonable hour.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: One-on-ones happen but the scheduling can slip when the semester gets hectic — I've learned to be proactive about putting time on the calendar rather than waiting. When we do meet the conversations are substantive and he asks genuinely good questions that redirect my thinking.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a lot of latitude in how you approach a problem, which I think reflects a real belief that students should develop their own intellectual voice. The flip side is that if you're spinning your wheels, you might spin for a while before someone intervenes.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't realize how much of the work would touch real applied datasets with all the messiness that entails — my coursework was a lot cleaner than the actual research environment. Coming in with some experience wrangling real data would have been a genuine asset.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People with intellectual curiosity who can connect quantitative methods to practical questions seem to get the most out of this environment. It also helps if you're okay with your research question evolving significantly from what you originally proposed.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you're hoping for a mentor who will check in on you proactively and give you detailed written feedback on drafts without prompting, you may feel a bit adrift. The relationship rewards students who communicate their needs clearly and early.

name: Negin Forouzesh

recruiting_status.rationale: active lab signals present (funding or publications) but no explicit recruitment statement

recent_papers.title: Multi-Objective Loss Function For Free Energy Calculations
recent_papers.venue: ACM International Conference on Bioinformatics, Computational Biology and Biomedicine
recent_papers.abstract: Accurate estimation of binding free energy (ΔΔG) remains a critical challenge in drug discovery, directly influencing the development of effective therapeutics. Physics-based computational methods—such as molecular dynamics simulations and implicit solvent modeling—offer rigorous, atomistic insights into molecular recognition but are often constrained by computational costs and limited scalability. In contrast, deep learning models, particularly graph convolutional networks (GCNs), have demonstrated the ability to rapidly predict molecular properties by learning hierarchical representations from large-scale chemical data, yet frequently lack explicit incorporation of physical laws, leading to potential issues with interpretability and generalizability. Hybrid physics-guided neural networks seek to overcome these limitations by embedding physically meaningful features and constraints within deep learning architectures. In this study, we introduce a multi-objective loss function that simultaneously optimizes empirical error, structural similarity, and physical consistency by integrating molecular fingerprints with physics-based features such as electrostatic and van der Waals energies in a unified GCN framework. Applied to 65–75 host-guest systems, this approach yields substantial improvements in entropy (TΔS) prediction accuracy, reducing the mean absolute difference (MAD) from 5.80 to 1.07 kcal/mol, while also enhancing the accuracy and convergence of binding free energy (ΔΔG

recent_papers.title: Physics-Guided Active Learning for New Ligand Discovery
recent_papers.abstract: Structure-based drug discovery seeks to identify small molecules that bind to protein targets and modulate their function, but current workflows remain slow, costly, and data-limited. While generative models can design ligands from protein structures, they often lack physical grounding and fail to generalize beyond static training data. We propose an active learning framework that generates 3D ligands conditioned on protein pocket geometry and iteratively retrains on the top 10% of candidates, evaluated using a composite score integrating binding affinity and solvation energy. On the refined PDBBind v2019 dataset, our approach yields consistent dataset-wide improvements: median binding affinity increases from -9.3 to -9.7 kcal/mol (compared to -8.7 kcal/mol for reference ligands), median solvation free energy decreases from 1.32 to 0.94 kcal/mol, and ASKCOS scores improve from -2.17e4 to -2.05e4, reflecting enhanced synthetic feasibility. On the BRD4 benchmark, our GNINA-AMBER hybrid scoring pipeline correctly ranks a known non-binder last, validating scoring robustness. These results demonstrate that physics-guided active learning systematically enhances ligand quality, diversity, and physical plausibility, offering a scalable and generalizable approach for structure-based drug design.

recent_papers.title: Optimal Dielectric Boundary for Binding Free Energy Estimates in the Implicit Solvent
recent_papers.venue: Journal of Chemical Information and Modeling
recent_papers.abstract: Accuracy of binding free energy calculations utilizing implicit solvent models is critically affected by parameters of the underlying dielectric boundary, specifically, the atomic and water probe radii. Here, a multidimensional optimization pipeline is used to find optimal atomic radii, specifically for binding calculations in the implicit solvent. To reduce overfitting, the optimization target includes separate, weighted contributions from both binding and hydration free energies. The resulting five-parameter radii set, OPT_BIND5D, is evaluated against experiment for binding free energies of 20 host–guest (H–G) systems, unrelated to the types of structures used in the training. The resulting accuracy for this H–G test set (root mean square error of 2.03 kcal/mol, mean signed error of −0.13 kcal/mol, mean absolute error of 1.68 kcal/mol, and Pearson’s correlation of r = 0.79 with the experimental values) is on par with what can be expected from the fixed charge explicit solvent models. Best agreement with the experiment is achieved when the implicit salt concentration is set equal or close to the experimental conditions.

recent_papers.title: Molecular Docking Improved with Human Spatial Perception Using Virtual Reality
recent_papers.venue: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
recent_papers.abstract: Adaptive steered molecular dynamics (ASMD) is a computational biophysics method in which an external force is applied to a selected set of atoms or a specific reaction coordinate to induce a particular molecular motion. Virtual reality (VR) based methods for protein-ligand docking are beneficial for visualizing on-the-fly interactive molecular dynamics and performing promising docking trajectories. In this paper, we propose a novel method to guide ASMD with optimal trajectories collected from human experiences using interactive molecular dynamics in virtual reality (iMD-VR). We also explain the benefits of using VR as a tool for expediting the process of ligand binding, outlining an experimental protocol that enables iMD-VR users to guide Amprenavir into and out of the binding pockets of HIV-1 protease and recreate their respective crystallographic binding poses within 5 minutes. Later, we discuss our analysis of the results from iMD-VR-assisted ASMD simulation and assess its performance compared to a standard ASMD simulation. From the accuracy point of view, our proposed method calculates higher Potential Mean Force (PMF) values consistently relative to a standard ASMD simulation with an almost twofold increase in all the experiments. Finally, we describe the novelty of the research and discuss results showcasing a faster and more effective convergence of the ligand to the protein's binding site as compared to a standard molecular dynamics simulation, proving the effectivene

recent_papers.title: Calculation of Protein-Ligand Binding Entropies Using a Rule-Based Molecular Fingerprint.
recent_papers.abstract: The use of fast in silico prediction methods for protein-ligand binding free energies holds significant promise for the initial phases of drug development. Numerous traditional physics-based models (e.g., implicit solvent models), however, tend to either neglect or heavily approximate entropic contributions to binding due to their computational complexity. Consequently, such methods often yield imprecise assessments of binding strength. Machine learning (ML) models provide accurate predictions and can often outperform physics-based models. They, however, are often prone to overfitting, and the interpretation of their results can be difficult. Physics-guided ML models combine the consistency of physics-based models with the accuracy of modern data-driven algorithms. This work integrates physics-based model conformational entropies into a graph convolutional network. We introduce a new neural network architecture (a rule-based graph convolutional network) that generates molecular fingerprints according to predefined rules specifically optimized for binding free energy calculations. Our results on 100 small host-guest systems demonstrate significant improvements in convergence and preventing overfitting. We additionally demonstrate the transferability of our proposed hybrid model by training it on the aforementioned host-guest systems and then testing it on six unrelated protein-ligand systems. Our new model shows little difference in training set accuracy compared to a previous

recent_papers.title: Improving the Accuracy of Physics-Based Hydration-Free Energy Predictions by Machine Learning the Remaining Error Relative to the Experiment.
recent_papers.venue: Journal of Chemical Theory and Computation
recent_papers.abstract: The accuracy of computational models of water is key to atomistic simulations of biomolecules. We propose a computationally efficient way to improve the accuracy of the prediction of hydration-free energies (HFEs) of small molecules: the remaining errors of the physics-based models relative to the experiment are predicted and mitigated by machine learning (ML) as a postprocessing step. Specifically, the trained graph convolutional neural network attempts to identify the "blind spots" in the physics-based model predictions, where the complex physics of aqueous solvation is poorly accounted for, and partially corrects for them. The strategy is explored for five classical solvent models representing various accuracy/speed trade-offs, from the fast analytical generalized Born (GB) to the popular TIP3P explicit solvent model; experimental HFEs of small neutral molecules from the FreeSolv set are used for the training and testing. For all of the models, the ML correction reduces the resulting root-mean-square error relative to the experiment for HFEs of small molecules, without significant overfitting and with negligible computational overhead. For example, on the test set, the relative accuracy improvement is 47% for the fast analytical GB, making it, after the ML correction, almost as accurate as uncorrected TIP3P. For the TIP3P model, the accuracy improvement is about 39%, bringing the ML-corrected model's accuracy below the 1 kcal/mol threshold. In general, the relative benefit

recent_papers.title: AmberTools
recent_papers.venue: Journal of Chemical Information and Modeling
recent_papers.abstract: AmberTools is a free and open-source collection of programs used to set up, run, and analyze molecular simulations. The newer features contained within AmberTools23 are briefly described in this Application note.

recent_papers.title: Physics-Guided Deep Generative Model For New Ligand Discovery
recent_papers.venue: ACM International Conference on Bioinformatics, Computational Biology and Biomedicine
recent_papers.abstract: Structure-based drug discovery aims to identify small molecules that can attach to a specific target protein and change its functionality. Recently, deep learning has shown great promise in generating drug-like molecules with specific biochemical features and conditioned with structural features. However, they usually fail to incorporate an essential factor: the underlying physics which guides molecular formation and binding in real-world scenarios. In this work, we describe a physics-guided deep generative model for new ligand discovery, conditioned not only on the binding site but also on physics-based features that describe the binding mechanism between a receptor and a ligand. The proposed hybrid model has been tested on large protein-ligand complexes and small host-guest systems. Using the top-N methodology, on average more than 75% of the generated structures by our hybrid model were stronger binders than the original reference ligand. All of them had higher ΔGbind (affinity) values than the ones generated by the previous state-of-the-art method by an average margin of 1.88 kcal/mol. The visualization of the top-5 ligands generated by the proposed physics-guided model and the reference deep learning model demonstrate more feasible conformations and orientations by the former. The future directions include training and testing the hybrid model on larger datasets, adding more relevant physics-based features, and interpreting the deep learning outcomes from biophysical per

recent_abstracts: Accurate estimation of binding free energy (ΔΔG) remains a critical challenge in drug discovery, directly influencing the development of effective therapeutics. Physics-based computational methods—such as molecular dynamics simulations and implicit solvent modeling—offer rigorous, atomistic insights into molecular recognition but are often constrained by computational costs and limited scalability. In contrast, deep learning models, particularly graph convolutional networks (GCNs), have demonstrated the ability to rapidly predict molecular properties by learning hierarchical representations from large-scale chemical data, yet frequently lack explicit incorporation of physical laws, leading to potential issues with interpretability and generalizability. Hybrid physics-guided neural networks seek to overcome these limitations by embedding physically meaningful features and constraints within deep learning architectures. In this study, we introduce a multi-objective loss function that simultaneously optimizes empirical error, structural similarity, and physical consistency by integrating molecular fingerprints with physics-based features such as electrostatic and van der Waals energies in a unified GCN framework. Applied to 65–75 host-guest systems, this approach yields substantial improvements in entropy (TΔS) prediction accuracy, reducing the mean absolute difference (MAD) from 5.80 to 1.07 kcal/mol, while also enhancing the accuracy and convergence of binding free energy (ΔΔG

Structure-based drug discovery seeks to identify small molecules that bind to protein targets and modulate their function, but current workflows remain slow, costly, and data-limited. While generative models can design ligands from protein structures, they often lack physical grounding and fail to generalize beyond static training data. We propose an active learning framework that generates 3D ligands conditioned on protein pocket geometry and iteratively retrains on the top 10% of candidates, evaluated using a composite score integrating binding affinity and solvation energy. On the refined PDBBind v2019 dataset, our approach yields consistent dataset-wide improvements: median binding affinity increases from -9.3 to -9.7 kcal/mol (compared to -8.7 kcal/mol for reference ligands), median solvation free energy decreases from 1.32 to 0.94 kcal/mol, and ASKCOS scores improve from -2.17e4 to -2.05e4, reflecting enhanced synthetic feasibility. On the BRD4 benchmark, our GNINA-AMBER hybrid scoring pipeline correctly ranks a known non-binder last, validating scoring robustness. These results demonstrate that physics-guided active learning systematically enhances ligand quality, diversity, and physical plausibility, offering a scalable and generalizable approach for structure-based drug design.

Accuracy of binding free energy calculations utilizing implicit solvent models is critically affected by parameters of the underlying dielectric boundary, specifically, the atomic and water probe radii. Here, a multidimensional optimization pipeline is used to find optimal atomic radii, specifically for binding calculations in the implicit solvent. To reduce overfitting, the optimization target includes separate, weighted contributions from both binding and hydration free energies. The resulting five-parameter radii set, OPT_BIND5D, is evaluated against experiment for binding free energies of 20 host–guest (H–G) systems, unrelated to the types of structures used in the training. The resulting accuracy for this H–G test set (root mean square error of 2.03 kcal/mol, mean signed error of −0.13 kcal/mol, mean absolute error of 1.68 kcal/mol, and Pearson’s correlation of r = 0.79 with the experimental values) is on par with what can be expected from the fixed charge explicit solvent models. Best agreement with the experiment is achieved when the implicit salt concentration is set equal or close to the experimental conditions.

Adaptive steered molecular dynamics (ASMD) is a computational biophysics method in which an external force is applied to a selected set of atoms or a specific reaction coordinate to induce a particular molecular motion. Virtual reality (VR) based methods for protein-ligand docking are beneficial for visualizing on-the-fly interactive molecular dynamics and performing promising docking trajectories. In this paper, we propose a novel method to guide ASMD with optimal trajectories collected from human experiences using interactive molecular dynamics in virtual reality (iMD-VR). We also explain the benefits of using VR as a tool for expediting the process of ligand binding, outlining an experimental protocol that enables iMD-VR users to guide Amprenavir into and out of the binding pockets of HIV-1 protease and recreate their respective crystallographic binding poses within 5 minutes. Later, we discuss our analysis of the results from iMD-VR-assisted ASMD simulation and assess its performance compared to a standard ASMD simulation. From the accuracy point of view, our proposed method calculates higher Potential Mean Force (PMF) values consistently relative to a standard ASMD simulation with an almost twofold increase in all the experiments. Finally, we describe the novelty of the research and discuss results showcasing a faster and more effective convergence of the ligand to the protein's binding site as compared to a standard molecular dynamics simulation, proving the effectivene

The use of fast in silico prediction methods for protein-ligand binding free energies holds significant promise for the initial phases of drug development. Numerous traditional physics-based models (e.g., implicit solvent models), however, tend to either neglect or heavily approximate entropic contributions to binding due to their computational complexity. Consequently, such methods often yield imprecise assessments of binding strength. Machine learning (ML) models provide accurate predictions and can often outperform physics-based models. They, however, are often prone to overfitting, and the interpretation of their results can be difficult. Physics-guided ML models combine the consistency of physics-based models with the accuracy of modern data-driven algorithms. This work integrates physics-based model conformational entropies into a graph convolutional network. We introduce a new neural network architecture (a rule-based graph convolutional network) that generates molecular fingerprints according to predefined rules specifically optimized for binding free energy calculations. Our results on 100 small host-guest systems demonstrate significant improvements in convergence and preventing overfitting. We additionally demonstrate the transferability of our proposed hybrid model by training it on the aforementioned host-guest systems and then testing it on six unrelated protein-ligand systems. Our new model shows little difference in training set accuracy compared to a previous

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My work sits at the intersection of computer science and life sciences, where I use physics-based modeling, high-performance computing, and machine learning to tackle real problems in drug discovery. I believe that grounding computational methods in physical reality is essential — for example, our work on 'Optimal Dielectric Boundary for Binding Free Energy Estimates in the Implicit Solvent' shows that careful parameter optimization rooted in physical principles can meaningfully improve predictive accuracy. I also push for integrating these rigorous approaches with modern AI, as in our 'Physics-Guided Active Learning for New Ligand Discovery' framework, because neither pure data-driven nor pure physics-based methods alone are sufficient.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are curious about both the computational and biological sides of problems — someone who isn't afraid to dig into the math behind implicit solvent models one day and prototype a machine learning pipeline the next. A strong foundation in algorithms, programming, or applied mathematics is important, and I especially value students who ask 'why does this method work?' rather than just accepting a tool at face value. Experience with molecular simulation, cheminformatics, or scientific computing is a plus, but genuine intellectual drive matters more to me than a perfectly polished résumé.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in my lab involves a mix of coding and experimentation — running molecular dynamics simulations, developing or tuning models like the multi-objective loss functions we explored for binding free energy calculations, and reading recent literature to stay current. Students also meet with me regularly to discuss results and troubleshoot, and we often have group discussions where everyone shares progress and gives feedback. Depending on the project, a student might also be building or testing VR-based docking workflows or optimizing active learning loops for ligand generation.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to meet students where they are — early on, I provide a lot of structure and close guidance as students build familiarity with our methods and tools, and I gradually encourage more independence as they grow. I care about helping students develop scientific communication skills alongside technical ones, whether that means workshopping a paper draft or practicing how to present results clearly. Being at Cal State LA, I'm especially mindful that many of my students are first-generation or come from underrepresented backgrounds, and I see supporting their full development as a core part of my role.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I currently do not have active external grants to report, so prospective students should be aware that funding support may be limited and opportunities may depend on teaching assistantships or other departmental support. I am actively working to secure external funding to support research in computational drug discovery and AI-guided molecular design. Students who are motivated and can contribute to grant development or who bring their own fellowship opportunities are especially welcome.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read a couple of our recent papers — such as 'Calculation of Protein-Ligand Binding Entropies Using a Rule-Based Molecular Fingerprint' or our active learning work — and come prepared to talk about what interested or confused you, because that conversation tells me a lot more than a list of courses. Be honest about your background and what you want to learn; I'd rather work with someone who is self-aware and eager than someone who overstates their experience. And if you're drawn to research that bridges rigorous physical modeling with modern machine learning in a meaningful application domain, this lab might be a great fit for you.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small group, which means everyone's work is visible and there's a real sense of collective investment in each other's projects — we'll sit together and debug someone's MD simulation setup or talk through why an active learning loop isn't converging. It can feel a little informal compared to bigger labs, but honestly that closeness makes it easier to ask 'dumb' questions without embarrassment.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: I meet one-on-one with Dr. Forouzesh about once a week, and we also have group meetings where everyone gives updates — those are genuinely useful because she'll push you to explain not just what your results show but why the method behaves that way. If I'm stuck mid-week I can usually get a response fairly quickly over email or messaging.
student_survey_responses.independence: Early on she held my hand a lot through the theory — implicit solvent models have a lot of moving parts and she didn't just point me to a paper, she actually walked through the math with me. Now that I'm further along she expects me to own my research direction more, which is good but did catch me off guard when the transition happened.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I had known upfront that TA-ing would probably be part of my financial support situation — it's not a dealbreaker, but it does eat into research hours and I hadn't fully planned for that. If you can come in with a fellowship application already drafted, do it.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who genuinely want to understand the physics underneath the machine learning — if you're someone who gets annoyed when a model works but you don't know why, you'll fit right in here. You also do well if you're comfortable context-switching between coding a simulation pipeline one day and reading a biophysics paper the next.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a large peer community or lots of senior PhD students to learn from, the smaller lab size can feel isolating at times. And if your background is purely one side — all ML and no chemistry, or all biology and no programming — the ramp-up is steep and you have to be patient with yourself.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a workshop-y feel — Dr. Forouzesh will literally pull up your code or your paper draft and go through it with you line by line, which is intense but you learn fast. There's no culture of pretending you understand something you don't; she actually seems to prefer it when you admit confusion and dig in together.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have structured individual check-ins weekly, and the group meetings rotate between everyone presenting progress and doing journal club-style discussions of recent papers. Sometimes an extra meeting gets scheduled if someone hits a milestone or a weird result that's worth unpacking as a group.
student_survey_responses.independence: She starts you off with a pretty defined problem and a clear set of tools to learn, which I appreciated because I came in not knowing much about binding free energy calculations. The expectation of independence grows gradually, but she's transparent about it — she'll tell you directly when she thinks you're ready to lead a direction rather than just assuming you know.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't fully appreciate how much scientific writing was going to be part of the job so early on — she cares about communication skills alongside technical ones, and I spent a lot of my first year rewriting and re-presenting things. It's valuable, but come prepared to treat writing as a first-class skill, not an afterthought.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are self-motivated and curious across domains do really well — the projects touch molecular dynamics, optimization, machine learning, and even some VR-based tools, so if you like variety and aren't precious about staying in one lane, there's a lot to explore. First-gen students especially seem to feel genuinely supported here rather than like they have to figure out the unwritten rules on their own.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: The funding situation is real — I'd encourage anyone considering this lab to have an honest conversation about it before committing, because relying on TA support affects your research bandwidth in ways that sneak up on you. Also, if you want a purely application-focused ML experience without caring about the underlying physical models, you'll butt heads with the lab's philosophy pretty quickly.

name: Behnaz Ghoraani

recent_papers.title: Technology-Enhanced Dual-Task Testing for Alzheimer’s Disease and Related Dementias: A Review of Trends, Tools, and Emerging Directions
recent_papers.venue: IEEE transactions on neural systems and rehabilitation engineering
recent_papers.abstract: Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias (ADRD) are growing global health challenges, projected to affect over 82 million people by 2030. Early diagnosis is essential, offering the potential to extend life expectancy by over 50% and reduce healthcare costs by up to ${\$}$ 150,000 per patient. Dual-task (DT) testing—evaluating motor performance under cognitive load—has emerged as a promising, non-invasive method for early ADRD detection. This review provides a comprehensive synthesis of DT-based ADRD assessments from January 2010 to October 2025, integrating insights from engineering and clinical neuroscience. We explore a broad range of DT paradigms (e.g., gait, balance, upper-limb function), sensing technologies (e.g., wearable sensors, electronic walkways, infrared/depth cameras, video, tablets, and brain imaging tools like fMRI and fNIRS), and analytic approaches, from traditional statistics to deep learning. Emerging tools, including eye-tracking and AI-based video pose estimation, are also discussed. We critically examine methodological trends, highlight key findings, and identify current limitations. Emphasizing the need for equitable, scalable, and clinically viable DT systems, this review highlights the role of modern sensor and AI technologies in enhancing early ADRD detection. It serves as a key resource for engineers, data scientists, and clinicians developing technology-driven tools for early detection and monitoring of neurodegenerative diseases.

recent_papers.title: A COMPREHENSIVE COMPARISON OF TEXT SUMMARIZATION PERFORMANCE: AMULTIFACETED EVALUATION OF LARG LANGUAGE MODELS WITH PRACTICALCONSIDERATIONS
recent_papers.abstract: Text summarization is crucial for mitigating information overload across domains. This research evaluates summarization performance across 17 large language models using seven diverse datasets at three output lengths (50, 100, 150 tokens). We employ a novel multi-dimensional framework assessing factual consistency, semantic similarity, lexical overlap, and human-like evaluation while considering both quality and efficiency factors. Key findings reveal significant differences between models, with specific models excelling in factual accuracy (deepseek-v3), human-like quality (claude-3-5-sonnet), processing efficiency (gemini-1.5-flash), and cost effectiveness (gemini-1.5-flash). Performance varies dramatically by dataset, with models struggling on technical domains but performing well on conversational content. We identified a critical tension between factual consistency (best at 50 tokens) and perceived quality (best at 150 tokens).

recent_papers.title: Multivariate EMD for EEG-Based Characterization of Neurodegenerative Diseases
recent_papers.venue: Asilomar Conference on Signals, Systems and Computers
recent_papers.abstract: Accurate and early detection of neurodegenerative diseases using electroencephalography (EEG) remains a challenging yet vital task in clinical neuroscience. This paper presents a data-driven framework for EEG signal decomposition and feature extraction using Multivariate Empirical Mode Decomposition (MEMD), aiming to enhance classification and spatial analysis of EEG signals in patients with neurodegenerative disorders. EEG recordings from healthy controls (HC), Alzheimer’s disease (AD), and frontotemporal dementia (FTD) subjects were processed through both MEMD and conventional Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD). A comprehensive set of temporal and spectral features was extracted from intrinsic mode functions (IMFs), and statistically significant features were used to train support vector machines (SVM), Random Forest, and XGBoost classifiers. Our results demonstrate that MEMD yields superior classification accuracy when compared to EMD, achieving the best results with XGBoost for AD vs. HC (0.83 vs. 0.71) and AD vs. FTD (0.76 vs. 0.70), and with SVM for FTD vs. HC (0.77 vs. 0.70). These findings highlight the potential of MEMD as a robust EEG processing tool for characterizing neural changes associated with neurodegenerative diseases.

recent_papers.title: Comparison of Wearable and Depth-Sensing Technologies with Electronic Walkway for Comprehensive Gait Analysis
recent_papers.abstract: Accurate and scalable gait assessment is essential for clinical and research applications, including fall risk evaluation, rehabilitation monitoring, and early detection of neurodegenerative diseases. While electronic walkways remain the clinical gold standard, their high cost and limited portability restrict widespread use. Wearable inertial measurement units (IMUs) and markerless depth cameras have emerged as promising alternatives; however, prior studies have typically assessed these systems under tightly controlled conditions, with single participants in view, limited marker sets, and without direct cross-technology comparisons. This study addresses these gaps by simultaneously evaluating three sensing technologies—APDM wearable IMUs (tested in two separate configurations: foot-mounted and lumbar-mounted) and the Azure Kinect depth camera—against ProtoKinetics Zeno™ Walkway Gait Analysis System in a realistic clinical environment where multiple individuals were present in the camera’s field of view. Gait data from 20 older adults (mean age 70.06±9.45 years) performing Single-Task and Dual-Task walking trials were synchronously captured using custom hardware for precise temporal alignment. Eleven gait markers spanning macro, micro-temporal, micro-spatial, and spatiotemporal domains were compared using mean absolute error (MAE), Pearson correlation (r), and Bland–Altman analysis. Foot-mounted IMUs demonstrated the highest accuracy (MAE =0.00–6.12, r=0.92–1.00), followed clo

recent_papers.title: Multi-channel Time-series Transformer for Wearable Monitoring of Rigidity in Parkinson’s Disease
recent_papers.abstract: This research targets a crucial unmet need in the remote digital monitoring of Parkinson’s Disease (PD) by introducing a novel Multi-Channel Time-Series (MCTS) Transformer to quantify rigidity severity from wearable sensor data. The model processes synchronized gyroscope and accelerometer data from wrist and ankle sensors, employing multiple convolutional layers and relative positional encoding to focus model attention on the complex spatiotemporal patterns of the rigidity signal. Supervised learning was conducted using leave-one-out cross-validation on data from 24 PD patients performing activities of daily living, with Movement Disorder Society-Unified Parkinson’s Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS) rigidity subscores annotated by neurologists. As wearable and IoT-enabled medical devices gain traction in clinical neurology, there is growing demand for AI models that can support high-resolution, continuous monitoring of PD symptoms outside of laboratory or hospital environments. The proposed transformer-based framework significantly outperforms conventional deep learning models, achieving superior correlation (r=0.78 vs. 0.67) and reduced error (MAE=1.82 vs. 2.04) between predicted and clinical rigidity scores. Attention pattern analyses revealed heightened attention and accurate predictions during periods of increased rigidity. These findings highlight the potential for AI-integrated wearable systems to provide neurologists with objective, longitudinal insights into symptom pr

recent_papers.title: QCSNN: A Memory-Efficient Spiking Neural Network for On-Device ECG-Based Arrhythmia Detection
recent_papers.abstract: This study introduces a novel Quantized Convolutional Spiking Neural Network (QCSNN) architecture for real-time arrhythmia detection from electrocardiogram (ECG) signals, designed for deployment on memory-constrained wearable healthcare devices. Unlike conventional models that rely on cloud-based processing or require substantial computational resources, our approach enables on-device inference through a two-stage, fully quantized spiking neural network trained with surrogate gradient descent. The proposed QCSNN classifies ECG signals into four clinically relevant categories—Normal, Supraventricular Ectopic Beat (SVEB), Ventricular Ectopic Beat (VEB), and Fusion (F)—achieving sensitivities of 88.97%, 83.51%, 96.4%, and 82.06%, respectively, and outperforming existing benchmarks while reducing memory usage by 76.2%. Trained with quantization-aware learning and leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) spiking neurons, this architecture enables accurate, energy-efficient arrhythmia detection within the power and memory limits of smartwatches and portable monitors. This work advances scalable edge artificial intelligence (AI) for digital health, supporting continuous, real-time cardiac monitoring and early intervention.

recent_papers.title: Transformer Neural Network for Estimating Tremor Severity in Parkinson’s Disease During Daily Living Activities
recent_papers.venue: Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society
recent_papers.abstract: This study addresses a critical gap in the digital monitoring of Parkinson’s Disease (PD) by introducing an innovative Transformer Neural Network with Multi-Head Self-Attention to predict tremor severity from raw gyroscope data captured by wearable sensors. The model incorporates a convolutional layer for spatio-temporal feature extraction, sinusoidal positional encoding to preserve sequence information, and self-attention mechanisms to capture both short- and long-term dependencies in the data. Supervised learning was conducted using leave-one-out cross-validation on a dataset of preprocessed signals from 24 PD patients performing activities of daily living, with tremor scores annotated by neurologists. The results show that the Transformer outperforms conventional recurrent deep learning approaches, achieving a lower error rate and a robust correlation (r=0.90 vs. 0.83) between predicted and actual tremor scores. Attention weight analyses further revealed the model’s ability to discern complex patterns in the input signals, providing enhanced interpretability compared to recurrent models. These findings demonstrate the significant potential of Transformer-based architectures for biomedical time-series signal monitoring, enabling impactful, timely interventions for improved outcomes.Clinical Relevance—Infrequent and unreliable evaluations have complicated the management of Parkinson’s Disease (PD). This study demonstrates how transformer neural networks process wearable sens

recent_papers.title: Self-supervised Contrastive Learning to Monitor Free-Body Movement Daily Activities of Parkinson’s Disease Patients Using a Single Wrist Sensor*
recent_papers.venue: Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society
recent_papers.abstract: In the evolving field of wearable sensors for human activity monitoring, our study introduces a new method to enhance deep model generalization beyond healthy populations in Parkinson’s disease (PD) patients. Utilizing a self-supervised contrastive learning methodology with a single wrist-mounted accelerometer sensor, we address the challenges posed by PD patients’ abnormal movements. Among 15 PD patients with 3.5 hours of recorded data, our method effectively recognized three unconstrained activities of daily living (ADL) profiles: Ambulation, Sitting, and Standing. The method achieved an average weighted F1-score of 84.85%, surpassing recent studies utilizing deep learning and wearable sensors to monitor individuals’ PD activities. The findings highlight the significance of our approach for reliable daily monitoring with a less invasive sensor setup. This advancement promises to aid clinicians in managing PD and personalizing physical therapy.Clinical relevance—Wearable sensor technologies, integrated with advanced deep learning methods, offer a valuable understanding of individuals’ PD activity levels. This information can assist clinicians in making informed decisions regarding personalized interventions, such as rehabilitation strategies, to enhance patients’ quality of life.
