pi_survey.research_philosophy: My work is driven by the belief that intelligent signal processing and machine learning can transform how we monitor and diagnose complex health conditions. Whether I'm developing a Multi-Channel Time-Series Transformer to quantify Parkinson's disease rigidity from wrist and ankle wearable sensors, or designing a quantized spiking neural network for on-device ECG arrhythmia detection, I aim to build systems that are clinically meaningful, computationally efficient, and deployable in real-world settings. I also care deeply about rigorous evaluation frameworks, as reflected in our multi-dimensional benchmarking of large language models for text summarization.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are genuinely curious about the intersection of biomedical challenges and computational methods, and who are comfortable working with both hardware-constrained systems and data-intensive models. A strong foundation in signal processing, machine learning, or deep learning is important, but so is the drive to ask clinically motivated questions—for example, understanding why early diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease and related dementias can extend life expectancy and dramatically reduce healthcare costs. I value students who can think rigorously across disciplines.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in my lab involves a mix of data analysis, model development, and collaborative discussion. Students might spend time preprocessing wearable sensor streams, tuning transformer architectures, or running comparative evaluations across multiple models and datasets, much like the work behind our 17-LLM summarization study spanning seven diverse datasets. We hold regular group meetings to review progress, troubleshoot experiments, and connect our technical findings back to the clinical or practical problem we're solving.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I mentor students by helping them develop both technical depth and the ability to communicate their findings clearly to interdisciplinary audiences. I encourage iterative experimentation and teach students to build robust evaluation pipelines rather than relying on a single metric—an approach that shaped our multi-dimensional framework assessing factual consistency, semantic similarity, and lexical overlap in summarization research. I try to meet each student where they are and gradually build their independence as researchers.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I do not currently have active external grants listed, so students should be prepared to discuss funding opportunities together, including potential teaching assistantships through Cal State LA's Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering and external fellowships relevant to their background. I am actively developing research directions in wearable health monitoring, neurological disease assessment, and on-device AI that are strong candidates for future grant submissions, and I welcome students who want to contribute to building those proposals.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Before reaching out, I encourage you to read recent work from my lab—particularly papers like our MCTS Transformer for Parkinson's rigidity monitoring and our QCSNN architecture for arrhythmia detection—and think about which problems genuinely excite you and why. Tell me specifically how your background connects to those directions rather than sending a generic inquiry. Strong applicants also come with some hands-on experience in Python, signal processing, or deep learning frameworks, and a clear sense of the kind of health or engineering problem they want to spend the next several years solving.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a collaborative, problem-solving vibe where people genuinely talk through each other's roadblocks — it's not competitive at all, which I appreciate. That said, because we're a smaller group at a teaching-focused institution, you're often the only person working on your exact problem, so you learn to be resourceful.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have weekly group meetings that tend to run about an hour, mixing progress updates with real troubleshooting sessions — Dr. Ghoraani is good at pulling the clinical 'so what' out of your technical results. One-on-ones happen more organically; if you need her, you can usually get on her calendar within a day or two.
student_survey_responses.independence: She'll give you a research direction and a strong evaluation framework, then step back and let you make choices about architecture and preprocessing — which I found empowering once I got comfortable but was honestly a bit disorienting in my first semester. The expectation is that you gradually take ownership, and she'll explicitly tell you when she thinks you're ready to lead a paper section.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known upfront that funding isn't guaranteed through an external grant, so I ended up TAing two semesters in a row to stay funded — it wasn't a dealbreaker but it slowed my research progress more than I expected. Come in with a fellowship application already drafted if you can.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who already have some Python and signal processing hands-on experience hit the ground running; she doesn't spend a lot of time on basics, and the projects — wearable sensor streams, transformer tuning, on-device model optimization — move fast. If you're the type who reads a paper and immediately wants to reproduce the experiments, you'll fit right in.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you're hoping for a lot of structured coursework-to-research scaffolding or a large cohort to learn alongside, this probably isn't the right fit — the group is small and the learning curve is steep. People who need frequent external validation before trying something new tend to stall out.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It feels like a working research group rather than a classroom extension — Dr. Ghoraani treats you like a junior colleague pretty quickly, which is motivating. The interdisciplinary angle is real; you end up reading clinical neurology papers one week and digging into quantized neural network architectures the next, and somehow that breadth becomes normal.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Group meetings happen every week and they're substantive — she'll push you to connect your benchmarking numbers back to what they'd actually mean for a clinician or a patient, which keeps the work grounded. Between meetings, communication is mostly async over email; she responds quickly but doesn't micromanage your daily schedule.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a noticeable arc where in your first year she's more hands-on about experimental design and evaluation pipelines, then by year two she's really asking you to defend your own methodological choices. I think some students find that transition abrupt, but for me it was the most valuable part of the mentorship.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The projects have a hardware-meets-software complexity that I underestimated — when you're working on something like on-device ECG inference, you can't just optimize for model accuracy without thinking about memory and power constraints simultaneously. I would've taken an embedded systems course before starting if I'd known.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who are genuinely driven by a health problem — not just by a cool algorithm — do really well here, because Dr. Ghoraani will keep steering you back to clinical relevance and you need to find that energizing rather than annoying. Strong self-starters who can read literature and generate their own next-step hypotheses also tend to publish early.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you're expecting a fully funded RA from day one with a clearly scoped dissertation project handed to you, you might be disappointed — the lab is still building out its grant portfolio, so there's some uncertainty about funding continuity. Students who aren't proactive about seeking out fellowships or having frank money conversations with her can end up feeling financially anxious mid-degree.

recruiting_status.rationale: lab page exists but no funding, publication, or recruitment signals

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My work centers on semantic computing and its real-world applications — from drug discovery to medical data management and mobile software. I believe that bridging formal knowledge representation with practical systems is where the most meaningful contributions happen, and my over 50 published papers in data and knowledge engineering reflect that conviction.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are curious about how machines can understand and reason over complex, real-world data — particularly in domains like healthcare informatics or software engineering. A solid foundation in computer science fundamentals, paired with genuine enthusiasm for applied research, goes a long way in my group.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: Students in my group typically divide their time between reading current literature in semantic computing or knowledge engineering, working on ongoing research projects, and attending check-ins with me to discuss progress and next steps. Given my involvement in semantic computing and ubiquitous computing conferences, students also get exposure to preparing and refining work for publication.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to guide students toward becoming independent researchers by connecting their projects to concrete application domains — whether that is medical data management or software learning tools — so they can see the tangible impact of their work. I maintain an open-door policy and encourage students to bring problems to me early rather than struggling alone.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I am transparent with prospective students that funding situations can vary, and I encourage applicants to explore all available university fellowships and assistantships at CSUN alongside any opportunities that may arise from collaborative projects. I would discuss the current funding landscape candidly with any serious candidate.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: If you are interested in semantic computing, knowledge engineering, or their applications in areas like drug discovery or healthcare, reach out and tell me specifically what draws you to these problems — a targeted, thoughtful email goes much further than a generic inquiry. Familiarity with data mining or software engineering concepts, which I also teach, is a definite plus.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a pretty small, close-knit feel — there aren't twenty students competing for attention, which means you actually get real face time with Dr. Wang. The flip side is that there's not much peer infrastructure, so you're not going to have a senior PhD student holding your hand through your first paper.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We do weekly one-on-ones and they're genuinely substantive — he comes prepared and expects you to have made some forward progress, even if it's just a new set of readings or a rough experiment. If you hit a wall mid-week, the open-door thing is real; I've knocked on his office door with half-baked questions and he'll actually sit down with you.
student_survey_responses.independence: He pushes you toward independence faster than I expected — early on I wanted more direction on exactly what to build, but he tends to frame things as 'here's the domain problem, now figure out the right approach.' It grows on you, but the first semester can feel a bit unmoored.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known going in that the healthcare and drug-discovery application angle is pretty central to how he frames almost everything — I came in thinking mostly about software systems and had to recalibrate toward more domain-specific knowledge representation work pretty quickly.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who already have some genuine curiosity about ontologies or knowledge graphs and can self-motivate between check-ins do really well here — if you need an external deadline every week to function, this setup will be rough.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Funding is genuinely uncertain and he's upfront about it, but that ambiguity is stressful if you don't have a fellowship lined up; I'd strongly recommend sorting out your financial situation before you commit.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a quieter, more academic atmosphere than the big research-university labs I've heard about from friends elsewhere — people are focused, there's no culture of staying until midnight to perform productivity, but there's also not a lot of spontaneous collaboration happening in the hallways. Dr. Wang sets a collegial tone and isn't at all intimidating to approach.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Meetings are weekly and fairly structured; he likes to see where the literature review sits alongside any implementation work, so you want to have something concrete to show on both fronts. Occasionally a week slips to async if there's a conference deadline on his end, but it's never felt neglectful.
student_survey_responses.independence: He connects your project to a real application domain pretty early — mine is around medical data management — which helps you feel like the work matters, but it also means he's not going to micromanage the technical choices. You own your methodology, which is great once you have confidence and intimidating before you do.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I underestimated how much conference prep would be part of the rhythm here — he's active in the semantic computing and ubiquitous computing conference circuits, so there are stretches where paper polishing dominates everything else and your other work just has to wait.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Someone with a solid CS fundamentals background who is genuinely excited about applied knowledge engineering — not just NLP hype — will find this lab a good fit; he has low tolerance for shallow enthusiasm and can tell the difference fast.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came expecting a large collaborative group with lots of co-authorship opportunities with peers, you'll be disappointed — the lab is small enough that most of the intellectual back-and-forth happens with Dr. Wang directly rather than among students.

pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I work in a department that is deeply committed to making science more diverse, inclusive, and equitable, and those values shape how I mentor — I strive to create a lab environment where students from all backgrounds feel genuinely welcomed and supported. I believe that good mentorship means recognizing and addressing the historical barriers that have kept marginalized voices out of science.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab genuinely feels like a place where you can show up as your whole self — Cherilyn is intentional about that in a way that doesn't feel performative. We talk openly about barriers in academia, and students from underrepresented backgrounds seem to exhale a little when they realize that's a real part of the culture, not just a line on a website.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have weekly one-on-ones that run about 30–45 minutes, plus a lab meeting every other week where someone presents data or we do a paper discussion. It's a reasonable cadence, though occasionally the individual meetings get rescheduled when departmental stuff piles up.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to take ownership of your project pretty early — she'll point you toward resources and ask probing questions, but she's not going to hand you a protocol and say 'do this.' That works well for me now, but the first semester I kept wishing for more explicit direction.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known that being at a teaching-focused CSU means Cherilyn carries a heavy course load, so her availability can be uneven during the semester. Building in extra lead time for feedback on drafts or proposals is just a survival skill here.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are self-motivated, comfortable asking for help when they genuinely need it, and who care about the broader 'why' behind their work tend to do really well. If you're also interested in mentoring undergrads yourself, there's a lot of opportunity for that.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need very structured, step-by-step supervision or frequent reassurance, you might find the independence a bit unsettling. The lab is also relatively small, so if you thrive on a big bustling cohort energy, it can sometimes feel a little quiet.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Honestly the DEI emphasis is baked into how we actually operate, not just how we describe ourselves — things like whose papers we read in lab meeting, how we talk about study populations, who gets credit for ideas. It's refreshing but also means you have to be willing to reflect on your own assumptions, which can be uncomfortable at first.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: She meets with me individually roughly every two weeks, which I sometimes wish were more frequent when I'm stuck on something technical. Lab meetings happen monthly-ish and are pretty collaborative — people give real feedback, not just polite nods.
student_survey_responses.independence: The expectation is that you come to meetings with updates and questions already organized, not waiting for her to diagnose your project. I've grown a lot from that, but early on I definitely spun my wheels longer than I needed to because I didn't want to 'bother' her.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The master's program here is two years on paper but most people take closer to three if they want to do the project justice — I wish someone had leveled with me about that timeline upfront so I'd planned my finances accordingly.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who genuinely enjoy connecting their science to questions of equity or community impact fit right in. There's also an informal culture of students helping each other troubleshoot, so being collaborative rather than competitive makes life a lot easier.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Grant and fellowship funding can be harder to navigate at a CSU than at an R1, so if you're counting on abundant external funding opportunities being handed to you, you'll need to be proactive about hunting those down yourself. It's doable, but nobody does it for you.

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a pretty collaborative vibe — people share code and talk through problems across projects, which I didn't expect coming in. It's not a cutthroat environment, but it's also not a hand-holding one; you're expected to show up ready to contribute.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We do a weekly group meeting where someone presents work-in-progress, and Daniel meets with each of us one-on-one roughly every two weeks, sometimes more if something is pressing. If you need more face time you have to ask for it explicitly — he's responsive but not proactive about scheduling extra check-ins.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a lot of independence here, which I both appreciate and find stressful some weeks. Daniel will point you toward an interesting problem and then largely let you figure out the approach, stepping back in when you're stuck or when a deadline is looming.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much self-directed reading and problem-scoping the early years would involve — I spent a few months feeling like I wasn't 'doing research' because I wasn't producing results yet. That ambiguity is just part of the process here, but it caught me off guard.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are genuinely curious, comfortable sitting with open-ended questions, and can manage their own time tend to do really well. If you like having a clear roadmap handed to you, this might not be the best fit.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need frequent external validation or very structured feedback cycles can find it tough. Feedback on writing especially can feel sparse during the middle stages of a project, so you have to be proactive about seeking it out.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Honestly the lab culture is one of the better parts of my PhD so far — there's a mix of senior and junior students and the seniors are genuinely willing to debug things with you or just talk through whether your framing of a problem makes sense. Group meetings can run long but they're usually worth it.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: One-on-ones happen but the cadence shifts depending on where you are in a project — when I was writing my first paper we talked almost every week, and in slower stretches it stretched to once a month. I'd say plan on biweekly as a baseline and adjust from there.
student_survey_responses.independence: You own your research direction to a pretty significant degree, which is exciting but also means you can spin your wheels for a while without anyone necessarily noticing. Daniel trusts you to raise your hand if you're stuck, so learning to do that early is important.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd understood sooner that 'this might be interesting to explore' from Daniel is not the same as 'I think this is a good thesis direction' — learning to read those signals took me longer than it should have.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who read broadly, have strong opinions about their problems, and can take high-level feedback and run with it tend to get a lot out of working here. There's real intellectual freedom if you're willing to take it.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came from an undergrad or master's environment where advisors checked in constantly or co-wrote things with you, the transition here can feel abrupt. The expectation is that you're driving the bus, and that can be isolating if you're not ready for it.

name: George Montañez

recruiting_status.rationale: active lab signals present (funding or publications) but no explicit recruitment statement

recent_papers.title: The Stepwise Informativeness Assumption: Why are Entropy Dynamics and Reasoning Correlated in LLMs?
recent_papers.abstract: Recent work uses entropy-based signals at multiple representation levels to study reasoning in large language models, but the field remains largely empirical. A central unresolved puzzle is why internal entropy dynamics, defined under the predictive distribution of a model, correlate so robustly with external correctness given by the ground-truth answer. In this paper, we argue that this correlation arises because autoregressive models reason correctly when they accumulate information about the true answer via answer-informative prefixes. We formalize this intuition via the Stepwise Informativeness Assumption (SIA), which states that reasoning prefixes accumulate answer-relevant information in expectation as generation progresses. We show that SIA naturally emerges from maximum-likelihood optimization on human reasoning traces and is reinforced by standard fine-tuning and reinforcement-learning pipelines. We then derive observable signatures of SIA linking conditional answer entropy dynamics to correctness. We empirically test SIA across multiple reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K, ARC, SVAMP) and a diverse set of open-weight LLMs (Gemma-2, LLaMA-3.2, Qwen-2.5, DeepSeek and Olmo variants), showing that training induces it and that correct traces exhibit characteristic conditional answer entropy patterns.

recent_papers.title: Model Characterization with Inductive Orientation Vectors
recent_papers.venue: International Conference on Agents and Artificial Intelligence
recent_papers.abstract: : As models rise in complexity, black-box evaluation and interpretation methods become critical. We introduce estimation methods for characterizing model-theoretic quantities such as algorithm flexibility, responsiveness to changes in training data, and ability to specialize. These methods are applicable to any black-box classification algorithm. Past theoretical work has shown how such qualities affect probability of task success, generalization, and tendency to overfit. We perform metric estimations of interpretable models across hyperparameters and corroborate the metrics’ behavior with known algorithm heuristics. This work presents a general model-agnostic interpretability tool.

recent_papers.title: Probabilistic Error Guarantees for Abductive Inference
recent_papers.venue: 2024 IEEE International Conference on Future Machine Learning and Data Science (FMLDS)
recent_papers.abstract: Abductive reasoning is ubiquitous in artificial intelligence and everyday thinking. However, formal theories that provide probabilistic guarantees for abductive inference are lacking. We present a quantitative formalization of abductive logic that combines Bayesian probability with the interpretation of abduction as a search process within the Algorithmic Search Framework (ASF). By incorporating uncertainty in background knowledge, we establish two novel sets of probabilistic bounds on the success of abduction when (1) selecting the single most likely cause while assuming noiseless observations, and (2) selecting any cause above some probability threshold while accounting for noisy observations. To our knowledge, no existing abductive or general inference bounds account for noisy observations. Furthermore, while most existing abductive frameworks assume exact underlying prior and likelihood distributions, we assume only percentile-based confidence intervals for such values. These milder assumptions result in greater flexibility and applicability of our framework. We also explore additional information-theoretic results from the ASF and provide mathematical justifications for everyday abductive intuitions.

recent_papers.title: From Targets to Rewards: Continuous Target Sets in the Algorithmic Search Framework
recent_papers.venue: International Conference on Agents and Artificial Intelligence

recent_papers.title: Finite-Sample Bounds for Two-Distribution Hypothesis Tests
recent_papers.venue: International Conference on Data Science and Advanced Analytics
recent_papers.abstract: With the rapid growth of large language models, big data, and malicious online attacks, it has become increasingly important to have tools for anomaly detection that can distinguish machine from human, fair from unfair, and dangerous from safe. Prior work has shown that two-distribution (specified complexity) hypothesis tests are useful tools for such tasks, aiding in detecting bias in datasets and providing artificial agents with the ability to recognize artifacts that are likely to have been designed by humans and pose a threat. However, existing work on two-distribution hypothesis tests requires exact values for the specification function, which can often be costly or impossible to compute. In this work, we prove novel finite-sample bounds that allow for two-distribution hypothesis tests with only estimates of required quantities, such as specification function values. Significantly, the resulting bounds do not require knowledge of the true distribution, distinguishing them from traditional p-values. We apply our bounds to detect student cheating on multiple-choice tests, as an example where the exact specification function is unknown. We additionally apply our results to detect representational bias in machine-learning datasets and provide artificial agents with intention perception, showing that our results are consistent with prior work despite only requiring a finite sample of the space. Finally, we discuss additional applications and provide guidance for those applyin

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My core mission is understanding *why* machine learning works, not just demonstrating that it does — a question I've been pursuing since my PhD at Carnegie Mellon under Cosma Shalizi. I approach this through the Algorithmic Search Framework, information theory, and formal probabilistic reasoning, as reflected in papers like 'Probabilistic Error Guarantees for Abductive Inference' and 'From Targets to Rewards: Continuous Target Sets in the Algorithmic Search Framework.' I believe science advances by ruthlessly stress-testing our best theories, and I try to bring that same rigor to empirical phenomena like the entropy-correctness correlations we explore in 'The Stepwise Informativeness Assumption.'
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are genuinely curious about foundational questions and aren't satisfied with purely empirical answers — people who want to know *why* something works, not just that it does. Strong mathematical maturity matters, especially comfort with probability, information theory, and logic, since our work spans formal guarantees like finite-sample bounds and black-box model characterization. I also deeply value intellectual honesty and the willingness to challenge prevailing assumptions rather than simply follow the crowd.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: Students in the AMISTAD Lab typically split their time between working through theoretical frameworks, running experiments to test those frameworks, and meeting with me or the broader group to discuss progress and new directions. Given that we work on projects ranging from hypothesis testing to LLM interpretability, a student might spend part of a week deriving probabilistic bounds and another part implementing and evaluating estimation methods for model characterization. Weekly lab meetings are a cornerstone — we push each other hard on rigor and clarity.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I direct a large group of research students and try to give each person enough autonomy to develop their own voice while keeping them anchored to problems with genuine theoretical depth. I see mentorship as collaborative: I'll push you to formalize your intuitions and defend your claims quantitatively, but I also want to understand what excites you and help shape projects around that. Harvey Mudd's undergraduate-focused culture means students often get involved in publishable research early, and I take that responsibility seriously.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I currently have no externally funded grants to report, which is worth being transparent about. Research in the AMISTAD Lab is supported through Harvey Mudd's institutional structures and student research programs, and I pursue projects that can be advanced with motivated students and computational resources available at the college level. Prospective students should be aware of this context and discuss funding arrangements directly with me.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read the papers coming out of the AMISTAD Lab — particularly work like 'Finite-Sample Bounds for Two-Distribution Hypothesis Tests' or 'Model Characterization with Inductive Orientation Vectors' — and ask yourself whether the foundational questions driving them genuinely excite you. Don't reach out with a generic email; tell me specifically what problem you want to understand and why you think a theoretical, information-theoretic lens is the right one. If you love both mathematical rigor and big 'why does this work?' questions, we'll have a lot to talk about.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a pretty intense intellectual culture — weekly meetings feel less like check-ins and more like informal defenses where you're expected to justify every assumption you made. That said, it's not hostile; people genuinely want to understand your work, and George models the kind of rigorous questioning he asks of us.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have a weekly full lab meeting that runs long if something interesting comes up, and George is generally available for one-on-ones when you need to hash something out. I wouldn't call it hand-holdy — you're expected to show up with something concrete to discuss.
student_survey_responses.independence: He gives you real autonomy, which I appreciate but also found disorienting at first. You're not handed a pre-baked project; you're expected to own the direction, and he'll push back hard if your framing is sloppy or your math is hand-wavy.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much of the work is genuinely open-ended in a way that can feel like floating in the dark before it starts to crystallize. Also, because there's no external grant structure, you should go in with eyes open about what funding your degree program provides — it varies.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who already have a taste for foundational 'why' questions and can sit with theoretical ambiguity for weeks before things click tend to do really well. If you're the kind of person who gets energized by deriving a bound from first principles rather than just tuning a model, you'll fit in.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who want a clear pipeline — here's the dataset, here's the method, here's the benchmark — tend to get frustrated because that's not what we do. And if you're not comfortable being pushed to formalize intuitions you can't yet fully articulate, the meetings can feel pretty uncomfortable.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's collaborative in the sense that everyone is working on related threads in the Algorithmic Search Framework, so conversations across projects are genuinely useful rather than just performative. But it's also a place where vague hand-waving gets called out pretty quickly, which took some getting used to.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Lab meetings are weekly and non-negotiable — George takes them seriously and so does everyone else. Between those, contact with him varies a lot depending on where you are in a project; when I was stuck on bounding something, he'd engage daily, but during writing phases I'd go a week or two mostly on my own.
student_survey_responses.independence: More independence than I expected at a school with this size and an undergrad-focused culture. George seems to actively want you to develop your own perspective on the problems, not just execute his vision, which is exciting but also means you have to be self-directed.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: Honestly, I wish I'd brushed up on information theory more seriously before starting — the papers reference concepts like algorithmic mutual information in ways that assume fluency, not just familiarity. Also worth knowing that the lab's work is quite theoretical, so if you're hoping to publish flashy empirical results fast, that's probably not the environment.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who find mathematical rigor genuinely satisfying rather than just instrumentally useful seem to thrive. There's also something to be said for intellectual honesty — George visibly respects students who push back on his framing if they have a real reason, and that creates a good dynamic.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a lot of structured milestones and external validation to stay motivated, the open-ended nature of the research can be draining. The lack of external grants also means you're thinking carefully about how your degree funding is structured from day one, which adds a practical stress layer on top of the research itself.

name: James Boerkoel

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

recent_papers.title: Sensitivity Analysis for Dynamic Control of PSTNs with Skewed Distributions
recent_papers.venue: International Conference on Automated Planning and Scheduling
recent_papers.abstract: Probabilistic Simple Temporal Networks (PSTN) facilitate solving many interesting scheduling problems by characterizing uncertain task durations with unbounded probabilistic distributions. However, most current approaches assess PSTN performance using normal or uniform distributions of temporal uncertainty. This paper explores how well such approaches extend to families of non-symmetric distributions shown to better represent the temporal uncertainty introduced by, e.g., human teammates by building new PSTN benchmarks. We also build probability-aware variations of current approaches that are more reactive to the shape of the underlying distributions. We empirically evaluate the original and modified approaches over well-established PSTN datasets. Our results demonstrate that alignment between the planning model and reality significantly impacts performance. While our ideas for augmenting existing algorithms to better account for human-style uncertainty yield only marginal gains, our results surprisingly demonstrate that existing methods handle positively-skewed temporal uncertainty better.

recent_papers.title: An Undergraduate Consortium for Addressing the Leaky Pipeline to Computing Research
recent_papers.venue: Technical Symposium on Computer Science Education
recent_papers.abstract: Despite an increasing number of successful interventions designed to broaden participation in computing research, there is still significant attrition among historically marginalized groups in the computing research pipeline. This experience report describes a first-of-its-kind Undergraduate Consortium (UC; https://aaai-uc.github.io/about) that addresses this challenge by empowering students with a culmination of their undergraduate research in a conference setting. The UC, conducted at the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence (AAAI), aims to broaden participation in the AI research community by recruiting students, particularly those from historically marginalized groups, supporting them with mentorship, advising, and networking as an accelerator toward graduate school, AI research, and their scientific identity. This paper presents our program design, inspired by a rich set of evidence-based practices, and a preliminary evaluation of the first years that points to the UC achieving many of its desired outcomes. We conclude by discussing insights to improve our program and expand to other computing communities.

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My work sits at the intersection of scheduling, uncertainty, and real-world applicability — I want the algorithms we build to actually hold up when conditions aren't clean or symmetric, as we explored in 'Sensitivity Analysis for Dynamic Control of PSTNs with Skewed Distributions.' I believe rigorous theoretical foundations and practical relevance aren't at odds; they push each other forward.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I'm drawn to students who are curious about problems that don't have tidy solutions — people comfortable with uncertainty, both mathematically and intellectually. A background in algorithms, probability, or AI planning is helpful, but intellectual tenacity and the ability to ask good questions matter just as much.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: Students in my group can expect a mix of reading and discussing recent literature, working through formal problem formulations, and implementing or evaluating solutions. We meet regularly to troubleshoot ideas and keep momentum going, with time carved out for writing since communicating research clearly is a core skill.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I care deeply about broadening who gets to participate in computing research — my involvement in the AAAI Undergraduate Consortium reflects that commitment to supporting students from historically marginalized groups through the pipeline. I try to mentor the whole student, not just the project, paying attention to confidence-building and long-term career development.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I don't have active external grants to report at this time, so prospective students should have a candid conversation with me about funding availability and potential support mechanisms such as teaching assistantships before applying.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read the papers coming out of my group — particularly work on probabilistic temporal networks — and think about what questions they leave open for you. A strong application letter will show me you've engaged with the ideas seriously and can articulate why your background positions you to push them further.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab feels like a small seminar group more than a factory — we actually read each other's drafts and argue about problem formulations in a way that makes the work sharper. It's collaborative without being cliquey, though the group is small enough that you really feel it when someone is having an off semester.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have regular one-on-ones that James takes seriously — he's not the type to cancel on you repeatedly or show up unprepared. There are also occasional group check-ins where everyone shares what they're stuck on, which I've found more useful than I expected.
student_survey_responses.independence: He gives you a lot of rope, which is both good and occasionally terrifying. If you need hand-holding on every decision you'll feel adrift, but if you have a thread you want to pull he'll let you run with it and then push back thoughtfully when you resurface.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: Funding is genuinely a conversation you need to have upfront — don't assume a TA line is guaranteed or that it covers everything you're planning. I wish I had pushed harder on those logistics before I committed.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who are genuinely comfortable sitting with an unsolved problem for weeks without panicking tend to do well — the research is in probabilistic temporal scheduling, so if you find uncertainty interesting rather than frustrating you'll fit right in.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a lot of external structure or a big lab community to feel energized, the smaller group size can feel isolating. It's not a place where you'll accidentally absorb momentum from twenty labmates down the hall.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: There's a real emphasis on writing and communication that I didn't fully appreciate until I started — James will push you to articulate your ideas clearly early and often, not just when a deadline is approaching. It can feel like extra work at first but it's made me a noticeably better researcher.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Meetings happen consistently enough that I never feel like I've lost the thread for too long. He's good at asking the one question that reframes something you've been spinning your wheels on, which makes the one-on-ones worth preparing for.
student_survey_responses.independence: Expect to own your research direction more than you might in a larger, more hierarchical lab — James will help you refine questions but he's not going to hand you a problem and a roadmap. That suits me, but I've seen it frustrate people who came in wanting more scaffolding.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The work on probabilistic temporal networks has a pretty steep learning curve if you're coming from a pure ML background — I spent my first semester doing a lot of foundational reading in scheduling theory that I hadn't anticipated. Coming in with some prior exposure would have saved me real time.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are intellectually honest about what they don't know and genuinely curious about algorithmic rigor tend to click with James's style. He responds well to people who ask specific questions rather than vague ones.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: The lack of a large external grant structure means there's less of a built-in conference travel budget or research fund than in some other labs, so you may have to be creative about finding supplemental support. It's manageable but worth knowing going in.

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My work sits at the intersection of computer science and biology — I develop computational and mathematical models to reconstruct gene histories across multiple species, with an emphasis on evolutionary genomics. I believe that bringing together machine learning, optimization, statistics, and biology allows us to ask and answer questions about evolution that neither field could tackle alone. My interdisciplinary background, from electrical engineering at Rice to computational biology at MIT with Manolis Kellis, shapes how I approach every research problem.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are genuinely curious about both computation and biology and who aren't afraid to engage with problems that require building real mathematical and algorithmic foundations. Strong quantitative skills — whether from computer science, statistics, or engineering — are essential, and an enthusiasm for understanding evolutionary and genomic questions is a big plus. Students who thrive in my group are comfortable working across disciplinary boundaries and motivated to build tools that the broader scientific community can use.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in my lab involves a mix of algorithmic development, reading and discussing the computational biology literature, and writing or refining code for our software tools. Students regularly meet with me one-on-one to talk through research progress and challenges, and we discuss both the biological intuition and the formal underpinnings of our methods. Because I am at a primarily undergraduate institution, students also have meaningful opportunities to engage with and mentor undergraduates working on related problems.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to tailor my mentorship to where each student is in their development — early on, I work closely with students to help them build the technical foundations they need, and I gradually encourage more independence as they grow into their own research voice. I received an NSF CAREER award that supports both my teaching and my research, which reflects my belief that the two are deeply intertwined. I want every student who works with me to leave with not just results, but a genuine ability to think rigorously and communicate their science clearly.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I have received an NSF CAREER award in support of my research and teaching program, which has been a cornerstone of our group's activities. Students interested in joining my lab should reach out to discuss the current funding situation directly, as grant cycles evolve over time. I am committed to supporting student research and will work with prospective students to identify the best path forward.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: If you are excited about computational biology and evolutionary genomics, I encourage you to look at the kinds of methods and questions my group works on — phylogenetics, comparative genomics, and the algorithmic challenges of reconstructing gene histories across species. Reach out with a clear sense of why this intersection of computer science and biology excites you, and don't worry if your background is more on the engineering or CS side than the biology side. A strong quantitative foundation and genuine intellectual curiosity are what matter most.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a genuinely collaborative feel even though it's small — people share code, talk through proofs together, and nobody hoards their methods. Being at an undergrad institution means the energy shifts a lot depending on the semester, since undergrads rotate through and you end up co-mentoring whether you planned to or not.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Jessica meets with me one-on-one weekly, and she comes prepared — she'll have read whatever I sent her and usually has specific questions about the math or the biological framing. There's no hand-wavy 'looks good, keep going'; she wants to see the derivations.
student_survey_responses.independence: Early on I felt pretty guided, which was helpful because the phylogenetics literature has a steep learning curve. Around year two she started stepping back and letting me drive the direction more, but she's still very reachable when I get stuck — it's a gradual ramp, not a cliff.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much biology I'd actually need to absorb — I came in strong on the CS and optimization side and assumed the bio would stay surface-level, but the research questions really do require you to care about what a gene duplication means biologically, not just algorithmically. Building that intuition takes real time.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you genuinely enjoy building tools that other researchers will use, this is a great fit — a lot of what we do ends up as publicly released software, and Jessica cares about it being usable and well-documented, not just technically correct. People who find satisfaction in that kind of rigorous, artifact-producing work do really well.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who want a large, buzzy lab environment with lots of senior PhD students to bounce ideas off will probably find it quieter than expected. The tradeoff is direct faculty access, but the peer mentorship network is thinner than at a big R1.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a focused, quiet-ish group where the expectation is that you're serious about both the math and the biology — casual, yes, but not relaxed in the sense of 'do whatever.' Jessica sets a high bar for rigor and it kind of becomes the lab norm pretty naturally.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We do weekly one-on-ones and I've found them dense in the best way — an hour goes fast because she's engaged the whole time and will push back on anything that feels hand-wavy. There's no standing meeting where everyone just reports status; it's more individualized than that.
student_survey_responses.independence: She gives you real ownership of your project, but she's not hands-off to the point of leaving you adrift. I'd say the independence feels earned rather than thrown at you — she scaffolded the early months pretty tightly and then gradually trusted me to make design decisions on my own.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't fully appreciate before joining that the audience for our work is both CS venues and biology journals, which means you're always writing and presenting for two different crowds. Learning to communicate the same result to a bioinformatics conference and to evolutionary biologists is a skill I had to build from scratch.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People with an engineering or strong quantitative background who are genuinely curious about why genomes evolve the way they do tend to click here quickly. You don't need to arrive knowing phylogenetics, but you need to find it interesting once you start reading about it.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you're someone who needs a lot of ambient social energy — lab lunches every day, a big cohort of grad students to decompress with — it can feel a bit isolated since the group is small. You have to be somewhat self-sufficient in building your own peer network outside the lab.

name: Daniel Stoebel

recruiting_status.rationale: active lab signals present (funding or publications) but no explicit recruitment statement

recent_papers.title: A Long-tailed Jaeger in Yolo County
recent_papers.abstract: This is the first record of Long-tailed Jaeger for Yolo County, the seventh for the Central Valley, and differs from other Central Valley records. This paper will discuss these records.

recent_papers.title: Human body temperature cues widespread changes in virulence gene expression in uropathogenic Escherichia coli
recent_papers.abstract: ABSTRACT As a bacterial pathogen enters a human host, it immediately encounters a temperature upshift to 37°C. Mimicking the early hours of infection, we analyzed the transcriptome and proteome of uropathogenic Escherichia coli CFT073 initially grown at 23°C, then shifted to 37°C for 4 h. Temperature caused a change in mRNA expression for 9% of the genome (1% false discovery rate, ≥2-fold); similar impacts were observed for the proteome with a good concordance amongst the most highly temperature-regulated genes. Comparison to E. coli K-12 MC4100 shows temperature to be a more broadly used regulatory cue in the uropathogen. Multiple operons associated with fimbrial adhesion, biofilm formation, immune evasion, and competitor defense show temperature regulation. Multiple fimbrial adhesins (pap, pap-2, foc) are increased in expression at 37°C, while others (ecp) are favored at 23°C. Decreased motility gene expression at 37°C and 23°C is correlated with the thermoregulation of multiple motility repressors (papX, focX, pdeL, and rpoS). Several biofilm formation and c-di-GMP signaling genes showed preferential expression at 37°C, suggesting human body temperature modulates this process. Growth at 37°C promotes a broad set of immune evasion genes (complement evasion, antimicrobial peptide cleavage, phagocyte killing/iron acquisition, copper export) along with genes associated with competitor bacterial and phage defense. recent_papers.title: The transcriptional response to low temperature is weakly conserved across the Enterobacteriaceae
recent_papers.abstract: ABSTRACT Bacteria respond to changes in their external environment, such as temperature, by changing the transcription of their genes. We know little about how these regulatory patterns evolve. We used RNA-seq to study the transcriptional response to a shift from 37°C to 15°C in wild-type Escherichia coli, Salmonella enterica, Citrobacter rodentium, Enterobacter cloacae, Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Serratia marcescens, as well as ∆rpoS strains of E. coli and S. enterica. We found that these species change the transcription of between 626 and 1057 genes in response to the temperature shift, but there were only 16 differentially expressed genes in common among the six species. Species-specific transcriptional patterns of shared genes were a prominent cause of this lack of conservation. Gene ontology enrichment of regulated genes suggested many species-specific phenotypic responses to temperature changes, but enriched terms associated with iron metabolism, central metabolism, and biofilm formation were implicated in at least half of the species. The alternative sigma factor RpoS regulated about 200 genes between 37°C and 15°C in both E. coli and S. enterica, with only 83 genes in common between the two species. Overall, there was limited conservation of the response to low temperature generally, or the RpoS-regulated part of the response specifically. This study suggests that species-specific patterns of transcription of shared genes, rather than horizontal acquisition of unique

recent_papers.title: The timing of transcription of RpoS-dependent genes varies across multiple stresses in Escherichia coli K-12
recent_papers.abstract: ABSTRACT The alternative sigma factor RpoS regulates transcription of over 1,000 genes in Escherichia coli in response to many different stresses. RpoS levels rise continuously after exposure to stress, and the consequences of changing levels of RpoS for the temporal patterns of expression of RpoS-regulated genes have not been described. We measured RpoS levels at various times during the entry to stationary phase, or in response to high osmolarity or low temperature, and found that the time required to reach maximum levels varied by several hours. We quantified the transcriptome across these stresses using RNA-seq. The number of differentially expressed genes differed among stresses, with 1,379 DE genes identified in stationary phase, 633 in high osmolarity, and 302 in cold shock. To quantify the timing of gene expression, we fit sigmoid or double sigmoid models to differentially expressed genes in each stress. During the entry into stationary phase, genes whose expression rose earlier tended to be those that had been found to respond most strongly to low levels of RpoS. The timing of individual gene’s expression was not correlated across stresses. Taken together, our results demonstrate E. coli activates RpoS with different timing in response to different stresses, which in turn generates a unique pattern of timing of the transcription response to each stress. IMPORTANCE Bacteria adapt to changing environments by altering the transcription of their genes. Specific proteins
