pi_survey.research_philosophy: I'm driven by fundamental questions about how bacteria sense and respond to their environment, particularly how gene regulation evolves and adapts. My work—ranging from studying how temperature shifts trigger virulence gene expression in uropathogenic E. coli to mapping the poorly conserved transcriptional responses to cold across the Enterobacteriaceae—reflects my belief that integrating genomics, molecular biology, and evolutionary thinking gives us the deepest understanding of bacterial physiology.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are genuinely curious and comfortable sitting with open-ended questions, since much of what we do involves exploratory transcriptomic and proteomic analyses where the answer isn't predetermined. I also value careful, quantitative thinkers who can critically evaluate large datasets, as projects like our RNA-seq comparisons across multiple Enterobacteriaceae species require both statistical rigor and biological intuition.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in my lab involves a mix of bench work—RNA extractions, growth curves, reporter assays—and computational analysis of sequencing data, depending on where a project stands. Students also spend time reading primary literature and meeting with me individually to discuss results and plan next experiments; our work on RpoS-regulated gene timing, for instance, required close iteration between experiment and interpretation.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to give students enough independence to develop their own scientific voice while staying closely engaged so they don't spin their wheels unnecessarily. I see my role as helping students learn to ask the right questions, and I make a point of working through experimental design and data interpretation together rather than just handing down protocols.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I currently do not have active external grant funding, which is something I'm working to address. Prospective students should have an honest conversation with me about the lab's financial situation and what support options are available before committing.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read a couple of our recent papers—particularly the work on temperature-dependent virulence gene expression or the comparative transcriptomics across Enterobacteriaceae—and come prepared to tell me what question you'd want to ask next. I'm also happy to talk with applicants who share broader biological interests, even something as different as my natural history work on bird records; intellectual range matters to me.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small lab, which means you actually talk to your PI regularly instead of being a number. The vibe is collaborative and intellectually honest — nobody pretends an experiment worked when it didn't.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: I meet with Dan one-on-one roughly every week or two, and those meetings are genuinely useful — we work through data together rather than him just asking for a progress update. If something urgent comes up mid-week, he's pretty reachable by email.
student_survey_responses.independence: He gives you real ownership of your project, which I appreciated once I got my footing, but early on I sometimes wished he'd been a bit more directive about which direction to prioritize. The expectation is that you develop your own scientific instincts, and he'll help you refine them — not hand them to you.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known upfront how much computational work this lab actually involves. If you're not comfortable with RNA-seq pipelines and R going in, plan to spend serious time building those skills in your first year. It's learnable, but it caught me off guard.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who genuinely like sitting with ambiguous data and poking at it from multiple angles do really well here. If you find a weird result exciting rather than annoying, you'll fit in.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a lot of external structure or a clear step-by-step roadmap handed to you, the open-ended nature of the projects can feel disorienting. Also, the funding situation is real — have that conversation before you commit.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab is small enough that everyone knows what everyone else is working on, which I actually like — there's no sense of competition between lab members. Dan sets a tone that's intellectually serious but not stressful in a toxic way.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We do individual meetings regularly, and Dan comes prepared to actually dig into your data, not just check a box. There's no formal lab meeting structure that eats your whole week, which I appreciate.
student_survey_responses.independence: Honestly, the independence here is real, maybe more than I expected from a smaller lab. You're expected to be driving your own project, reading the literature and proposing the next experiment. Dan pushes back and helps you sharpen ideas, but the initiative has to come from you.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish someone had told me that the projects naturally blend bench and computational work, and that you should be comfortable moving between both within the same week. I came in thinking I'd be mostly at the bench and had to recalibrate.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who read broadly and get excited about evolutionary context — like why a given regulatory response exists across species rather than just whether it does — really thrive here. Dan rewards curiosity that goes beyond the immediate experiment.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: The lack of external grant funding does create some uncertainty that you just have to be okay with, and it can limit certain experiments. Also, if you want a big bustling lab with lots of senior postdocs to learn from day-to-day, that's not really the environment here.

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a quiet, self-directed atmosphere — people are collegial but you're not going to find a lot of group hangouts or lab happy hours. Everyone is pretty heads-down on their own projects, which suits some people perfectly and can feel isolating for others.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: One-on-one meetings with Stephen happen roughly every few weeks, sometimes stretching longer if fieldwork season is busy. There's no regular lab-wide meeting that I've found particularly structured, so a lot of coordination happens informally.
student_survey_responses.independence: You get a lot of autonomy here, probably more than I expected coming in — Stephen trusts you to drive your own project and doesn't micromanage day-to-day decisions. That's great once you've found your footing, but early on it can feel like you're flying blind without much scaffolding.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd understood how field-heavy the work can get seasonally; there are stretches where you're essentially living out of a cooler in the field for weeks. It's exciting, but it reshapes your whole calendar in ways that affect coursework and writing deadlines.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are genuinely self-motivated about natural history questions and comfortable with ambiguity do really well — if you can design your own experiments and troubleshoot independently, Stephen's mentorship style is a great match. People with a deep love for lizard and amphibian biology in particular seem to find the work intrinsically rewarding enough to sustain them through the harder stretches.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need frequent structured feedback or a tight-knit lab community to stay motivated, this setup can be hard. Students who came in expecting more hand-holding on analysis or writing have found the experience frustrating.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: I'd describe the culture as intellectually serious and low-drama — people respect each other's work and there's no weird competitiveness, but it's also not a particularly warm or social environment by default. You have to actively build relationships here rather than having them handed to you.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Stephen is accessible and genuinely responsive over email, but formal sit-down meetings aren't super frequent — maybe once a month or so unless you push for more. I've learned to come with a prepared agenda and specific questions, which actually makes those meetings really productive.
student_survey_responses.independence: This is fundamentally an independent-researcher training environment; Stephen will engage deeply with your ideas when you bring them to him, but he's not going to generate the questions for you. I've grown a lot from that, though my first year was rocky because I kept waiting for more direction that wasn't coming.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish someone had told me that funding timelines can be uncertain and that I should have a fellowship application strategy from day one rather than assuming grant support would always be in place. Also, the thermal biology and ecophysiology literature is vast — starting reading groups with peers outside the lab helped me enormously.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who already have a clear organismal or conceptual interest and want the freedom to pursue it rigorously thrive here. If you're excited by field ecology, physiological ecology, or life-history evolution and you're comfortable with long data-collection timelines, this lab rewards that patience.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need a lot of social cohesion or structured mentorship milestones can find themselves adrift. The pace of feedback on manuscripts and proposals can also be slow during certain periods, which is stressful when you're on a defense timeline.

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a pretty relaxed, collaborative vibe — people share ideas freely and nobody hoards their work, which I genuinely appreciate. It can feel a little unstructured at times, especially when you first join and aren't sure what the norms are.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We do a one-on-one with John roughly every two weeks, which is enough to stay on track but sometimes I wish it were weekly when I'm stuck on something. Group meetings happen maybe once a month and are more of a check-in than a deep dive.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own project pretty much from the start, which is exciting but can be overwhelming if you came from a very guided undergrad environment. John trusts you to figure things out, and he'll point you toward resources, but he's not going to hand you a roadmap.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how important it is to proactively schedule time with him — if you wait for him to check in, weeks can slip by. Also, getting familiar with the interdisciplinary side of the work early would have saved me a lot of catch-up time.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are self-motivated, comfortable sitting with ambiguity, and genuinely curious across more than one subfield do really well here. If you like having a clear deliverable handed to you every week, you might find it frustrating.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need frequent external validation or a very structured schedule tend to drift a bit, and I've seen a couple of students lose momentum because of it. The low-pressure atmosphere is a double-edged sword.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small lab, so you end up knowing everyone well, and there's a real sense that we're all figuring things out together rather than competing. That said, because it's small, there isn't a lot of senior-student mentorship built into the structure — you kind of have to seek that out yourself.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: John and I meet individually about once every two to three weeks, and honestly the cadence shifts depending on where I am in the project cycle — crunch before a deadline and he's very available, quieter periods and it can stretch out. I've learned to just send a Slack message when I need to talk something through.
student_survey_responses.independence: The level of independence here is high, which I mean as both a compliment and a heads-up. By my second year I was making most of the methodological decisions on my own, which has been great for my development but required me to build my own scaffolding for staying accountable.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't realize how much the success of a project here depends on your ability to scope it yourself — there's no one telling you when something is too big or too narrow. I also wish I'd asked more explicit questions about funding and timelines before I started.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who have a genuine passion for the specific intersection of topics the lab works in, and who enjoy building things from scratch rather than refining existing frameworks, tend to produce really interesting work. Intellectual curiosity goes a long way.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a lot of structure or a mentor who will meet with you every week without being asked, this probably isn't the right fit. The feedback loop can feel long at times, and you have to be comfortable with uncertainty between check-ins.

recruiting_status.rationale: lab page exists but no funding, publication, or recruitment signals

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a pretty quiet, heads-down atmosphere — most people are working on their own projects and there isn't a lot of structured collaboration, which suits some people better than others. It's collegial enough, but don't expect a big group-hangout culture.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: One-on-ones with the PI happen roughly every one to two weeks, though they can slip if deadlines are busy on his end. There's no mandatory lab-wide meeting every week, which I appreciate, but it also means you can go stretches without much group feedback.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own work pretty substantially — he'll point you in a direction and ask for updates, but he won't micromanage your day-to-day. For me that's been good, but I know a labmate who struggled because they wanted more structured guidance early on.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd understood upfront how important it is to self-advocate for resources and feedback here — things don't just come to you automatically. Also, publication timelines can be longer than you'd expect if your project hits a technical snag, so plan accordingly.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Self-starters who are comfortable reading widely, defining sub-problems on their own, and just getting things done without a lot of hand-holding tend to do well. If you genuinely love the technical depth of the research area, you'll find the environment stimulating.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who need frequent structured check-ins or a tight-knit cohort for motivation may find it isolating. If you're not proactive about asking for feedback, you can drift for longer than is healthy.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small group, so you get a lot of direct access to the PI, which I genuinely value — there's no layer of senior postdocs you have to go through. The vibe is serious and research-focused rather than social, which honestly fits me, though I can see it feeling a bit sparse for others.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We meet individually maybe once a week or every other week depending on where I am in a project cycle; when I'm close to a submission it becomes more frequent and more useful. I wouldn't say the meetings are long — they're usually 30–45 minutes — but they're substantive if you come prepared.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a high degree of ownership over your project, which felt uncomfortable in year one but now feels like the right way to do a PhD. He'll redirect you if you're going off the rails, but he's not going to design your experiments for you.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The onboarding is pretty informal — no structured reading list or rotation — so it helps a lot if you come in already knowing the broad literature and can hit the ground running. I also didn't realize how much grant-cycle timing would affect equipment availability in the early months.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who have a genuine intellectual curiosity about the specific problem space, not just a vague interest in 'CS research,' tend to flourish because the conversations go deep quickly. Being organized and keeping notes on your own progress matters more here than in labs with tighter top-down structure.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a lot of emotional support or career mentorship beyond the technical work itself — networking advice, internship guidance, interview prep — you'll need to seek that out elsewhere because it won't come proactively from the lab environment.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a collaborative, low-ego feel — people share code and data pretty openly, and there's no obvious hierarchy between senior and junior students. That said, it can feel a bit unstructured if you need clear milestones to stay motivated.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have a weekly group meeting where everyone gives brief updates, and one-on-ones with Almudena happen roughly every two weeks, though scheduling can slip when she's traveling or dealing with administrative stuff.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own project from fairly early on, which I found exciting but a little disorienting at first — there's no one handing you a roadmap. If you go to her with a well-formed question or a specific decision point, you get really useful feedback; if you show up vague, the meeting ends vague.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much time I'd spend navigating the institution itself — room bookings, equipment procurement, IRB paperwork — rather than just doing research. Coming in with some project management habits would have saved me months of chaos.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are genuinely self-directed and can tolerate ambiguity for stretches of time do really well here, especially if they're excited about where technology intersects with real-world applications in the Bay Area ecosystem.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need frequent check-ins or structured feedback loops can feel adrift, and if you're someone who needs a lot of emotional mentorship alongside the intellectual kind, you may have to build that support network elsewhere in the department.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: There's a genuine sense that everyone is working on something they actually care about, which creates good energy in the space — people will spontaneously talk through each other's problems over coffee. It's a small enough group that one person being stressed out or checked-out is noticeable, for better or worse.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Group meetings are a real anchor of the week and Almudena is pretty consistent about showing up prepared, which I appreciate. Individual meetings are less predictable in frequency — I've gone three weeks without one during crunch periods, which required me to make judgment calls on my own.
student_survey_responses.independence: The expectation of independence is high and real, not just lip service. I've found that refreshing compared to friends in more top-down labs, but it does mean you need to be proactive about flagging when you're stuck rather than hoping someone notices.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I underestimated how much the Northeastern Oakland campus is still building itself out — some infrastructure and resources you'd take for granted at a larger established campus just aren't there yet, and you sometimes have to be resourceful in ways that eat into research time.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who come in with some prior research exposure, know how to ask pointed questions, and are excited about emerging tech applications — especially anything touching XR or human-centered computing — seem to hit the ground running.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If your background is purely theoretical and you're not comfortable with prototyping or working with real users, you might find the applied orientation of the lab a bit of an adjustment; the work tends to live in the messy middle between systems and people.

recruiting_status.rationale: lab page exists but no funding, publication, or recruitment signals

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My research centers on the strategic search for knowledge — I want to understand how agents, human or artificial, recognize gaps in what they know and then systematically close those gaps. This spans questions about when to keep searching, what to search for, and how to verify or abandon an answer when confidence is low. I find the intersection of cognitive science and computer science the most natural place to pursue these questions.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I'm drawn to students who are genuinely curious about how minds — biological or computational — manage what they know and don't know. Because my work touches on human information-seeking behavior, memory mechanisms in agent architectures, inference in semantic networks, and reinforcement learning of memory use, I appreciate applicants who can move comfortably between empirical and formal ways of thinking. An ability to engage with both cognitive science literature and computational modeling is a real asset.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in my lab involves a mix of reading and discussion, hands-on implementation or experimentation, and regular one-on-one check-ins with me. Because Occidental is a small liberal arts college, students often have close contact with me and contribute meaningfully to projects from early on rather than being one of many in a large group.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I try to meet students where they are and help them develop their own research voice over time. I've written openly about building a department from the ground up at Occidental, so I have a deep appreciation for learning-by-doing and for the value of incremental progress — I try to bring that patience and scaffolding to how I mentor students.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I don't currently have external grant funding to report, which is worth being transparent about. Students considering working with me should be aware of that context and may want to discuss funding pathways, fellowships, or institutional support at Occidental as part of their planning.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: If my research on strategic knowledge search resonates with you, I'd encourage you to articulate specifically which thread interests you most — whether that's human information-seeking behavior, memory in agent architectures, semantic network inference, or reinforcement learning of memory use. Reaching out with a focused, concrete message about how your background connects to one of those areas will make for a much more productive conversation.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small operation — sometimes it feels like just me and one or two other students working on adjacent problems — which means there's a lot of genuine intellectual exchange rather than siloed project work. The vibe is collaborative but quiet; we're not a big social lab, but conversations about papers tend to go deep and run long.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Justin does weekly one-on-ones and actually holds to them, which I've heard isn't universal. They're not status-report meetings — he usually comes in with a question or a paper excerpt he wants to think through together, which keeps things generative.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own project direction pretty early, which I wasn't entirely ready for as a first-year. He'll scaffold you — suggest readings, push back on vague framing — but he won't hand you a well-scoped problem on a silver platter. That's either exciting or stressful depending on the week.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I had known more concretely what the funding situation looks like before I arrived. Justin is upfront about it if you ask, but I didn't think to ask, and navigating fellowships mid-program added stress I wasn't expecting.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who genuinely enjoy reading across cognitive science and CS literature — not just tolerating one side — do really well here. If you light up when a memory model paper echoes something from human information-seeking research, this is the right place.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a lot of external structure or a large cohort of peers to bounce ideas off, the smallness of Occidental can feel isolating. There's no grad student lounge full of labmates to decompress with at the end of the day.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Honestly more collegial than I expected from a CS-adjacent lab — Justin has clearly thought about what kind of environment he wants to build, and there's an openness to half-formed ideas that I've found rare. That said, it's a lean group so if you hit a rough patch, the support network is mostly just you and him.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Weekly check-ins are the norm and they're substantive — he reads what you send him beforehand, which sounds basic but makes a real difference. Occasionally things slip when he has heavy teaching loads, but he usually reschedules rather than just cancels.
student_survey_responses.independence: The expectation of independence ramps up faster than I anticipated. By the end of my first semester I was already expected to be defending specific research questions, not just exploring. It's pushed my growth but I've also had moments of genuine confusion about whether I'm on the right track.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I had a clearer picture of what 'moving between empirical and formal thinking' actually requires on a day-to-day basis — it means you might spend one week running a behavioral task and the next debugging a reinforcement learning loop, and the context switching is real cognitive overhead.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are comfortable with ambiguity and who find the 'knowledge gap' framing genuinely interesting — not just as a metaphor but as a research object — seem to thrive. There's a coherent intellectual thread running through everything, and if it resonates with you it makes the work feel connected.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If your background is heavily on one side — pure ML engineering or pure experimental psych — you'll feel the pull to stay there, but Justin will keep nudging you toward the other. That's the point, but it can feel slow and uncomfortable before it clicks.

name: Joseph Schulz

recent_papers.title: The feasibility of a patient oriented interactive panoramic virtual tour for external beam radiation therapy
recent_papers.abstract: Introduction The integration of digital technology in healthcare, particularly for patient education and experience, is rapidly advancing. This pilot study examined the feasibility of an interactive panoramic virtual tour for improving patient experiences in an external beam radiation therapy (RT) setting at a smaller center. Methods A virtual tour of the RT department was developed using specialized software and 360-degree 8K camera. The study utilized a two-group design: a control group (33 patients) not exposed to the tour and an experimental group (35 patients) who accessed the tour via the MyHealth platform prior to RT treatment. The survey measured levels of anxiety, comfort with treatment course, knowledge about the facility, navigation through a course of RT, and satisfaction with overall treatment on a 1–10 scale, with 10 being a more desirable outcome. Results The experimental group reported improved outcomes compared to the control group across all parameters: anxiety levels were lower (mean: 7.3 ± SD: 2.6 vs. 6.5 ± 3.3; p = 0.32), though variances differed significantly (p = 0.03). Comfort levels were higher (9.1 ± 1.7 vs. 8.4 ± 2.1; p = 0.27), knowledge about the cancer center increased (8.7 ± 1.5 vs. 7.8 ± 2.4; p = 0.27) with unequal variances (p = 0.03), and ease of navigation slightly improved (9.8 ± 0.6 vs. 9.4 ± 1.9; p = 0.61). Satisfaction levels were similar in the experimental group (9.6 ± 1.1 vs. 9.5 ± 1.2; p = 0.74). Conclusions This pilot study provide

recent_papers.title: AVATAR 2.0: next level communication systems for radiotherapy through face-to-face video, biofeedback, translation, and audiovisual immersion
recent_papers.abstract: Purpose This paper discusses an advanced version of our audiovisual-assisted therapeutic ambience in radiotherapy (AVATAR) radiolucent display systems designed for pediatric radiotherapy, enabling anesthesia-free treatments, video communication, and biofeedback. The scope of the AVATAR system is expanded here in two major ways: (i) through alternative mounting systems to accommodate a broader range of radiotherapy machines (specifically to fit robotic-arm and toroidal geometry photon radiotherapy and proton radiotherapy systems) and (ii) through additional hardware to provide video-calling, optimized audio for clear communication, and combined video inputs for biofeedback, translation, and other advanced functionalities. Methods and materials Because robustness requires strong parts and radio-transparency requires thin, light parts, three-dimensional printing was used to rapidly prototype hollow structures and to iteratively improve robustness. Two system designs were made: one that mounts superior and another that mounts inferior to the patient’s head. Radiation dose measurements and calculations were conducted to assess dose perturbations at surface and depth due to the screen. Results For 6-MV volumetric modulated arc therapy (VMAT) plans, with and without the screen, the mean and maximum dose differences inside the planning target volume were 0.2% and 2.6% of the 200 cGy prescription, respectively. recent_papers.title: Multi-Institutional Audit of FLASH and Conventional Dosimetry with a 3D-Printed Anatomically Realistic Mouse Phantom.
recent_papers.venue: International Journal of Radiation Oncology, Biology, Physics
recent_papers.abstract:
PURPOSE
We conducted a multi-institutional dosimetric audit between FLASH and conventional dose rate (CONV) electron irradiations by using an anatomically realistic 3D-printed mouse phantom.


METHODS
A CT scan of a live mouse was used to create a 3D model of bony anatomy, lungs, and soft tissue. A dual-nozzle 3D printer was used to print the mouse phantom using acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (∼1.02 g/cm3) and polylactic acid (∼1.24 g/cm3) simultaneously to simulate soft tissue and bone densities, respectively. The lungs were printed separately using lightweight polylactic acid (∼0.64 g/cm3). Hounsfield units (HU), densities and print-to-print stability of the phantoms were assessed. Three institutions were each provided a phantom, and each institution performed two replicates of irradiations at selected anatomic regions. The average dose difference between FLASH and CONV dose distributions and deviation from the prescribed dose were measured with radiochromic film.


RESULTS
Compared to the reference CT scan, CT scans of the phantom demonstrated mass density differences of 0.10 g/cm3 for bone, 0.12 g/cm3 for lung, and 0.03 g/cm3 for soft tissue regions. Differences in HU between phantoms were <10 HU for soft tissue and bone, with lung showing the most variation (54 HU), but with minimal impact on dose distribution (<0.5%). Mean differences between FLASH and CONV decreased from the first to the second replicate (4.3% to 1.2%), while differences from the prescribed dose decre

recent_papers.title: A clinical solution for non‐toxic 3D‐printed photon blocks in external beam radiation therapy
recent_papers.venue: Journal of Applied Clinical Medical Physics
recent_papers.abstract: Abstract Purpose A well‐known limitation of multi‐leaf collimators is that they cannot easily form island blocks. This can be important in mantle region therapy. Cerrobend photon blocks, currently used for supplementary shielding, are labor‐intensive and error‐prone. To address this, an innovative, non‐toxic, automatically manufactured photon block using 3D‐printing technology is proposed, offering a patient‐specific and accurate alternative. Methods and materials The study investigates the development of patient‐specific photon shielding blocks using 3D‐printing for three different patient cases. A 3D‐printed photon block shell filled with tungsten ball bearings (BBs) was designed to have similar dosimetric properties to Cerrobend standards. The generation of the blocks was automated using the Eclipse Scripting API and Python. Quality assurance was performed by comparing the expected and actual weight of the tungsten BBs used for shielding. Dosimetric and field geometry comparisons were conducted between 3D‐printed and Cerrobend blocks, utilizing ionization chambers, imaging, and field geometry analysis. Results The quality assurance assessment revealed a −1.3% average difference in the mass of tungsten ball bearings for different patients. Relative dose output measurements for three patient‐specific blocks in the blocked region agreed within 2% of each other. Against the Treatment Planning System (TPS), both 3D‐printed and Cerrobend blocks agreed within 2%. For each patient

recent_papers.title: Tungsten filled 3D printed lung blocks for total body irradiation.
recent_papers.abstract:
PURPOSE
Lung blocks for total-body-irradiation (TBI) are commonly used to reduce lung dose and prevent radiation pneumonitis. Currently, molten Cerrobend containing toxic materials, specifically lead and cadmium, is poured into molds to construct blocks. Here, we propose a streamlined method to create 3D-printed lung block shells and fill them with tungsten ball-bearings (BBs) to remove lead and improve overall accuracy in the block manufacturing workflow.


METHODS AND MATERIALS
3D-printed lung block shells were automatically generated using an inhouse software, printed, and filled with 2-3mm diameter tungsten BBs. Clinical Cerrobend blocks were compared to the physician drawn blocks as well as our proposed tungsten filled 3D-printed blocks. Physical and dosimetric comparisons were performed on a linac. Dose transmission through the Cerrobend and 3D-printed blocks were measured using point dosimetry (ion-chamber) and the on-board Electronic-Portal-Imaging-Device (EPID). Dose profiles from the EPID images were used to compute the full-width-half-maximum (FWHM) and to compare with the treatment-planning-system (TPS). Additionally, the coefficient-of-variation (CoV) in the central 80% of FWHM was computed and compared between Cerrobend and 3D-printed blocks.


RESULTS
The geometric difference between TPS and 3D-printed blocks was significantly lower than Cerrobend blocks (3D: -0.88±2.21mm, Cerrobend: -2.28±2.40mm, p=0.0002). Dosimetrically, transmission measurements through the

recent_papers.title: Shaping success: clinical implementation of a 3D-printed electron cutout program in external beam radiation therapy
recent_papers.abstract: Purpose The integration of 3D-printing technology into radiation therapy (RT) has allowed for a novel method to develop personalized electron field-shaping blocks with improved accuracy. By obviating the need for handling highly toxic Cerrobend molds, the clinical workflow is significantly streamlined. This study aims to expound upon the clinical workflow of 3D-printed electron cutouts in RT and furnish one year of in-vivo dosimetry data. Methods and materials 3D-printed electron cutouts for 6x6 cm, 10x10 cm, and 15x15 cm electron applicators were designed and implemented into the clinical workflow after dosimetric commissioning to ensure congruence with the Cerrobend cutouts. The clinical workflow consisted of four parts: i) the cutout aperture was extracted from the treatment planning system (TPS). A 3D printable cutout was then generated automatically through custom scripts; ii) the cutout was 3D-printed with PLA filament, filled with tungsten ball bearings, and underwent quality assurance (QA) to verify density and dosimetry; iii) in-vivo dosimetry was performed with optically stimulated luminescence dosimeters (OSLDs) for a patient’s first treatment and compared to the calculated dose in the TPS; iv) after treatment completion, the 3D-printed cutout was recycled. Results QA and in-vivo OSLD measurements were conducted (n=40). The electron cutouts produced were 6x6 cm (n=3), 10x10 cm (n=30), and 15x15 cm (n=7). PURPOSE
We conducted a multi-institutional dosimetric audit between FLASH and conventional dose rate (CONV) electron irradiations by using an anatomically realistic 3D-printed mouse phantom.


METHODS
A CT scan of a live mouse was used to create a 3D model of bony anatomy, lungs, and soft tissue. A dual-nozzle 3D printer was used to print the mouse phantom using acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (∼1.02 g/cm3) and polylactic acid (∼1.24 g/cm3) simultaneously to simulate soft tissue and bone densities, respectively. The lungs were printed separately using lightweight polylactic acid (∼0.64 g/cm3). Hounsfield units (HU), densities and print-to-print stability of the phantoms were assessed. Three institutions were each provided a phantom, and each institution performed two replicates of irradiations at selected anatomic regions. The average dose difference between FLASH and CONV dose distributions and deviation from the prescribed dose were measured with radiochromic film.


RESULTS
Compared to the reference CT scan, CT scans of the phantom demonstrated mass density differences of 0.10 g/cm3 for bone, 0.12 g/cm3 for lung, and 0.03 g/cm3 for soft tissue regions. Differences in HU between phantoms were <10 HU for soft tissue and bone, with lung showing the most variation (54 HU), but with minimal impact on dose distribution (<0.5%). Abstract Purpose A well‐known limitation of multi‐leaf collimators is that they cannot easily form island blocks. This can be important in mantle region therapy. Cerrobend photon blocks, currently used for supplementary shielding, are labor‐intensive and error‐prone. To address this, an innovative, non‐toxic, automatically manufactured photon block using 3D‐printing technology is proposed, offering a patient‐specific and accurate alternative. Methods and materials The study investigates the development of patient‐specific photon shielding blocks using 3D‐printing for three different patient cases. A 3D‐printed photon block shell filled with tungsten ball bearings (BBs) was designed to have similar dosimetric properties to Cerrobend standards. The generation of the blocks was automated using the Eclipse Scripting API and Python. Quality assurance was performed by comparing the expected and actual weight of the tungsten BBs used for shielding. Dosimetric and field geometry comparisons were conducted between 3D‐printed and Cerrobend blocks, utilizing ionization chambers, imaging, and field geometry analysis. Results The quality assurance assessment revealed a −1.3% average difference in the mass of tungsten ball bearings for different patients. Relative dose output measurements for three patient‐specific blocks in the blocked region agreed within 2% of each other. pi_survey.research_philosophy: My lab is driven by a deep curiosity about how cone snails have evolved one of nature's most sophisticated venom delivery systems — combining rapid injection mechanics with exquisitely selective peptide neurotoxins. I believe that understanding the physiology and biomechanics of prey capture in these venomous gastropods, from molecule to behavior, yields insights that span neuroscience, biochemistry, and evolutionary biology. Integrating approaches from peptide biochemistry, neurophysiology, and advanced imaging keeps our science genuinely multidisciplinary.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are genuinely curious about animals and eager to work hands-on with live organisms, including fish-hunting cone snails and zebrafish. Strong candidates are comfortable with ambiguity and excited to learn techniques that span molecular biology, electrophysiology, and imaging — often within the same project. A willingness to present work at conferences like the Society for Neuroscience or the International Society of Toxinology is also something I value highly.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in the lab might involve maintaining cone snail and zebrafish colonies, running patch clamp recordings on isolated neurons in primary cell culture, or using genomics and peptidomics pipelines to identify novel conotoxins. Students also spend time on imaging — whether that's SEM, confocal microscopy, CT scanning, or high-speed videography of prey-capture events. Writing, data analysis, and lab meetings round out the week.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I see mentorship as a long-term investment; previous students from my lab have gone on to careers in medicine, research biology, biotech, and conservation, and I stay connected with many of them. I try to give students real ownership of a project early on so they develop scientific independence, while remaining closely involved as they navigate new techniques. Presenting at regional and national conferences is something I actively prepare students for as part of their development.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I do not currently hold externally funded grants, so students should be aware that resources are tied to the college's support for undergraduate research rather than a large external budget. That said, Occidental College has a strong culture of faculty-student collaborative research, and I leverage the Cosman Shell Collection and institutional resources to keep projects moving. Students interested in fellowship or scholarship opportunities to support their own work are encouraged to pursue those actively.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Come in having thought carefully about why venomous animals or neurotoxin biology genuinely excites you — vague interest in 'neuroscience' is less compelling than a specific curiosity about how a snail's peptide can silence a fish's motor circuit. Read up on conotoxins and be ready to discuss what questions you'd want to ask. And if you have any prior experience with animal handling, cell culture, or molecular biology, make sure that comes through clearly in your application.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a pretty tight-knit feel — there aren't tons of us, so you end up doing a lot across the whole project rather than owning one narrow slice. It's collaborative in a low-key way; if you're stuck on a patch clamp recording or a peptidomics pipeline, someone usually knows enough to help you troubleshoot.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: One-on-ones with Schulz happen roughly weekly, though during busy stretches like prepping for a conference they can get more frequent. He's genuinely reachable by email and tends to respond quickly when something unexpected comes up with the snail colonies.
student_survey_responses.independence: He hands you a real project with real ownership pretty early, which is exciting but also a little intimidating when you're still learning the techniques. I appreciated that he didn't micromanage, but you do need to be self-directed — nobody is going to chase you to get your imaging done.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known going in that there's no big external grant funding the lab, so you shouldn't expect a lot of budget flexibility for supplies or travel unless you apply for it yourself. That's manageable, but figuring out fellowship applications on top of coursework and lab work is its own time commitment.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who genuinely think cone snails are fascinating — not just 'I like biology' but actually nerding out over how a peptide can selectively knock out a fish's sodium channels — fit in really well here. If you're comfortable jumping between SEM one week and cell culture the next, you'll love the variety.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who need a lot of structured day-to-day direction or who want a single, well-defined protocol to master and repeat will probably find it frustrating. The multidisciplinary setup means you're constantly learning new things, and some weeks that feels overwhelming rather than exciting.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Working with live animals — cone snails, zebrafish — sets the culture in a specific way; the animals don't care about your schedule, so there's a shared sense of responsibility that bonds people. It's a smaller operation than a big R1 lab, which means your contributions are actually visible and valued rather than getting lost.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Lab meetings happen regularly and Schulz is well-prepared, often pushing you to think about the bigger evolutionary or neuroscience context of your data rather than just the technical results. Individual check-ins feel less formal — sometimes they happen at the tanks or microscope rather than in an office, which I actually prefer.
student_survey_responses.independence: The expectation of independence is real and ramped up faster than I expected. He'll point you toward techniques and literature, but you're largely responsible for designing the next experiment once you have preliminary data, which has genuinely made me a better scientist even when it was stressful.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish someone had told me to start thinking about external fellowships from day one — the institutional support is solid for an undergrad-focused college but it has real limits, and having your own funding changes what's possible. Also, high-speed videography of prey capture sounds cool (it is cool) but the data analysis pipeline takes way longer than you'd guess.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who read widely — not just in cone snail biology but across neuroscience and evolutionary biology — get a lot out of conversations with Schulz because he connects dots across fields constantly. If you're the kind of person who gets genuinely annoyed by unanswered mechanistic questions, you'll find the work rewarding.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need the security of a well-funded, large lab environment with dedicated postdocs to train you on every technique, this isn't that place. And the work can feel a bit isolated from mainstream neuroscience circles, so you have to be proactive about positioning your research when you talk to people outside the cone snail world.

pi_survey.research_philosophy: I believe that understanding how plants survive stressful environments requires working across many scales simultaneously — from remote sensing of chlorophyll fluorescence and carbon dioxide uptake at the canopy level all the way down to aquaporin expression at cell membranes. My lab focuses especially on how plant traits like vascular tissue dimensions shape responses to water limitation, heat, and now increasingly fire, a focus that became urgent after the Los Angeles fires in the winter of 2025. Connecting physiology to real-world environmental challenges, whether in southern California shrublands or Costa Rican rainforests, is at the heart of what we do.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are genuinely curious about plant biology and excited to work across both field and laboratory settings — someone comfortable collecting soil moisture data in the field one day and imaging cellular responses under a scanning electron microscope the next. An enthusiasm for hands-on, question-driven science matters more to me than having a perfectly polished background. I also value students who can be collaborative and contribute to a community-oriented lab culture.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week in my lab might involve fieldwork in southern California measuring chlorophyll fluorescence or plant gas exchange with our portable instruments, followed by laboratory sessions using light or scanning electron microscopy to examine plant tissue responses to drought or heat stress. Students may also spend time in the Occidental Herbarium updating and digitizing dried plant specimens, which connects our work to broader botanical collections. The balance between field and lab shifts with the season and the project, but both are always part of the experience.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I work closely with students at every stage of a project, from designing questions and collecting data to interpreting results, and I try to meet students where they are in terms of prior experience. Occidental is a liberal arts college, so I place a high value on helping students develop as scientific thinkers and communicators, not just as technicians. I want every student who comes through this lab to feel ownership over their work.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I currently have active NSF S-STEM funding through a grant focused on creating opportunities for high-achieving students in science and mathematics through scholarships, research experiences, leadership, and community, which runs through September 2026. This grant directly supports undergraduate researchers and helps ensure that financial barriers do not prevent talented students from engaging in meaningful scientific work. I am actively pursuing additional funding to sustain and expand research on plant responses to drought, heat, and fire.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: I encourage prospective students to think carefully about whether the questions my lab asks — how do plants cope with water stress, heat, and fire? — genuinely excite them, because that curiosity is what sustains you through the hard parts of research. Reach out to me directly and tell me specifically what aspect of plant ecophysiology draws you in, whether it is remote sensing, root-microbiome interactions, post-fire recovery, or something else entirely. Students who thrive here are those who are eager to get both their boots muddy in the field and their hands into careful laboratory work.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab feels genuinely collaborative — people share field equipment, troubleshoot microscopy together, and nobody hoards data. It's small enough that you actually know what everyone else is working on, which is nice but also means there's no hiding if you're behind.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Gretchen meets with me one-on-one roughly every week or two, though during field season it can be more frequent just because logistics demand it. She's responsive over email when something comes up between meetings.
student_survey_responses.independence: She gives you real ownership over your project, which I genuinely appreciate, but early on I wished someone had told me that means you have to advocate for yourself when you feel lost — she won't micromanage you back on track.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't realize how much time would go into herbarium digitization work alongside the primary research; it's interesting in its own way but it was a surprise when it showed up on my weekly schedule. Just good to know going in.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who can shift gears quickly — like, literally driving out to a field site at 7am and then spending the afternoon at a scanning electron microscope — will feel right at home here. If you're energized by switching between big-picture ecological questions and fiddly cellular work, this is the right lab.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a very structured, step-by-step training environment, it might feel a little loose at times. The multi-scale approach is intellectually exciting but it can also mean you're learning several techniques at once without feeling expert in any single one for a while.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Gretchen has built a community-first atmosphere — there's a real sense that your success matters to her personally, not just as a research output. That said, it's a liberal arts college lab, so resources and lab size are different from a big R1, which shapes everything.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have regular check-ins, usually weekly during active data collection phases, and she's good about making time before presentations or deadlines. I've never felt like I had to chase her down, which isn't something everyone can say about their advisor.
student_survey_responses.independence: The expectation is that you'll drive your own questions forward once the project is underway — she's very present for framing and interpretation, but the day-to-day execution is on you, which has pushed me to grow faster than I expected.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The research scope is genuinely broad, from remote sensing down to aquaporin expression, and it's worth thinking before you join about which end of that spectrum you want to live in most, because you will be asked to do both regardless of your preference.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who get excited about the 'why does this plant not die' question in any of its forms — fire recovery, drought, tropical canopies — and who don't mind a research agenda that shifts a bit with what the environment throws at southern California.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: The funding situation is real — NSF support runs through 2026 and she's candid that she's pursuing new grants, but if you need guaranteed multi-year stipend security, you should have that conversation early and directly with her.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a pretty small, low-key group — more like a quiet study collective than a buzzing research lab. People are collegial but you have to be comfortable with a lot of self-directed silence.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We meet one-on-one maybe every week or two, depending on where you are in a project, but there's no mandatory group meeting cadence that I've experienced. If you need more face time you have to ask for it directly.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to drive your own work pretty heavily; there's guidance when you seek it out, but nobody is checking in on your daily progress. That suits me fine, but I've seen labmates flounder when they needed more structure.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much the reading and writing load rests entirely on you — there's no enforced reading group or writing workshop to keep you accountable. Coming in with strong self-regulation habits would have saved me a rough first semester.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who genuinely enjoy digging into foundational computer science concepts — theory, systems, pedagogy — and who can sustain momentum without a lot of external cheerleading do really well here.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who need frequent milestones, structured feedback loops, or a lively collaborative group energy tend to feel a bit adrift after the first year.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The vibe is intellectually serious but relaxed — there's no competitive posturing between students, which I genuinely appreciate. It can feel a little isolated compared to bigger labs at other schools, though.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Meetings happen when there's something substantive to discuss, which in practice means I'm scheduling them myself rather than having a standing slot. Some weeks that's great; during stuck periods it means I can go too long without a reality check.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a real emphasis on developing your own research voice, which is valuable long-term but can feel like being thrown in the deep end when you're still figuring out what a tractable question even looks like. The expectation is that you'll figure it out, and mostly you do.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't realize how much the work here leans toward careful, methodical problems — often touching on how computing concepts are understood or taught — rather than flashy systems-building. That's not a complaint, just something to know before you apply.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you're the kind of person who gets quietly obsessed with getting something exactly right and doesn't need external hype to stay motivated, this environment will suit you well.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Anyone expecting a large collaborative team, frequent co-authored sprints, or a PI who proactively pushes you toward conferences will probably find the pace and style frustrating.

pi_survey.research_philosophy: My scientific foundation is rooted in understanding how cell-cell signaling governs fundamental developmental decisions. My early work on Notch signaling — examining how it blocks differentiation of myoblasts, inhibits oligodendrocyte development, and interacts with viral immortalization pathways — shaped my conviction that a single signaling pathway can have remarkably diverse and context-dependent consequences across cell types.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I value students who are genuinely curious about molecular and cellular mechanisms and who aren't afraid to wrestle with ambiguous results. A strong foundation in biology is important, but intellectual persistence and careful experimental thinking matter just as much to me.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: Students in my lab at Seaver College can expect a blend of hands-on bench work and regular one-on-one discussions about data interpretation and next steps. Because I am at a primarily undergraduate institution, there is also real emphasis on developing strong scientific communication skills alongside technical ones.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I approach mentorship as a professor at a teaching-focused institution, which means I invest heavily in each individual student's development. I want students to leave the lab not just with results, but with the ability to think critically about experimental design and to communicate science clearly.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I currently do not have active external grant funding detected, which is something prospective students should factor into their expectations. My lab operates within the resources available at Pepperdine's Seaver College, and I am transparent with students about what that means for project scope.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: If you are interested in developmental cell biology and signaling — the kinds of questions I have pursued around Notch pathway function across different cell lineages — reach out and tell me specifically what draws you to that area. Showing genuine engagement with the science, rather than a generic interest in research, will always stand out to me.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small, close-knit environment — most weeks it's just a couple of us plus some undergrads rotating through, which means you actually get a lot of face time with Dr. Nofziger rather than being lost in a big group. The flip side is there's no hiding; she notices if you're not engaging deeply with your project.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We meet one-on-one pretty regularly, usually once a week or sometimes more if something interesting comes up with the data. She takes those meetings seriously and expects you to come in having thought through your results, not just reporting what pipette you used.
student_survey_responses.independence: She gives you real ownership over experimental decisions fairly early, which I appreciated but also found disorienting at first. She won't just hand you a protocol and say 'run this' — she wants to know why you're choosing that approach and what you'd do if it didn't work.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish someone had told me upfront how much the resource constraints shape what's actually feasible here — some experiments I was excited about simply weren't practical given what the lab has access to. That's not hidden, she's honest about it, but the reality of working within those limits took some adjusting.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you genuinely love thinking about signaling mechanisms at a mechanistic level and you're okay wrestling with ambiguous Western blot results for weeks without panicking, this is a great fit. Students who also like explaining science to others — undergrads, in seminars, wherever — seem to really flourish.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you need a big team around you for motivation, or you came expecting a fully funded pipeline of projects ready to go, you might find it isolating or frustrating. The pace can feel slower than labs with more infrastructure, and you have to be self-motivated enough to push your own project forward.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Dr. Nofziger runs a pretty intellectually demanding environment even though the lab is small — she expects you to actually care about Notch biology and cell signaling, not just treat this as a line on your CV. That said, she's approachable and the conversations can get genuinely interesting when you hit a weird result.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Meetings happen consistently on a one-on-one basis, and she's usually pretty available if something urgent comes up between scheduled check-ins. She's not the type to micromanage your bench time, but she does want to discuss the 'so what' of your data regularly.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a high expectation that you'll develop your own scientific reasoning — she asks a lot of 'why did you design it that way' questions that can feel intense but honestly make you a better thinker. I've grown more in experimental design here than I expected, though it required me to get comfortable with uncertainty.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't fully appreciate before joining that being at a teaching-focused institution means Dr. Nofziger also has heavy teaching commitments, so her bandwidth isn't unlimited even when she's invested in your work. Knowing that would have helped me plan timelines more realistically.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who are comfortable with a slower, more methodical research pace and who want genuine mentorship — like actual back-and-forth about science, not just task assignments — do really well. Strong scientific writing and communication are things she actively helps you build, which is a real asset if you're heading toward grad school or industry.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who are used to large, well-funded labs with lots of technical support staff or shared resources may find the setup lean. If your goal is high-volume data output or you need external grant prestige on your record right away, the current funding situation is worth thinking hard about before you commit.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a seminar-like atmosphere — conversations about landscape history, urbanism, and design theory happen organically in the hallways as much as in scheduled settings. It attracts people who genuinely read widely, so you feel intellectually stimulated but also occasionally intimidated if you come from a purely technical background.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: One-on-one meetings with Lance aren't on a rigid weekly schedule; they tend to cluster around deadlines or when you've produced something substantial to discuss. That works fine once you're self-directed, but early on I wished there were more structured check-ins.
student_survey_responses.independence: Expect a high degree of autonomy — he'll point you toward a horizon and trust you to chart the route. This is genuinely freeing for someone with a clear project, but if you're still narrowing your focus it can feel like you're floating for longer than is comfortable.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd understood upfront that the program sits at a genuinely hybrid intersection of design practice and historical scholarship, so your committee and your portfolio need to reflect both registers. Leaning too hard into one side can leave gaps that reviewers notice.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who have a strong personal design sensibility and can also sustain a rigorous archival or theoretical argument do really well here. If you already have a thesis question burning in you and just need intellectual space and good feedback, this environment is hard to beat.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need frequent structured guidance or explicit task lists tend to drift. There's also limited lab infrastructure for quantitative or computational methods, so if that's central to your work you may need to build those bridges yourself.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: It's a small, quiet operation — no big research team machinery, more like a scholarly studio where everyone is working on something distinct but shares a sensibility about landscape as cultural and historical practice. Collaboration happens through critique and conversation rather than joint projects.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Meetings are substantive when they happen, usually an hour or more with real engagement on your ideas and writing. The gap between meetings can stretch to a few weeks, though, so you have to be proactive about scheduling when you need momentum.
student_survey_responses.independence: Lance treats you as a colleague-in-formation fairly quickly, which is flattering but also means you're expected to bring developed thinking to the table rather than waiting to be assigned directions. The independence is real, not just rhetorical.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: The program's reputation is stronger in certain design-history circles than it is in the broader academic job market, so it's worth thinking early about how you'll frame your dissertation for different audiences — landscape architecture programs, geography, planning, and so on.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students with a background that mixes making and thinking — someone who has practiced design and also spent time in archives or with critical theory — seem to find the most traction. Intellectual curiosity that crosses disciplinary lines is genuinely rewarded here.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you're hoping for tight feedback loops, co-authored papers, or a lab grant structure that carries your funding predictably, this isn't that. Funding arrangements can feel uncertain, and you'll likely need to piece together support through teaching, fellowships, and your own grant applications.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab is small and fairly collaborative — you get to know everyone's projects pretty well, which is nice when you're troubleshooting. It can feel a bit isolated compared to bigger programs since there aren't a lot of people to bounce ideas off of on a daily basis.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We meet one-on-one with Marion roughly every two weeks, sometimes less frequently if field season is hectic. There's no formal lab meeting every week, which some people love and others find disorienting at first.
student_survey_responses.independence: You're expected to take real ownership of your project early — Marion will point you toward resources and ask sharp questions, but she's not going to hand you a protocol and watch over your shoulder. I appreciated that once I found my footing, but the first year felt pretty sink-or-swim.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I'd known how much time the animal husbandry side of things would take up, especially if you're working with live reptiles or birds. It's not glamorous work, but it's constant, and you need to genuinely enjoy it or it will wear you down.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are self-motivated, comfortable with physiological and field-based methods, and can tolerate a slower feedback loop do really well here. If you like having a lot of autonomy to design your own experiments, this is a good fit.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need frequent check-ins or a lot of structured guidance tend to feel a bit adrift. The funding situation for extras like conference travel has sometimes required creative solutions, so going in with your eyes open about that helps.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Marion is genuinely enthusiastic about the science, and that energy is contagious when you're deep in a project — she'll light up talking about comparative physiology in a way that reminds you why you came to grad school. The lab is intimate enough that there's no real hierarchy drama, though it also means you don't always have senior grad students to learn techniques from.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Meetings happen when you need them more than on a fixed schedule, which works fine if you're proactive about requesting time. I've found that if you go quiet for a few weeks, no one necessarily checks in, so you have to manage your own momentum.
student_survey_responses.independence: The independence is real and I think it's made me a better scientist — I've had to learn how to defend my methodological choices rather than just follow instructions. That said, early on I made some experimental design choices I probably wouldn't have made with tighter mentorship, and I had to backtrack.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't fully appreciate how field-dependent some of the work is — weather, animal availability, permits — so timelines can shift in ways that feel out of your control. Building extra buffer into your projected graduation timeline is genuinely wise.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you have a strong background in or genuine curiosity about animal physiology and ecology, and you're comfortable working with vertebrates hands-on, you'll find the work deeply satisfying. People who can read broadly and connect their question to the bigger comparative literature get a lot out of conversations with Marion.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: The writing feedback loop can be slow during busy periods, so if you need rapid manuscript turnaround to stay motivated, plan ahead and be explicit about your deadlines. It's not neglect — it's just a small lab with a lot going on.

recruiting_status.rationale: no lab text, funding, or publication data available

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a collaborative but quietly competitive atmosphere — people share code and paper drafts freely, but there's an unspoken pressure to keep up with each other's output. It feels small enough that you actually know what everyone is working on, which I appreciate.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have a weekly group meeting where someone presents their current work, and Tzu-Yi meets with each of us one-on-one roughly every two weeks, sometimes more if you're approaching a deadline. The one-on-ones are substantive — she reads your drafts beforehand and comes with real feedback.
student_survey_responses.independence: There's a fair amount of independence here, probably more than I expected as a first-year. She'll give you a problem space and some initial direction, but after that you're largely expected to chart your own course and bring her updates rather than waiting to be guided step by step.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wished I knew how important it is to be proactive about communicating when you're stuck — she won't check in on you if things go quiet, and it's easy to lose weeks spinning your wheels before anyone notices. Also, writing skills matter a lot here; plan on revising papers many more times than you think is reasonable.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: Students who are self-motivated, comfortable with ambiguity, and genuinely enjoy digging into technical problems without needing constant reassurance tend to do really well. If you like having ownership over your research direction, this is a good place.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: People who need a lot of structured check-ins or who want a very tightly scoped dissertation project can find the open-endedness frustrating. The lab is not the place for you if you're hoping your advisor will hand you a clear roadmap from day one.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: Honestly the culture is pretty low-drama and collegial — nobody is territorial about their ideas and there's a genuine sense that we want each other to succeed. That said, it's not a super social lab outside of work; most bonding happens around conference travel or paper deadlines rather than regular lab hangouts.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Group meetings happen weekly and rotate who's presenting, so you might go a few weeks without being in the hot seat, which is both a relief and a risk if you need external pressure to make progress. Individual meetings are scheduled but can get rescheduled when she's traveling to conferences, which happens a few times a year.
student_survey_responses.independence: The level of independence skews high, and my sense is that it increases as you get further along — by your third year you're expected to be driving the research agenda yourself and essentially bringing her along rather than the reverse. Early on she's more involved, but it tapers off deliberately.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish someone had told me that feedback on writing can be pretty blunt and direct here, which is ultimately useful but took some getting used to coming straight from undergrad. Also, there isn't a lot of hand-holding around the administrative side of grad school, so you need to stay on top of deadlines and requirements yourself.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People with a strong theoretical foundation who also like building and evaluating real systems seem to find the most traction here — the work tends to live in that intersection and it's hard to contribute fully if you're only comfortable on one side. Intellectual curiosity that outlasts setbacks is probably the single most important trait.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: If you came in expecting a mentor who will actively push your career — making introductions, nominating you for awards, brokering collaborations — you might feel a bit on your own in that regard. You can make those things happen, but you'll need to take initiative yourself rather than waiting for it to come from her.
