pi_survey.research_philosophy: My lab is driven by a fundamental curiosity about how cells acquire, maintain, and can be redirected to adopt specialized identities — questions that have direct relevance to regenerative medicine and disease resilience. Whether we are dissecting how transcription factors like Dmrt2 and Hmx2 drive intercalated cell diversity in the kidney, or exploring why female proximal tubule cells are more resilient to ischemia-reperfusion injury, I believe rigorous mechanistic work at the cellular and molecular level is the foundation for any meaningful translational advance. Understanding normal biology deeply is, in my view, the surest path to understanding what goes wrong in disease.
pi_survey.what_i_look_for: I look for students who are genuinely intellectually curious and unafraid to engage with complex biological systems across multiple scales — from transcription factor binding to whole-organ physiology. Because our work spans kidney injury and repair, inner ear hair cell regeneration, and adipose tissue biology, I value candidates who can think across organ systems and bring creative, cross-disciplinary perspectives. Strong quantitative reasoning and comfort with genomic or proteomic datasets are increasingly important, as projects like our in vivo proximity-labeling adipocyte proteome work demonstrate.
pi_survey.typical_student_week: A typical week involves a mix of hands-on experimental work — which might include mouse genetics, single-cell analyses, or in vitro lineage reprogramming assays — alongside reading current literature and participating in lab meetings where we critically discuss ongoing projects and new findings. Students are expected to own their projects intellectually, so time spent analyzing data, troubleshooting, and iterating on hypotheses is just as important as bench time. Collaborative discussions with labmates working on adjacent questions, such as how Nfkb1 removal from proximal tubule cells affects injury outcomes, often spark unexpected insights.
pi_survey.mentorship_approach: I aim to give students enough independence to develop their own scientific voice while remaining closely engaged during critical experimental junctures and career decisions. I try to tailor my mentorship to each student's stage and needs — early trainees get more structured guidance, while more advanced students are encouraged to drive their own intellectual agenda and take ownership of manuscripts. I see my role as helping students build not just technical skills but the scientific judgment they will need throughout their careers.
pi_survey.funding_outlook: I am not in a position to detail specific active grants at this moment, but our lab has a productive publication record across kidney biology, sensory cell regeneration, and metabolic tissue research, and I am actively pursuing funding to support these research directions. Prospective students should speak with me directly about the current funding landscape and project availability when they reach out.
pi_survey.advice_to_applicants: Read our recent papers carefully — work like the Dmrt2/Hmx2 intercalated cell study or our direct lineage reprogramming approach for inner ear hair cells will give you a genuine sense of the questions and experimental strategies that excite us. When you write to me, tell me specifically which biological problem resonates with you and why, rather than sending a generic inquiry. The students who thrive in my lab are those who arrive with intellectual passion and a willingness to embrace the inevitable complexity of in vivo biology.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: The lab has a genuinely collaborative feel — people working on kidney, ear, and fat biology all sit near each other and you end up borrowing ideas across projects more than you'd expect. It's not a high-drama environment, but there's a quiet expectation that you'll bring intellectual seriousness to every conversation.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: We have a weekly lab meeting that rotates between project updates and paper discussions, and Andrew gives substantive feedback rather than just nodding along. One-on-ones happen but you sometimes have to be proactive about scheduling them, especially mid-semester when things get busy.
student_survey_responses.independence: Independence here is real, not just a talking point — by year two or three you're expected to own the direction of your project and defend your experimental choices. Early on I found that a little disorienting coming from an undergrad lab where everything was spelled out, but in hindsight it forced me to actually think like a scientist.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I wish I had known how mouse-genetics-heavy the work actually is — the timelines for getting the right crosses can add months to a project and you have to plan around that from the start. Also, comfort with single-cell data analysis isn't optional anymore; I had to pick up R and some bioinformatics on the fly.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: People who have a specific biological question they're genuinely obsessed with do really well here, because Andrew engages most deeply when you can articulate *why* a mechanism matters. If you like connecting molecular data to whole-organ physiology, you'll find a lot to work with.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: Students who need a very defined checklist of daily tasks or constant reassurance can find the open-endedness stressful. If you're not comfortable sitting with uncertainty while a mouse colony matures or a dataset doesn't cooperate, it can feel like you're spinning your wheels for stretches.

student_survey_responses.lab_culture: There's a lot of mutual respect between lab members, and people are generally willing to troubleshoot each other's experiments even if they're on totally different organ systems. The tone Andrew sets is that rigorous, curiosity-driven work is the point — not chasing trendy topics or padding a CV.
student_survey_responses.meeting_frequency: Lab meetings are weekly and Andrew is usually well-prepared with pointed questions about controls and interpretation. Individual check-ins vary — some students meet with him every week, others go longer between formal meetings, and a lot depends on where you are in a project or how close you are to submitting something.
student_survey_responses.independence: You get substantial intellectual freedom, which I've mostly appreciated, but it does mean you can spend time pursuing an angle that turns out to be a dead end before getting redirected. The upside is that when something works, it genuinely feels like yours.
student_survey_responses.wished_i_knew: I didn't fully appreciate how much the funding conversation matters when you're choosing a lab — I'd encourage anyone joining to have a frank conversation with Andrew upfront about where project support stands rather than assuming it's sorted. That said, the lab has been productive and there haven't been major disruptions during my time.
student_survey_responses.thrives_here: If you're the kind of person who reads a paper and immediately starts thinking about what the authors didn't test, you'll fit in well. Andrew responds really positively to students who push back on their own data before he has to.
student_survey_responses.struggles_here: The breadth of the lab — kidney, inner ear, adipose tissue — is intellectually exciting but it can make you feel like you're working in relative isolation on your specific system without a big cohort of labmates doing exactly the same thing. You have to be a bit self-directed about finding your community.

recent_papers.title: Photovoltaic amorphous feroxyhyte nanostructures synthesized by atmospheric AC microplasma
recent_papers.abstract: Feroxyhite (δ-FeOOH) nanomaterials were successfully synthesized through the atmospheric AC microplasma method at room temperature from ferrous sulfate aqueous solutions. Various syntheses conditions, including electric voltage, electric field strength, ferrous concentration, hydrogen peroxide concentration, and reaction duration, were systematically investigated. The synthesized products were characterized through x-ray diffraction, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, infra-red spectroscopy, and electron microscopy. The bandgap of the produced materials were strongly dependent of the ferrous concentration while the product ratio was dependent on all experimental conditions. The synthesis mechanism was thoroughly discussed. The synthesized nanomaterials were amorphous nanospheres, showing superparamagnetic properties at room temperature. The synthesized oxyhydroxide is a potential photovoltaic material besides its reported applications in photocatalysts and supercapacitors. The application of this synthesis technique could be extended to synthesize other oxy-hydroxide nanomaterials for renewable energy applications facilely, scalablely, cost-effectively, and environmentally.
